Current issues in counseling	
Danger of fragmenting into separate groups along the lines of specialties, instead of working toward public recognition as one unified profession of counseling.
Working toward standardization of licensure requirements and school counselor certification requirements across the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and possessions of the United States.
Scope of practice: Ensuring that other mental health professional groups do not interfere with counselors diagnosing and treating mental and emotional disorders or limit the testing practices of counselors. Advocating for equal representation, compensation, recognition
Establishing job classifications in state governments and at the federal level for counselors (titles at times do not include a counseling title- “social work, psychologist, metal health technician…”) 
Telehealth-Standards for practice?
More issues to consider
Third party reimbursement
Increasing salaries for MA clinicians
Becoming united and societally-recognized as a profession
Increasing publications of experimental research studies
Increase availability of counseling services for everyone
Discussion
LINKS
https://doh.wa.gov/licenses-permits-and-certificates/professions-new-renew-or-update/mental-health-counselor/supervisor-directory
https://counselingcompact.gov/faq/
ethics
Definitions
Definitions
Professional Practice: “built from within but balanced from outside of the self” (P. 8)
Personal Values
Apply broadly to both the personal and professional functioning of counselors
Serve as a guide to determining what is good or right behavior
Each of us holds some values more strongly than other values
Each value system is unique and is influenced by upbringing, culture, and religious beliefs
In counseling we are asked not to impose our values, rather help the client live out their own. It is critical that we know and understand our own values and practice bracketing (see case of Carole on p. 4).
Professional values
What comes to mind when you think of values of the following groups/companies:
US Army
Red Cross
Amnesty International
Culinary arts (American culinary federation)
Interior Design
Landscape 
Museums
Professional values of counseling (ACA, 2014)
Ethics
Ethics: a discipline within philosophy that is concerned with human conduct and moral decision making.
Professional ethics refers to conduct judged as good or right for counselors as a professional group. 
Consensus about right behaviors have been codified and have become the ethical standards to which counselors are expected to adhere in their professional life.
Thus, ethics should guide one’s professional behavior and interactions.
Theories of Ethics
Ethical theories provide a framework that counselors can use to decide whether an action or contemplated action is ethical. 
Ascribing to an ethical theory will enable you to resolve various ethical dilemmas you encounter in your work and help you defend the solutions you reach. 
Principle Ethics in a field answer the question “what should I do”
Why Ethical theories are important
Theories of ethics have existed for centuries and provide a foundation for the ethical reasoning of contemporary professional counselors.
Principle ethics in Counseling (ACA 2014)
Review counseling’s principle ethics on pages 12-13
Define the principles
Review the case of Gabriella considering the principle ethics. Which Ethical principles come into play?
What other ways might these principles might come into play in a counseling relationship
Why a Code of Ethics?
Educate members of the profession as well as consumers about what constitutes ethical practice
Help to ensure accountability through enforcement of the standards
Protect the profession from government by allowing the profession to regulate itself and function more autonomously
Promote stability within the profession by establishing agreed-upon guidelines for practice
Serve as a catalyst for ongoing improvement of practice
examples of ethical codes	
ACA: Ethical Standards 
AACC ethical standards-https://aacc.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/AACC_Code-of-Ethics-2023_FINAL.pdf
Ethical Guidelines for Counseling Supervisors
Code of Ethics of the American Mental Health Counselors Association Ethical Standards for School Counselors
AAMFT Code of Ethics
Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct Ethical Standards of Human Service Professionals
Ethics of the International Association of Marriage and Family Counselors Code of Ethics of the National Association of Social Workers
Canadian Counselling Association Code of Ethics
Code of Professional Ethics for Rehabilitation Counselors National Board for Certified Counselors Code of Ethics.
NBCC Standards for the Ethical Practice of Internet Counseling
Association for the Specialists in Group Work Professional Standards for the Training of Group Workers.
Association for the Specialists in Group Work Best Practice Guidelines
Association for the Specialists in Group Work Principles for Diversity-Competent Group. Workers
The ACA code includes
Preamble
8 sections (A-I)
The Counseling Relationship
Confidentiality and Privacy
Professional Responsibility
Relationships with Pther Professionals
Evaluation, Assessment and Interpretation
Supervision Training and Teaching
Research and Publication
Distance Counseling, Technology and Social Media
Resolving Ethical Issues
Principles Ethics vs Virtue Ethics
Principle ethics refer to the professionally agreed upon obligations that are always considered in ethical decision making.
Principle ethics ask: “What should I do?” 
Virtue ethics assume that professional ethics involve more than moral actions; they also involve traits of character or virtue.  
Virtue ethics ask: “Who should I be?” 
Virtue ethics
Beyond moral actions, virtue ethics looks at character, virtue ethics asks “Who should I be”, not just as a clinician but as a person.  The focus is on YOU as a person, not a particular action or decision.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kq-rOelLciE
Virtue ethics asks us to consider virtue OVER what emotion dictates, what is financially beneficial, the easiest solution. 
People have different strengths of character and should aim to express the virtues most important to them
To prepare For next class: Free character strength’s test: https://www.viacharacter.org/
Legal Issues
Most areas of counselor practice are affected by law
Counselors must know how to recognize legal issues and how to obtain legal advice
Counselors should: 
seek advice from fellow mental health professionals when they have clinical or ethical questions 
seek advice from lawyers when they have legal questions
Following laws typically represents minimum requirement
You are not alone! Support structures offer guidance and support
How should you get advice when a legal issue presents itself?
Steps in Making Ethical Decisions
A general eight-step process for ethical decision making. Keep in mind cultural context and your emotions. 
1. Identify the problem or dilemma-Is it a legal issue or a clinical issue? Take time to review the situation carefully.  (example-termination/leave p. 23)
2. Involve your client in the decision-making process -Throughout the process, involve your client, this empowers the client and builds the relationship with your client (part of safeguarding the integrity of the counselor. (example p. 9)
3. Review the relevant ethics codes -Review codes, laws and literature
4. Consider the principles and virtues. Consider not just what to DO but who you want to BE in the situation 
Steps continued
5. Tune into your feelings-consider the emotions you are experiencing.  Do you have fear/doubt? Do you feel you are being influenced by others in a direction you don’t agree with?  Be aware of emotions, beliefs, values and motivations at play. It is important that your body is relaxed and your mind is engaged throughout this process. Tend to your reaction and take steps to relax and engage. 
6. CONSULT with colleagues or experts-Emotions can interfere. Obtain consultation. This is important from a legal standpoint as well. 
7. Consider the context-Be culturally competent. What ramifications might there be for the client’s family, community, other professionals etc.
8. Make a decision and act on your choice-Don’t linger in indecision. Have courage to come to a decision and act it out.
Activity
Apply the ethical decision model on pages 12-13 to the scenarios in the text. At this point focus particularly on considering the 6-principle ethics in counseling, which of the 6 principle ethics come into play in making a decision?
case 1-1, p. 4
case 1-2, p. 6
case 1-3, p. 11
case 1-4, p. 13
case 1-5., p. 25
Discussion
Assuming that you are a moral and responsible person (as are most counselors), why do you think it is important for you to study ethical and legal principles and the decision-making process?

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 2/9)
content:
Honoring diversity, embracing culture
The introduction to Section A of the ACA Code of Ethics states that counselors must take an active role in 
attempting to understand clients’ diverse cultural backgrounds and the ways that their own cultural 
identities affect their values and beliefs relative to the counseling process.
The ACA Code of Ethics requires that counselors respect the diversity of clients, trainees, and research 
participants (Section A.4.b.). All sections of the ACA Code of Ethics require that counselors pay attention 
to the diversity of their clients.
Counselors wield power over clients; therefore, avoiding the abuse of that power in areas of cultural 
differences is an ethical responsibility of counselors.
The ACA Code of Ethics states that multicultural counseling competency is required in all counseling 
specialties (C.2.a.).

•Professional counselors must address the implicit and explicit challenges of diversity in a manner that is both culturally competent and 
ethically responsible . According to the ACA Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), counselors should aspire to engage in the following behaviors:
•Actively attempt to understand the diverse cultural backgrounds of the clients they serve (Section A: Introduction)
•Explore their own cultural identities and how these affect their values and beliefs about the counseling process (Section A: Introduction)
•Communicate the parameters of confidentiality in a culturally competent manner (Section B: Introduction)
•Use assessment as one component of the counseling process, taking into account the clients' personal and cultural context (Section E: 
Introduction)
•Minimize bias and respect diversity in designing and implementing research  (Section G: Introduction)
•Recognize that support networks hold various meanings in the lives of clients and consider enlisting the support, understanding, and 
involvement of others (e.g., religious/ spiritual/community leaders, family members, friends) as positive resources, when app ropriate, 
with client consent (Standard A.1.d.)
•Communicate information in ways that are both developmentally and culturally appropriate (Standard A.2.c.)
•When clients have difficulty understanding the language that counselors use, counselors provide necessary services (e.g., arr anging for a 
qualified interpreter or translator) to ensure comprehension by clients (Standard A.2.c.)
•In collaboration with clients, counselors consider cultural implications of informed consent procedures and, where possible, counselors 
adjust their practices accordingly (Standard A.2.c.)
•Are aware of —and avoid imposing —their own values, attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors (Standard A.4.b.)
•Respect the diversity of clients and seek training in areas in which they are at risk of imposing their values onto clients (Standard 
A.11.b.)
•Understand the challenges of accepting gifts from clients and recognize that in some cultures, small gifts are a token of respect and 
gratitude (Standard A.10.f.)

•Maintain awareness and sensitivity regarding cultural meanings of confidentiality and privacy . . . [and] respect differing views toward 
disclosure of information (Standard B.1.a.)
•Gain knowledge, personal awareness, sensitivity, dispositions, and skills pertinent to being a culturally competent counselor in working with 
a diverse client population (Standard C.2.a.)
•Do not condone or engage in discrimination against prospective or current clients, students, employees, supervisees, or research 
participants based on age, culture, disability, ethnicity, race, religion/spirituality, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, 
marital/partnership status, language preference, socioeconomic status, immigration status, or any basis proscribed by law (St andard C.5.)
•Recognize that culture affects the manner in which clients' problems are defined and experienced. Clients' socioeconomic and cultural 
experiences are considered when diagnosing mental disorders (Standard E.5.b.)
•Recognize historical and social prejudices in the misdiagnosis and pathologizing of certain individuals and groups and strive to become 
aware of and address such biases in themselves or others (Standard E.5.c.)
•Carefully consider the validity, reliability, psychometric limitations, and appropriateness of instruments when selecting assessments and, 
when possible, use multiple forms of assessment, data, and/or instruments in forming conclusions, diagnoses, or recommendatio ns 
(Standard E.6.a.)
•Select and use with caution assessment techniques normed on populations other than that of the client. Counselors recognize the effects of 
age, color, culture, disability, ethnic group, gender, race, language preference, religion, spirituality, sexual orientation, and socioeconomic 
status on test administration and interpretation , and they place test results in proper perspective with other relevant factors (Standard E.8.)
•[As supervisors,] are aware of and address the role of multiculturalism/diversity in the supervisory  relationship (Standard F.2.b.)
•[As counselor educators,] infuse material related to multiculturalism/diversity into all courses and workshops for the development of 
professional counselors (Standard F.7.c.)
•[As counselor educators,] actively attempt to recruit and retain a diverse student body, . . . demonstrate commitment . to 
multicultural/diversity competence by recognizing and valuing the diverse cultures and types of abilities that students bring  to the training 
experience,. . [and] provide appropriate accommodations that enhance and support diverse student well -being and academic perform ance 
(Standard F .11.b.)
•[In the informed consent process] issues, unique to the use of distance counseling, technology, and/or social media, are addressed . . . 
[including] cultural and/or language differences that may affect delivery of services (Standard H.2.a.)
•[In maintaining websites,] provide accessibility to persons with disabilities . . . [and] translation capabilities for clients who have a different 
primary language, when feasible (Standard H.5.d.)

Cultural Humility 
Recognizing our limitations
Manifested in our willingness to: 
•seek consultation
•seek continuing education
•make referrals 
The most important recourse for learning about cultural differences is YOUR 
CLIENT

Counselor Attitudes in Working with 
Culturally Diverse Clients
Issues and Ethics - Chapter 4 (4)•denies differences: "I don’t recognize differences; I treat 
everyone the same."Color blind
•recognize, appreciate, and celebrate cultural differences; 
strives for freedom from judgments of diverse clientsVerses appreciating, curiosity and respect

Ask about these things in intake! Consider each 
dimension in assessment and treatment
Addressing  (see Hays, 2002)
•Age
•Developmental and acquired
•Disability
•Religion/spirituality
•Ethinicity
•Socioeconomic status
•Sexual Orientation
•Indigenous status
•Nationality
•GenderRespectful -(see D’ Andrea  and Daniels, 
2001) more psychological/individual components \
•Religious/spiritual ID
•Ethnic id
•Sexual id
•Psychological id
•Economic class
•Chronological/developmental challenges
•Threats to one’s wellbeing
•Family history
•Unique physical challenges
•Location of residence
Intersectionality (see Crenshaw, 1989) Compounded oppression for those with multiple minority ID’s

Counseling and preparation to work with all people
•Early years, the impact of cultural diversity was largely ignored
•1960’s - rise of civil rights and women’s movements increased awareness of equity 
issues
•1970’s – Growing concern about lack of preparation in counselor training to counsel 
diverse individuals
•1980’s – Multicultural counseling increasingly recognized as a discipline within 
counseling
•1990’s -Compencies identified for all counselors (Arredondo et al., 1996)
•2000’s - Shift to social justice and advocacy

Cultural competencies include considering
•Attitudes and Beliefs -awareness of social identities, group statuses, power, privilege, 
strengths, limitations, attitudes, beliefs, biases, etc
•See Marlene p. 68, and LaTasha p. 74
•Knowledge – Develop knowledge to decrease assumptions and increase understanding of 
their worldview and others (privileges, marginalization, etc) 
•See Jack, p. 68
•Skills  (reflective abilities, critical thinking, broaching, curiosity, evaluation, psychoeducation 
etc)
•See Theo p. 70
•Actions (Advocating for change for individuals or at the community level)
•See Malik, p. 71 (started peer groups after meeting a minority client who was being left 
out and made fun of. The counselor advocated for his client with a teacher to increase 
knowledge about this client’s heritage)

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 3/9)
content:
Practice Together (Remley & Herlihy, p. 68)
3-1 The Case of Marlene
Marlene, a White high school counselor, is conducting a group for non –college -
bound senior girls. The topic of this session is “preparing for life after graduation.” 
The nine -member group includes equal numbers of girls who are Black and White, 
and Consuela, who identifies as Latine. The discussion has turned to a 
consideration of the advantages and disadvantages of continuing to live at home 
with parents for a while versus moving into an apartment with friends as soon as 
one finds a job. Marlene notices that Consuela has remained silent for the past 
several minutes. In an effort to draw her in, Marlene asks her, “Consuela, isn’t it 
more of an expectation among Latine families that girls will continue to live at 
home until they marry?”
•What do you think of the question that Marlene posed to Consuela?
•What are some other strategies Marlene might have used to draw Consuela into 
the conversation?

Video examples/discussion
•Video examples
•3.1, 3.2

Activity
•Discuss how could the counselor have responded differently in two of 
these, then role play 
•Case of Theo
•Case of Jack
•Case of LaTasha

Social Justice vs. Advocacy
ACA competencies related to social justice and advocacy
•Social justice -promoting access for all people to mental health services, especially 
those who have been marginalized (See  social justice competencies )
•Advocacy - Commitment to social justice through action ( see advocacy
competencies ) to promote change in clients and their communities.

Christian character virtues related to 
professional principle ethics, Watson, 2018
Character virtue Professional Ethic Spiritual disciplines 
Faith Fidelity Cont. Prayer & Church 
Hope Beneficence Sabbath & Worship 
Love Respect/Autonomy Intercessory prayer 
Justice Justice Attentiveness, Hospitality & 
Service 
Temperance Nonmaleficence Examen & Fasting 
Courage Integrity/Veracity Rule of life & Example 
Practical wisdom Competence Scripture & discern.

Justice  
•Justice is defined by Aquinas “to render to each person that which is his or her right, 
what is due to each person, what each person ought to receive” 
•Justice is an other oriented virtue (in comparison with the other cardinal virtues that 
are individually oriented), involving cultivating habits related to fair and just treatment 
of others as individuals in society.
•In the mental health justice is focused on prosocial behavior, equal access and 
“treating individuals equitably and fostering fairness and equality” (ACA, 2014)
(Watson, 2018)

Christian virtue of Justice
•Followers of Jesus should be disturbed at examples of injustice
•Christian doctrine teaches that all persons are created by God and have eternal value 
•Early church growth involved diverse individuals coming together and helping each 
other, taking care of those in need

A few biblical references to justice
What does the Lord require of you? To act justly and to love mercy
and to walk humbly with your God ( NIV, 2011, Micah 6:8)
Learn to do right; seek justice. Defend the oppressed. Take up the cause of the fatherless; plead 
the case of the widow.  (NIV, 2011, Isaiah 1:17)
Blessed are those who act justly  (NIV, 2011, Psalm 106:3)
Jesus came to “bring good news to the afflicted, bind of up the brokenhearted, proclaim 
freedom...comfort those who mourn” (NIV, 2011, Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4)

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 4/9)
content:
Findings in Neuroscience regarding ways to develop 
virtue
1.Developing ingrained habits of moral thought and action over time is important for ethical decision -making, which in critical 
moments, is often determined more by automatic and largely unconscious cognitive processes* 
2.Moral exemplars  are integral to facilitating moral thinking and behavior, as imitation and simulation are key processes in moral 
development (mirror neuron research)** 
3.Religious communities can be highly effective contexts for modeling and reinforcing virtuous habits*** 
Slide provided by Watson (2023)
*Van Slyke, J.A. (2015). Understanding the moral dimension of spirituality: Insights from virtue ethics and moral exemplars. 
Journal of Psychology and Christianity, 34(3), 205 -215. 
**Edwards, K. (2015). When word meets flesh: A neuroscience perspective on embodied spiritual formation. Journal of 
Psychology and Christianity , 34(3), 228 -239. 
***Edman, L.R.O. (2015). Applying the science of faith: The cognitive science of religion and Christian practice. Journal of 
Psychology and Christianity , 34 (3), 240 -251.

Spiritual Practices
•Christian theology and spiritual practices are a tremendous resources we can draw from 
in fostering character formation of students
•Christian theology provides a telos for character formation: formation in Christlikeness 
for service to God’s kingdom
•Christian community provides ideal context for character formation through stories, 
practices, models, exemplars
•Character formation is a lifelong process that is Spirit -guided and mediated
Slide provided by Watson (2023)

Practicing attentiveness in developing an increased 
focus on justice
•Giving attention to what is happening around us, and curiously listening, learning, observing, and participating with others 
(Watson, 2018)
•Paying attention to God, recognizing his attention to you
•Intentionality in what you expose yourself to (who you talk with, what you read, where you go)
•Where do you see injustice, where do you see God’s redeeming work, or opportunity for God’s redeeming work? 
•What role can you play in bringing about justice?
•Direct attention to the God of justice who is Lord over all and recognize your dependence on his power to restore what is 
broken. 
•Engage in worship and gratitude towards God, remembering God’s deliverance for humanity (Watson, 2018)
•Do a word study on the virtue of attentiveness in the Christian tradition
What would this look like for you?

Practicing hospitality and service in developing the 
virtue of justice
•Hospitality includes “faithful generosity to make room for others” (Watson, 2019, p. 
139)
•Begins with gratitude
•Includes invitations, giving and receiving gifts, gestures
•Interrupting our daily tasks to paying attention to others
•Seeing others who cross our paths with openness
•‘Asking God to use our time talents and energies “for the good of others and causes of 
God in our world” (Willard, 1988, p. 182 as cited in Watson 2019, p. 140)

What about 
stereotypes
•Working with marginalized 
groups requires “recognizing and 
dealing with our own blind spots” 
(Watson, 2019, p. 120)
•Although certain groups share 
similar traits or beliefs, each 
individual is unique and may not 
be like most others from his or 
her group

Video and discussion about stereotypes
•https://www.ted.com/talks/chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_
of_a_single_story/transcript?language=en
Becoming a counselor requires the disorienting experience of 
recognizing and dealing with our own biases and blind spots (Watson, 
2018, p. 120)
When has the impact of a “single story” been challenged in your life?  
When have you been shaken and forced to reconsider your views on a 
topic?

•Ethical questions often emerge when there is a 
conflict between competing values, or ethical 
principles.

What assumptions might you hold that you 
need to be aware of? 
•Age
•Sex
•Class
•Sexual Orientation
•Ableism
•Ageism
•Spiritual/religious bias
Read the case of LaTasha (p. 73). 
•Which counselor assumptions arise the examples?
•Answer the questions posed in these examples and discuss in groups .

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 5/9)
content:
Value conflicts
•It is not unusual for counselors to hold values that are different or even in conflict 
with the values of their clients. Counselors should examine their own values 
regarding these issues and ensure they can provide counseling services without 
allowing their own beliefs to interfere. 
•Counselors who hold values that are different from the values of their clients can 
provide effective counseling services, but only if counselors avoid imposing their 
values on their clients.
•James Marcia’s Theory of identity development emphasize that identities 
should to be defined internally, not externally. If they are externally defined, 
they are not identities at all but are foreclosures on values of others.
•It is critical for counselors to make sincere efforts to understand and appreciate 
client perspectives that differ from their own. Again, a high level of differentiation 
is requires while also leaning in to understand the perspective and experience of 
others.

Bracketing
•Bracketing: “the intentional separating of a counselor’s
personal values from his or her professional values . . . in order
to provide ethical and appropriate counseling to all clients”
(Kocet  & Herlihy, 2014, p. 182
•Without bracketing, when a difference arises, emotional suppression, 
denial or fantasy may occur (Sells and Hagadorn, 2016)
•To bracket you must know what your values are.

Personal values
•Self awareness allows you to be aware of differences 
between your values, beliefs, culture and personal 
choices and a client’s
•The ACA code of ethics  A.4.b. regarding personal 
values states Counselors are aware of —and avoid 
imposing —their own values, attitudes, beliefs, and 
behaviors. Counselors respect the diversity of clients, 
trainees, and research participants and seek training 
in areas in which they are at risk of imposing their 
values onto clients, especially when the counselor’s 
values are inconsistent with the client’s goals or are 
discriminatory in nature.

When would a referral be 
appropriate according to the ACA?
•Review the following sections regarding termination and referral in the ACA code of 
ethics with a partner.
•A.4 a, b
•A 11 a -d
•A 12
•C.2 a,b
•C. 5

Example
Rafael, a counselor for the juvenile court system, is assigned to work 
with a young man who has problems relating to authority figures. This 
client constantly ridicules and insults police, administrators, and other 
authority figures with whom he interacts. After making several 
unsuccessful attempts to develop rapport with the client and after 
consulting with his clinical supervisor, Rafael determines that he is not 
able to establish a therapeutic relationship with this young man. With 
the client's permission, he refers the client to another counselor who 
has a record of success in working with challenging young people who 
have difficulty relating to authority figures.
Is this an appropriate referral?
Example from Herlihy & Corey  (2015) ACA Ethical Standard’s Casebook. American Counseling Association

Appropriate referral:
•Avoid harm A.4.A (specialization is required)
•Lack competence (11.A)

Jonathan is a counseling student in his first semester of 
internship. He tells his site supervisor that he has been seeing 
an interracial couple for the past 2 weeks, but he feels a bit 
uncomfortable because he was raised to believe that 
interracial marriage is wrong. 
Should Jonathan refer this client?

Inappropriate
•Personal values of a client, attitudes, beliefs and behaviors 
that differ from the therapist’s 
•Possible action: He asks his supervisor to observe his 
sessions to determine if his bias is present while he is 
counseling and to give him feedback. He also registers for an 
intensive weekend workshop in multicultural counseling to 
help increase his awareness.

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 6/9)
content:
Values in termination/referral
•Values Within Termination and Referral: ACA ethical code A.11.b
“Counselors refrain from referring prospective and current clients 
based solely on the counselor’s personally held values, attitudes, 
beliefs, and behaviors. Counselors respect the diversity of clients and 
seek training in areas in which they are at risk of imposing their values 
onto clients, especially when the counselor’s values are inconsistent 
with the client’s goals or are discriminatory in nature.”

Case study
•Review the case of Clarence on page 75. Imagine this was your client
•Identify your beliefs and opinions about what you think would be best for Amber.
•Which personal values should be aware of? 
•Identify cultural identities for Amber (consider the addressing model)

Working with LGBTQ+ individuals 
•Professional counselors may not deny counseling services to a homosexual person (or an 
individual belonging to any other protected class of clients) on the basis of the counselor’s 
values.
•Referrals are to be made on the basis of skill -based competency, not values.
•To avoid abandonment, referral is an option of last resort. 
•The counselor’s ethical obligations to an individual starts at first contact or assignment, not at 
the first session.” (Kaplan, 2014, p. 146)
•See text for several critical cases within the counseling profession.

•In CACREP accreditation, ethics, and the affirmation of both religious 
and sexual identities: A response to Smith and Okech , Sells and 
Hagedorn (2016) say counseling is called to honor both religious 
diversity and sexual orientation diversity.
•Right to free exercise of religion personally and in groups, (faith -based 
institutions).
“Counselors must not deny any client group from receiving competent 
counseling services based on differences In sexual identity, gender race, 
age, religious values, physical limitation or any other class distinction” 
(Sells & Hagadorn, 2016)

What resources can you use when resolving ethical dilemmas?
•The best approach counselors can use to resolve ethical dilemmas is to consult with 
colleagues . If a counselor’s supervisor is a mental health professional, that person 
certainly should be consulted. Other possible consultants include counselors who 
perform similar duties as the counselor, counselors in other settings that are similar, 
experts in the counseling field, and perhaps former professors.
•Relevant sections of the ACA Code of Ethics and other relevant codes of ethics 
should be read.  
•Journal articles and textbook chapters that deal with the issue should also be read.

Exclusion and Embrace (Volf, 2019)
•Volf identifies a “crisis of exclusion…a threat to all peoples” (Sells & Hagedorn, 2015)
•Intragroup strife, “otherness” has led to wars, genocide, atrocities
•Volf (Yale university), native of Croatia, grew up as a religious minority, born out of the 
suffering of his people in the Balkan wars where a quarter of a million people died
•Rwanda
•Holocaust

What kind of self do we need to be to live in 
harmony with others?
•Understand my part in cultural sin may aid in protection from the extremes of exclusion, 
inviting us into the reality of membership with humanity
•Paul in Romans 3:23 - we have all fallen short, no is exempt, we must become 
conscious of sin and seek Christ’s redemption
•Recognize the dangers of exclusion and our tendency at times to lean towards it
•Inclusion cannot simply be the cure for exclusion, because the dangers of a world without 
boundaries, without other, would leave us without distinction (Volf, 2019, loc. 1531). The 
challenge to be distant yet belong is a high call and difficult to express well in an 
increasingly divided global platform.
•Challenge to be distinct, yet belong is a high call, difficult to express well
(Volf, 2019)

Developing “double vision”
•Volf (2019) says that while j ustice requires a voice,  justice also necessitates an ear . “A 
voice without an ear can only create frustration and when frustration has its full 
presence, violence, chaos and to use Nagy’s term, destructive entitlements . We must 
look towards opportunities for “exchange, a giving and receiving, a leaning towards 
and welcoming” 
As counselors you have opportunity to give your ear… and practice justice
•Eriksson and Abernethy (2014) use the writings of Volf to see experiences through 
another’s perspective empathically without leaving behind their own views and 
valuees.  This is especially important when they don’t a line.  A high level of 
differentiation between self and others is required.

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 7/9)
content:
Steps in making ethical decisions 
(p. 19 -21)
•A general eight -step process for ethical decision making. Keep in mind cultural context  
and your emotions . 
1. Identify the problem or dilemma -Is it a legal issue or a clinical issue? Take time to 
review the situation carefully.  
2. Involve your client in the decision -making process -Throughout the process, 
involve your client, this empowers the client and builds the relationship with your 
client (part of safeguarding the integrity of the counselor . 
3. Review the relevant ethics codes -Review codes, laws and literature
4. Consider the principles and virtues . Consider not just what to DO but who you 
want to BE in the situation

Steps continued
5. Tune into your feelings -consider the emotions you are experiencing.  Do you 
have fear/doubt? Do you feel you are being influenced by others in a direction 
you don’t agree with?  Be aware of emotions, beliefs, values and motivations at 
play. It is important that your body is relaxed and your mind is engaged 
throughout this process. Tend to your reaction and take steps to relax and engage. 
6. CONSULT with colleagues or experts -Emotions can interfere. Obtain 
consultation. This is important from a legal standpoint as well. 
7. Consider the context -Be culturally competent. What ramifications might there be 
for the client’s family, community, other professionals etc.
8. Make a decision and act on your choice -Don’t linger in indecision. Have courage 
to come to a decision and act it out.

This week’s 
CCS-R 
Professional 
Disposition 
Competencies

Supervisors will evaluate you on the following
•2.F Multicultural : Demonstrates awareness, appreciation and respect of cultural 
difference (rae, ethnicity, spirituality, sexual orientation, disability, SES, etc)
•2.K Congruence and Genuineness : Demonstrates ability to be present and true to 
oneself
•2.G Emotional Stability and Self Control : Demonstrates emotional stability 
(congruence between mood and affect), and self control (impuse control) in 
relationships with supervisor peers and clients.

Discussion
•How do we balance genuineness with not imposing values? Your text says
“A counselor’s values will influence the counseling process in many ways (Levitt & Moorhead, 
2013), including which goals are considered appropriate or desirable in counseling, whether 
and how the client will be diagnosed, and which client issues will become the focus of 
therapy. Even the theory from which the counselor operates can influence the counseling 
process in value -laden ways: Certain theories promote specific values. For instance, the 
Adlerian approach emphasizes the development of social interest as a benchmark of mental 
health, whereas the existential approach emphasizes individual freedom and responsibility. 
The influence of counselor values on the counseling process can be very subtle, and 
counselors can communicate their values in many indirect ways: through nonverbal 
responses, by focusing on some elements of a client’s story while not responding to others, 
and through the interventions they select (Francis & Dugger, 2014 as cited in Remley & 
Herlihy, 2024 p. 77)

References
American Counseling Association. (2014). 2014 ACA code of ethics: As approved by the ACA governing 
council  [Electronic version]. https://www.counseling.org/Resources/aca -code -of-ethics.pdf
Herlihy B. & Corey G.  (2015) ACA Ethical Standard’s Casebook. American Counseling Association
Remley, P . R. & Herlihy, B. (2025). Ethical, Legal, and Professional Issues in Counseling. Pearson
Sells, J. N., & Hagedorn, W. B. (2016). CACREP Accreditation, Ethics, and the Affirmation of Both 
Religious and Sexual Identities: A Response to Smith and Okech. Journal of Counseling & 
Development ,94(3), 265 –279. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcad.12083
Watson T. (January 4, 2024).  Character development in the classroom: encouraging students’ growth 
in Christ -likeness. Northwest University Faculty ProDay 
Watson, T. S. (2018). Developing clinicians of character: A Christian integrative approach to clinical 
supervision . Downers Grove, IL: InterVarsity Press

Client rights
•Counselors are responsible for safeguarding clients’ welfare from the initiation of the 
counseling relationship and extend through termination of the relationship. see NBCC 
Booklet for clients
•This does not mean you work 24 -7 but that you have plans in place for example 
you do not give out your cell number or be available outside of work hours for 
your client.  You provide clients to call a 24 -hours mental health line, 911 or go to 
an emergency room when you are not available.
•Counselors have a fiduciary relationship with their clients (this is a legal term 
meaning you act in their benefit). Clients are not there for your benefit, you are there 
for them.

Awareness of personal needs and motivations
•Your history and reactions will come into play. They can become entangled in the 
counseling relationships. This is called a countertransference reaction . See Case of 
Lynn on pg. 87
•Personal motivations in keeping clients, Video example 4.1

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 8/9)
content:
Mandated clients
•Mandated clients may refuse services we must explain the consequences of their refusal (ACA, A. 2. e). We also 
must be accurate, honest and objective in the evaluations (ACA, 2014, C. 6. b.)
•Counselors must work to provide a safe context with clear guidelines and transparency.
•Court mandates often require disclosure to the court. Make it clear to the client if the purpose of the meeting 
is evaluation that will be disclosed or counseling.  
•Consider viewing counseling and evaluation and two separate process for these clients, involving two different 
clinicians “Reporting on counseling attendance and progress toward reaching counseling goals set by clients is 
different from evaluating clients for issues such as fitness for parenting, ability to work after an injury, a mental 
condition, or progress toward eligibility for parole.”
•See p. 477 for disclosure example for a mandated client. 
•Written informed consent is required to move from a therapeutic role to an evaluative role.

Treatment plans and techniques  
•Establishing goals and treatment plans should be done jointly with clients. What are 
their hopes for counseling? What do they want to focus on?
•Specialized techniques should be grounded in empirical research (ACA, 2014, C.7.a.) 
and counselors should ensure they have completed training and are aware of diversity 
considerations.
•Experimental techniques should be discussed and may require written disclosure 
•Potential risks and benefits should be disclosed to the client

Termination reasons
•Client has reached their goals and no longer needs assistanece
•Client is not benefiting or is being harmed by counseling
•Client does not pay the fees agreed upon
•The counselor is in jeopardy of being harmed by the client or someone the client has a 
relationship with 
•The counselor and client moves
•Sometimes termination is unexpected, in this case a plan should be in place to safeguard the 
client’s welfare. It is a good idea to have a plan on who would notify your clients/who has 
access to client records in a severe health or emergency situation

Avoiding abandonment (inappropriate 
termination, ACA, 2014, A.12)
•Do not terminate when the client is in crisis, when the client is angry or has an objection to counseling.
•Honestly discuss with the client your intention to terminate and the specific reason.
•Give the client an opportunity to make changes that would be necessary to continue the relationship.
•Give written notice to the client that the relationship will be terminated.
•Offer the client one to three termination sessions.
•Give your client up to three good referrals.
•Give your client places to contact in the event of emergencies.
•Place in the client’s file a summary of your interactions with the client that are related to the termination issue. 
Do not transfer that document to another individual or entity without an express written request from the client.
•Give the client plenty of time to find another mental health professional. If more time is requested, allow it.
•Transfer records to the new mental health professional promptly.

Counseling Techniques
•Is my approach a good fit for this client?
How might I need to adjust?
•Counselor have an obligation to select appropriate counseling strategies or 
techniques. 
•Any techniques that could be considered experimental or unusual should be 
used very cautiously
•Strategies should fit your counseling style and be tailored to the specific needs 
of the client
•Critically, counselors should not use techniques that might cause harm to a client

Federal laws such the Health Insurance Portability 
and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and state laws 
require specific written consent to treatment be 
obtained from clients, prior to counseling services 
being rendered.

Getting informed consent from clients
•Securing the client’s informed consent is both a legal and an ethical obligation. 
•Demonstrates respect for a client’s autonomy and self -determination
•Integral to the formation of an effective therapeutic alliance
•The more that clients understand about how counseling works, the more they are 
likely to benefit from the therapeutic process.

Informed consent continued
•Written contracts for informed consent are commonly referred to as disclosure 
statements . 
•These documents disclose to clients the nature of the counseling relationship into 
which they are entering. 
•Serve as legal contracts that are signed by both the client and the counselor.
•It can be difficult to achieve a balance between providing too little information and 
giving clients so much information that they feel overwhelmed or intimidated. 
•video example 4.3

title: Slides: class 2-Ethics 2-2026 (Part 9/9)
content:
Diversity considerations
•Diversity considerations:
•Counselors must be sensitive to the fact that informed consent, as traditionally 
conceptualized, may not be respectful of people of all cultures.
•Using language that clients can understand is equally important with clients who 
are children, adults who are not well educated, and clients whose first language 
is not English.

Informed consent is an ongoing process that 
should be conducted both orally and in 
written form.

•Watch video 4. 3 and read the case of Ibrahim

Informed consent should include:
1. the purposes, goals, techniques, procedures, limitations, and potential risks and 
benefits of services; 
2. the counselor’s qualifications;
3. information about termination and referrals;
4.  emergency protocols and crisis resources; 
5. the role of technology ( and a backup plan if tech fails); 
6. the implications of diagnosis and intended use of testing and reports; 
7. fees and billing information; 
8. confidentiality and its limitations; 
9. the right to obtain records and actively participate in ongoing counseling plans ( see 
HIPPA requirements to include regarding disclosure on p. 98 ); 
10. and the right to refuse recommended services or modality change and be advised 
of the consequences of refusal.

8 areas that, if not handled properly, could lead to 
legal problems (see examples in text, p. 100)
•Failing to include required H  I  P  A  A elements 
•Guaranteeing an outcome as a result of counseling 
•Guaranteeing privacy with no exceptions
•Agreeing to fee arrangements different from what was intended
•Touching without implied or actual permission
•Misrepresenting credentials
•Failing to communicate the nature of counseling
•Neglecting to warn client about possible stigma

Informed Consent Documents
•In groups of 2 -3 look at the counseling disclosure agreements on pg. 
472-475, compared to one of the examples in Discovery. Some are 
missing required elements. 
•What items are missing?
•What similarities and differences do you notice?
•Is there anything you might add if you were creating an informed 
consent document

content:
ETHICS CLASS 3
JANUARY 29
CONFIDENTIALITY AND PRIVILEGED 
COMMUNICATION

AGENDAClinical Training Q’s
Client Right and Informed Consent
•Time with partners to work on your consent forms
Faith, Fidelity and Responsibility
Confidentiality 
•Rational
•Exceptions
•Duty to warn, protect and report
•Panel activity
Privileged Communication

ELC: SITE 
SELECTIONGo to the ELC:
ONLY AFTER YOU HAVE RECIEVED AND ACCEPTED AN 
OFFER!
1.Select a site (or ‘suggest a site’ for new sites)
2.Select a supervisor (or start an application for 
new supervisors)
3.Supervision Dates Form (first day of Spring 
Semester (4/27/26) and go to August of 2026 
(8/6/2027)
4.Professional Practice Agreement
(this form is signed by you, your supervisor and 
me)

REVIEW : WHEN IS IT APPROPRIATE 
TO REFER A CLIENT? (ACA, 2014, 
A.11 A,C)
•Client no longer needs services (c)
•Client is unlikely to benefit (c)
•Client is at risk of being harmed (c)
•Client does not pay fees (c)
•Client or someone the client knows puts the counselor in 
jeopardy of harm (c)
•Counselor lacks competency in a clinical area to be of 
professional assistance to a client (outside of the clinician’s scope 
of practice) (a)

REVIEW: WHEN WOULD IT BE 
INAPPROPRIATE TO REFER 
ACCORDING TO ACA CODE (A.11 B, D, 
A.12?)
•Discrimination
•Differences in values, attitudes, 
beliefs, behavior or personality 
preferences 
•Abandonment considerations 
(when client is in crisis, and other 
services are not set up; termination 
services have not occurred)

CLIENT RIGHTS

Client rights
•Counselors are responsible for safeguarding clients’ welfare from the 
initiation of the counseling relationship and extend through termination of the 
relationship. see NBCC Booklet for clients
•This does not mean you work 24 -7 but that you have plans in place for 
example you do not give out your cell number or be available outside of 
work hours for your client.  You provide clients to call a 24 -hours mental 
health line, 911 or go to an emergency room when you are not available.
•Counselors have a fiduciary relationship with their clients (this is a legal 
term meaning you act in their benefit). Clients are not there for your benefit, 
you are there for them.

Awareness of personal needs and motivations
•As counselors, we get the chance to work with and care for people we never 
thought we would. It is a high calling and privilege to love people from varied 
backgrounds. 
•However, your history and reactions will come into play. They can become 
entangled in the counseling relationships. This is called a countertransference 
reaction . See Case of Lynn on pg. 87
•Personal motivations in keeping clients, Video example 4.1

title: Slides: class 3, Ethics 2026 (Part 2/10)
content:
The counselor as a person and a professional
•Counselors must be aware of the influence of their own personality and needs
•Personal needs of counselors based on unresolved personal conflicts:
•a need to tell people what to do
•a desire to take away all pain from clients
•a need to have all the answers and to be perfect
•a need to be recognized and appreciated
•a tendency to assume too much responsibility for the changes of clients
•a fear of doing harm, however inadvertently
•Corey et al., Issues and Ethics - Chapter 2 (1)

T ransference
•Transference is the process whereby clients project onto their therapists past 
feelings or attitudes they had toward significant people in their lives
•Transference: the “unreal” relationship in therapy
•Counselors need to be aware of their personal reactions to a client’s 
transference
•All reactions of clients to a therapist are not to be considered as transference
•Ethical issue is dealing appropriately with transference 
Cory et al. Issues and Ethics - Chapter 2 (2)

Recognize your own reactions
•Transference : The feelings, attitudes experiences your client projects onto you
•Countertransference  – YOUR feelings, attitudes and experiences that come up 
for you in your work with a client (you need to be aware of these)
•Desire to nurture?
•Desire to be liked?
•Reaction to an issue/topic that is presented
Discussion: How might countertransference reactions be dangerous in a 
therapeutic relationship

Countertransference check ins
•being overprotective with a client
•treating clients in kind ways
•rejecting a client
•needing constant reinforcement and approval
•seeing yourself in your clients
•developing sexual or romantic feelings for a client
•giving advice compulsively
•desiring a social relationship with clients

Examples of ways counselors might engage in ethically questionable 
behavior that would be difficult to detect and, thus, difficult to enforce:
•Prolonging the number of counseling sessions to satisfy the counselor's 
emotional or financial needs
•Being unaware of countertransference reactions to a client, student, or supervisee 
and thereby inadvertently increasing uncooperative behavior and thwarting 
growth
•Imposing values, goals, or strategies on clients that are not congruent with their 
cultural background
•Using techniques or strategies that are comfortable for the counselor rather than 
those that are aimed at helping clients achieve their therapeutic goals
•Practicing with little enthusiasm or tolerating boredom and apathy
•Ignoring self -care practices and thus not being able to practice effectively

Mandated clients
•Mandated clients may refuse services we must explain the consequences of their refusal (ACA, A. 2. e). We 
also must be accurate, honest and objective in the evaluations (ACA, 2014, C. 6. b.)
•Counselors must work to provide a safe context with clear guidelines and transparency.
•Court mandates often require disclosure to the court. Make it clear to the client if the purpose of the 
meeting is evaluation that will be disclosed or counseling.  
•Consider viewing counseling and evaluation and two separate process for these clients, involving two 
different clinicians “Reporting on counseling attendance and progress toward reaching counseling goals set 
by clients is different from evaluating clients for issues such as fitness for parenting, ability to work after an 
injury, a mental condition, or progress toward eligibility for parole.”
•See p. 477 for disclosure example for a mandated client. 
•Written informed consent is required to move from a therapeutic role to an evaluative role.

T reatment plans and techniques  
•Establishing goals and treatment plans should be done jointly with clients. 
What are their hopes for counseling? What do they want to focus on?
•Specialized techniques should be grounded in empirical research (ACA, 2014, 
C.7.a.) and counselors should ensure they have completed training and are 
aware of diversity considerations.
•Experimental techniques should be discussed and may require written 
disclosure 
•Potential risks and benefits should be disclosed to the client

T ermination reasons
•Client has reached their goals and no longer needs assistance
•Client is not benefiting or is being harmed by counseling
•Client does not pay the fees agreed upon
•The counselor is in jeopardy of being harmed by the client or someone the client has a 
relationship with 
•The counselor and client moves
•Sometimes termination is unexpected, in this case a plan should be in place to 
safeguard the client’s welfare. It is a good idea to have a plan on who would notify 
your clients/who has access to client records in a severe health or emergency situation

title: Slides: class 3, Ethics 2026 (Part 4/10)
content:
8 areas that, if not handled properly, could lead to 
legal problems (see examples in text, p. 100)
•Failing to include required H I  P  A A elements 
•Guaranteeing an outcome as a result of counseling 
•Guaranteeing privacy with no exceptions
•Agreeing to fee arrangements different from what was intended
•Touching without implied or actual permission
•Misrepresenting credentials

Informed consent should include:
1.the purposes, goals, techniques, procedures, limitations, and potential 
risks and benefits of services; 
2.the counselor’s qualifications;
3.information about termination and referrals;
4. emergency protocols and crisis resources; 
5.the role of technology (and a backup plan if tech fails); 
6.the implications of diagnosis and intended use of testing and reports; 
7.fees and billing information; 
8.confidentiality and its limitations; 
9.the right to obtain records and actively participate in ongoing 
counseling plans ( see HIPPA requirements to include regarding 
disclosure on p. 98 ); 
10.and the right to refuse recommended services or modality change and 
be advised of the consequences of refusal.

Informed Consent Documents
•In groups of 2 -3 look at the counseling disclosure agreements 
on pg. 472 -475, compared to one of the examples or your own 
in Discovery and the list provided. Some are missing required 
elements. 
•What items are missing?
•What similarities and differences do you notice?
•Is there anything you might add if you were creating an 
informed consent document

Faith and Fidelity
The professional ethical 
principle of fidelity: 
Keeping promises is 
related to the topics of 
privileged communication, 
privacy and confidentialityWatson relates the the 
theological virtue of faith  
with fidelity

WHAT IS 
FAITH?Aquinas wrote it involves “believing someone 
and believing something”
Have you ever had someone believe in you 
when you couldn’t see it?  What was the 
impact of this experience?
Hebrews 11 highlights the mystery in faith, 
being a part of something much bigger than 
can be understood in this life. How do
Now faith is confidence in what we hope for 
and assurance in about what we do not see . 
(Hebrews 11:1 -3, NIV)

Faith, fidelity and responsibility
•Watson (2018) argues many theologians connect faith with trust.  She says, “The virtue of 
faith in practice can be evidence by acts of fidelity and trustworthiness toward one’s 
community” (Watson, 2018, p. 42). Christian therapists can work to exhibit a virtue of faith not 
only in their spiritual and personal lives, but professionally as well. According to Watson, 
therapists live out faith through building trust in relationships with clients and colleagues . 
Studies over the last 25 years show trust is an essential ingredient in psychological and 
relationship health (Furrow et al., 2019). 
•While a general understanding of faith or trust in the counseling room is different from 
having faith in God, security and trust can be learned from being in relationship with God.
•Fidelity (same root as fiduciary) is: fulfilling a responsibility of trust in the counseling 
relationship by honoring commitments and keeping promises . Counselors strive to be 
faithful to the promises they make such as keeping clients disclosers confidential” (p. 11).

How does God 
see us? You are loved (Jeremiah 31:3) 
“I have loved you with an everlasting love” ”
You are cared for ( Naham  1:7) 
“God is a refuge in times of trouble, He cares for those who trust in him”
You are delighted in (Zephaniah 3:17)
“He will take great delight in you, he will quiet you with his love, he will rejoice 
over you with singing”
How might God’s example to us guide us in caring for clients? 
What do you hope your clients experience in their counseling relationship with you?

Cultivating faithWatson recommends two critical components for the Christian in 
developing faith: contemplative prayer and church involvement.
“For the mental health professional (student)...developing habits of 
prayers  is as essential as food and water for nourishing the soul and sustaining 
trust and belief in God during the rigors of graduate school, academic and life 
and professional practice” (Watson, 2018, p. 58)
Involvement in Christian community, regular worship, participation in the 
sacraments... remind us of the reality of God as creator, redeemer and 
sanctifier...and provide opportunity to realign and affirm our relationship 
with our creator. (Westberg, 2015, as cited in Watson)

title: Slides: class 3, Ethics 2026 (Part 5/10)
content:
Faith integration“we uphold the ethical principle of fidelity not only because we have to 
but because it has become an essential part of our personal and professional 
character, guiding our attitudes, activates and relationships”.  
(Watson, 2018, p. 46 -47)

Fidelity and 
responsibilityFidelity - “honoring commitments and keeping 
promises including fulfilling one’s responsibilities 
of trust in professional relationships ” (ACA, 2014)  
Which promises and responsibilities come to 
mind for you that are important to honor as

Counseling dispositions and behaviors (CCS -R 2d)

CONFIDENTIALITY AND 
PRIVILEGED 
COMMUNICATION

Key terms in 
counseling 
related to 
fidelity and 
respecting 
client 
autonomyPrivacy  – Broadest term, includes client has a right 
to privacy of their information and the rights of an 
individual to decide which information about 
themselves is shared with others.  
Confidentiality  – confidentiality implies an explicit 
contract or promise not to reveal anything about a 
client except under certain circumstances agreed to 
by both parties. 
Privileged communication – A legal term that 
protects clients from having confidential 
communications with their counselors disclosed in a 
court of law without their permission  (Law and 
courts)
 Court must prove that disclosure is vital for 
the wellbeing of society

Confidentiality is one of the most 
fundamental of all professional 
obligations in counseling.
Pearson Slide, 2025

Confidentiality (1 of 2)
•Helping professionals assume that an assurance of 
confidentiality is an indispensable requirement for 
effective counseling. 
•Without this assurance, many clients would not feel 
safe to openly discuss the intimate and personal 
aspects of their lives or would not seek counseling.
•Clients need to know that they can trust their counselors 
to respect their privacy 
•A counselor’s confidentiality pledge is the 
cornerstone on which this trust is built . 
Pearson Slide, 2025

Confidentiality (2 of 2)
•Fidelity : being faithful and keeping promises
•One of the moral principles on which confidentiality 
rests
•Accordingly, one of the most important promises that 
counselors make to clients is that they will keep the 
secrets shared in counseling.
Pearson Slide, 2025

addressed

mandated by legal as well as ethical considerations, 
the standards are carefully written to minimize conflicts 
between law and ethics

title: Slides: class 3, Ethics 2026 (Part 7/10)
content:
1976 Tarasoff vs University of California 
•Counselors are charged with violence prevention
•You must assess for harm that clients have the POTENTIAL to act on
•Poddar became obsessed with another student. He was mentally ill. He told a doctor 
about his obsession and desire to kill Tatiana Tarasoff. The doctor called the police, 
the police called him in, he said he wouldn’t act. Poddar then did kill Tarasoff. Her 
family sued the University.  Went to the supreme court and decided that the doctors 
could be liable for not protecting Tatiana. Before this there was an assumption that 
you couldn’t control the actions of another.
•Therapist must take reasonable steps to care and protect the intended victim . 
One of these steps may be to warn the potential victim, notify the police or whatever 
other steps may be necessary to protect the victim. 
•CA has a law that counselors are immune from liability when a client expresses to 
harm another, and the therapist should inform the victim and the police. 
Shaub, 2023

Washington State case
•Volk v. Demeerleer
•Demeerleer  (hx of severe mental illness) murdered his girlfriend Rebecca. 
•Ashby was the psy chiatrist 
•No hx of violence but rageful thoughts.  
•April 2010 Ashby saw him and reported SI but no plan to act, Ashby reports stability
•July DeMeerleer  kills girlfriend Rebecca and one of her sons
•Rebecca’s mother sues Ashby’s clinic
•Ashby says in court Demeerleer  never identified who he might hurt or any homicidal ideation
•The opposing argument was that Ashby did not provide a thorough enough assessment and 
monitoring, psychiatrist could have gotten family involved, scheduled more appointments etc.

Washington State
Supreme court said:
“We hold that Ashby and DeMeerleer shared a special relationship and that special relationship required 
Ashby to act with reasonable care, consistent with the standards of the mental health profession to 
protect the foreseeable victims of DeMeerleer” 
A therapist’s duty is NOT limited to a duty to warn in WA. The duty to protect can arise even if the client 
does NOT express an intention to harm a reasonably identifiable victim.
These kinds of cases can go to court to be argued/discussed.  This case was settled on 12/19/17. It never 
went to trial. We don’t know what would have happened, but it didn’t get thrown out.
Interesting case. You can look up this article: The Therapist and the Murderer: A psychiatrist and a 
lawyer analyze Volk v. DeMeerleer. Published in: NW Lawyer,  Feb2018, By:Berner, Jon;Preston, Patrick J.  
NW Lawyer; Feb2018, Vol. 72 Issue 1, p16 -26, 11p

This could look like -

situation

What is Child Abuse and Neglect?
 According to Washington State
•RCW 26 -44-020 (12)  defines abuse and neglect as injury, sexual abuse, sexual 
exploitation, negligent treatment or maltreatment of a child by any person under 
circumstances which indicate that the child's health, welfare, and safety is harmed. 
Abuse and neglect does NOT include the physical discipline of a child as defined in 
• RCW 9A.16.100  -It is the policy of this state to protect children from assault and abuse 
and to encourage parents, teachers, and their authorized agents to use methods of 
correction and restraint of children that are not dangerous to the children. However, the 
physical discipline of a child is not unlawful when it is reasonable and moderate and is 
inflicted by a parent, teacher, or guardian for purposes of restraining or correcting the 
child. Any use of force on a child by any other person is unlawful unless it is reasonable 
and moderate and is authorized in advance by the child's parent or guardian for 
purposes of restraining or correcting the child.
 The following actions are presumed unreasonable when used to correct or restrain a 
child: (1) Throwing, kicking, burning, or cutting a child; (2) striking a child with a closed 
fist; (3) shaking a child under age three; (4) interfering with a child's breathing; (5) 
threatening a child with a deadly weapon; or (6) doing any other act that is likely to 
cause and which does cause bodily harm greater than transient pain or minor 
temporary marks. The age, size, and condition of the child and the location of the injury 
shall be considered when determining whether the bodily harm is reasonable or 
moderate. This list is illustrative of unreasonable actions and is not intended to be 
exclusive.

Who is a Vulnerable Adult? 
 Washington State Department of Social and Human Services
A vulnerable adult is defined in WA law as:
•a person 60 years of age or older who 
lacks the functional, physical, or mental 
ability to care for him or herself; 
•an adult with a developmental disability 
per 71A.10.020 ; 
•an adult with a legal guardian per 11.88 
RCW ; 
•an adult living in a long -term care facility 
(an adult family home, boarding home or nursing home); 
•an adult living in their own or family’s 
home receiving services from an agency 
or contracted individual provider; or an 
adult self -directing their care per law 
(74.39.050 RCW )

Keep in mind
•Every decision involves both a yes and a no
•Decisions to breach confidentiality are nuanced and numerous. Consult 
consult consult. Is disclosure? required? Who should make the disclosure? 
Which individuals should be informed? Should the client be involved? How 
do you talk about disclosures with clients?

Privileged communication is a legal 
concept that is closely related to 
confidentiality but is different.

Privileged Communication (1 of 2)
•Privileged communication laws protect clients from having 
confidential communications with their counselors 
disclosed in a court of law without their permission.
•Means that a judge cannot order a counselor to reveal 
information in court that has been recognized by law 
as privileged.

Asserting Privilege
•A statutory privilege belongs to clients rather than to 
counselors. 
•If you or your records are subpoenaed it is up to your 
client to assert the privilege so that you will not have to 
disclose the information.
•If a client cannot be located when counselors are asked to 
disclose privileged information, the counselor has an 
obligation to assert the privilege on behalf of the client. 
•Counselors should secure the advice of an attorney if they 
are put in the position of asserting a client’s privilege.

Exceptions to Privileged Communication (1 of 2)
•Glosoff  et al. (2000) categorized exceptions into nine areas (listed from the 
most to the least frequently cited in state statutes):
•Complaints filed include after a counselor and client have had a dispute 
about another matter (such as billing or disagreements)
•When a client is litigating their mental condition in a lawsuit
•When a client might be a danger to self or others
•When a report of child abuse has to be filed
•When a client is contemplating committing a serious crime that might 
harm others
•When a client is ordered to have a psychological examination by a judge
•When a legal proceeding is held to determine if a client needs to be 
involuntarily hospitalized
•When a client has been a victim in a crime
•When a client has been accused or harming a vulnerable adult

Privilege belongs to clients not counselors 
•It is up to the client to assert privileged communication unless:
•the client cannot be located, then the counselor should assert privilege
•If the client is deceased, the individual's legal representative should assert 
privilege 
•See case example: video 5.1 caught in the middle

WA state

pertaining to

5.105

Activity1.Your group will receive a case study
2.Each group member should take a different role as stakeholders/members 
of a panel (examples may be the counselor, a teacher, a doctor, a lawyer, a 
supervisor, a peer consultant, a parent (if applicable), an agency director, a 
CPS representative, psychiatrist, etc-be creative) who will prepare answers 
regarding the legal and ethical issues  raised in their assigned case.
1.Panel Question and Answer Session: 5 - 7 minutes per group.
•One at a time, each panel will take their place in seats in front of the 
classroom.
•One panel member will introduce each member and give their expert 
credentials (be creative).
•Another panel member will explain or read the case to the class.
•Once the case has been read, the panel provides their expert analysis and 
recommendations.
•The panel then asks if there are any questions the audience has regarding 
this case. The panel will answer the questions that another group has 
prepared regarding this case.

content:

Records and 
Subpoenas

ACA ON 
RECORDS: 
WHAT 
STANDS 
OUT OR 
SUPRISES 
YOU?•B.6.a -B.6.h
•B.6.a. – Counselors maintain documentation necessary for 
rendering professional services to their clients. 
•B.6.b. – Records must be kept private/secure.
•B.6.c. – Must have permission from client to record.
•B.6.d. – Must have permission to observe sessions or review 
transcripts or review recordings of sessions.
•B.6.e. – Competent clients should have reasonable access to 
their records. 
•B.6.f. – Counselors provide assistance and consultation to clients 
to help them interpret counseling records. 
•B.6.g.. – Counselors obtain written permission from clients to 
disclose or transfer records to third parties.
•B.6.h. – Counselors securely store records following termination. 
Follow federal and state laws in disposing records keeping ( 5 
years in WA per WAC 246 -809-035). Be cautious about destroying 
records that may be needed in court (child abuse, suicide, 
harassment, or violence).

WHY SHOULD 
COUNSELORS 
KEEP GOOD 
NOTES?•Includes plans for a case, keeps track of goals and progress, measures change 
and growth, affirm significant turning points
•Documents important actions/decisions. Records can serve to protect 
counselors. If a counselor’s decision are challenged, it is critical that they have 
documented decisions and the action steps they have taken.
•Helps counselor remember where the session left off (especially if infrequent 
session occur or a break)
•Important for continuity of care if a client changes counselors
•Are primarily for a counselor’s used for maximum effectiveness in treatment but 
keep in mind that clients may ask to see them and at some point, they could be 
subpoenaed.  Clinicians should write note with the understanding that someone 
may read your notes at some later date ( Herlihy & Corey, 2015  
•Should be written with enough clarity for another professional to follow
•Records procedures followed in an emergency 
Also, consider your setting. WHY you are taking notes. Is it primarily for your 
benefit? Is the client in the hospital and you are tracking progress, are other 
professionals likely to see the notes? Is it likely to be subpoenaed?

A FEW IMPORTANT 
CONSIDERATIONS 
REGARDING NOTE 
TAKINGSOAP notes (basic tool to follow, your site may have other suggestions/requirements)
•Subjective -client reported...(perceptions, feelings experiences)
•Objective - “counselor observed....(hand ringing, lack of eye contact, unkempt 
appearance”  
•Assessment - Making sense of S and O
•Plan- Action steps you took (for client and/or therapist) towards treatment goals
Or DAP ( Data, Assessment, Plan)
Read Case 6 -4 and write a DAP or SOAP note

A FEW IMPORTANT 
CONSIDERATIONS

experiences)

towards treatment goals

Read Case 6 -4 and write a DAP or SOAP note

WRITING WHAT 
WE DID AS

PART OF NOTESInterventions you offered (example)
