that you utilized them in session.

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 2/9)
content:
OTHER 
RECORDS 
COUNSELORS 
KEEP•Administrative Records – appt. books, billing 
accounts, correspondence, intake forms, and 
other routine papers.
•Audio Recordings or Video Recordings of 
clients.
•Records of client logging into computer 
information system, telephone call records, 
computerized or video records of client 
entering or leaving the facilities. 
•Any other documentation made about client 
outside of the counseling session.

AUDIO AND 
VIDEO TAPES•Often used in training and supervision (you will 
do this for your practicum and internship). 
Counselors and supervisors are responsible for:
•Getting informed consent
•After review in supervision, erase the tapes (end 
of each semester in clinical training).
•If labeled, do not bring attention the 
confidential nature of the videos
•supervisors are responsible for returning any 
tapes to the supervisee and handling the tapes 
appropriately.

Read the case of Paolo on p. 136 -137
•What required information listed here may be in excess to content that is 
typically included? 
•Would you suggest that Paolo take further steps to challenge the requirement? 
Why or why not? 
•What step(s) can Paolo take to ensure that his record keeping is in compliance 
with his agency’s expectations? 
•What rationale might Paolo provide to his clients for requiring them to 
complete 12 pages of intake forms?

If a client requests their records 
1.Document and get a signed document recording release of the record
2.Explain any risks of disclosure (no longer under agency protection, misinterpretation etc.), consider offering 
a summary of pertinent information
3.Ensure the records would not harm the client
4.Ensure other clients information is not disclosed
5.Provide assistance and consultation to review the record (consider charging a fee for this service if 
extensive)
6.Have a clear written policy

ACTIVITY:
WATCH THE 6.1 VIDEO 
AND ANSWER THE 
FOLLOWING 
QUESTIONS1. In the video, the client states: “I know you’ve been keeping little 
notes on me to share with other people.” Assuming the client was 
in a more calm and receptive state, what response should the 
counselor provide to clarify the client’s misconception? 
Review the client’s right to confidentiality, noting that the client must provide 
written consent for any release of her information.  
2. What is a solid rationale, rooted in counseling ethics, that 
supports the counselor’s decision to limit a client’s access to their 
counseling records?
Counselors may exercise discretion with sharing client records. In instances where 
providing full access to records would likely harm the client or damage the 
therapeutic alliance, it is ethically permissible for a counselor to limit a client’s 
records. Based on the client’s words and demeanor, it can be reasonably assumed 
that she would be harmed by gaining access to her full case file. 
3. What would you say to a client who requested to view records 
forwarded from outside mental health professionals, such as 
previous  counselors or psychiatrists?
If subpoenaed client records contain files and reports that were received from 
outside mental health professionals, counselors should request for the court to 
subpoena the records directly from the originating clinician. 
4. Practice writing a SOAP or DAP note for this portion of the 
session (see p. 145 -146):

Counselors must take steps to 
ensure client notes remain 
confidential. What steps are 
important (see case 6 -2). However, 
you should also assume others will 
see your records.

THE HEALTH 
INSURANCE 
PORTABILITY AND 
ACCOUNTABILITY 
ACT (HIPAA) 
REQUIRES Counselors must:
•give clients a clear written explanation of how 
they use, keep, and disclose their health care 
information.
•give clients access to their records (unless it 
would cause them harm)
•follow a written process for clients to request 
amendments to their records.
•keep a written history of disclosures of client 
information, available to clients

HIPPA 
EXCEPTIONS FOR 
CLIENTS THAT 
NEED EXTRA 
SUPPORT (P . 150)•If clients are informed and given an 
opportunity to orally object counselors may 
give health case information to family 
members or others assisting in the client’s care 
(consider adding this to informed consent 
documents)
•Counselors may provide information disaster 
relief organizations, such as the Red Cross.

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 3/9)
content:
SUBPOENASSubpoenas are used:
•to obtain copies of records.
•to cause potential witnesses to testify at 
a deposition, hearing, or trial.
•to ask potential witnesses to respond in 
writing to questions.
•to require witnesses to appear at a 
deposition, hearing, or trail and to bring 
their records with them.

IF YOU ARE 
SUBPOENAED :•Don’t ignore it but get advice before responding. I
•Contact your client, did they receive the subpoena? 
•Talk with your supervisor about obtaining legal 
advice or seek it yourself.  Is the information 
requested privileged communication? 
•If you are working for an agency, check with them 
before you hire an attorney on your own. 
•Do not just hand over records . There are 
procedures lawyers must follow to give health care 
professionals a subpoena (see RCW 70.02.060) and 
the information may be privileged
•ACA Code of Ethics B.2.d. (2014) recommends 
counselors obtain a written release of information 
from their client to release records.

CCS-R, (E) ON RECORDS

COUNSELOR COMPETENCY

CompetencyWHAT IS COUNSELOR COMPETENCY?
 A complex construct with many possible levels 
along a continuum 
•Ethical and legal considerations “do no 
harm”, nonmaleficence is the most salient 
moral principle.
•Skill considerations “perform at an 
acceptable level”
•Practice considerations ”Within the 
boundaries of their competence”
•Offering ethical referrals when clients are 
outside counselor’s area of service (not 
discriminatory reasons )

Ways 
graduate 
programs 
attempt to 
ensure 
competent 
graduates:  •Selecting and admitting individuals who are likely to succeed at 
developing the skills, knowledge, and characteristics needed to 
become effective counselors.
•Using personal interviews, written essays, or other subjective 
criteria in selecting candidates for admission.
•Providing curriculum, feedback, and guidance for master’s 
students that prepare students to be effective counselors.
•Requiring successful supervised experience as a part of the 
graduate program.
•Pointing to necessary areas of growth and offering remediation 
for students who fail to meet requirements
•Professors we are called to be gatekeepers for the profession.

How does the field encourage counselor competency?

LICENSURE Professional licensure statues establish the minimum standards for 
practice as a licensed provider.  It is the most powerful type of 
credentialling established by state law. 
Is typically required for practice, billing insurance, practice as a clinician
Legislatures choose to license professions so that the public can 
determine which practitioners are competent, and to prevent harm by 
incompetent members of that profession.
All 50 states offer counseling licenses -reciprocity continues to be a 
salient issue since due to the variety in requirements from state to state 
but we are making progress: https:// counselingcompact.org /map/
Typically needed to bill insurance 
Certification (broader) also offers a way to support evidence of 
competency and are often a way to show areas of specially .

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 4/9)
content:
WASHINGTON 
COUNSELING LICENSES
 
Https://doh.Wa.Gov/lice
nses-permits -and-
certificates/professions -
new-renew -or-
update/mental -health -
counselor/licensing -
information
 LMHC -A
•education requirements (this degree)
•application
LMHC
•educational requirements
•application
•Passing score on NCE or NCMHCE  Exam
•3000 hours (1200 direct,100 supervision hours) or 3 years 
in full time work as a counselor
.

CERTIFICATION
 Certification (broader) also offers a way to support evidence 
of competency and are often a way to show areas of 
specially .
•Counselors can offer evidence that supports their 
competence by becoming certified
•Various types of counselor certifications exist
•Some types of certification are mandatory for practicing 
in certain settings and occur at the state level (e.g., 
public school counselors must be certified by the states 
in which they practice)
•There are also national, voluntary certifications, such as 
those offered by the National Board of Certified 
Counselors
•Specialty certifications are another means of establishing 
a counselor’s competence to work with certain types of 
clients, in certain settings, or in specialized areas of 
counseling. 
•Specialty certifications are offered by various groups in 
such areas as hypnotherapy, biofeedback, and sex 
therapy
Pearson, 2025

Once counselors begin to practice, they 
are responsible for determining their own 
competence.
Pearson, 2025

Maintaining Competence (1 of 4)
•Continuing Education:
•The A C A Code of Ethics  (2014) states that counselors 
recognize the need for continuing education to acquire 
and maintain awareness of current scientific and 
professional information.
•Most counselor licensure and certification boards have 
established continuing education requirements for 
maintaining one’s license. 
Pearson, 2025

Maintaining Competence (2 of 4)
•Peer Review/Peer Consultation:
•Useful for counselors at all levels of experience and 
should continue as a lifelong learning process. 
•Benefits include enhanced self -awareness, mutual 
support, objective feedback in dealing with 
countertransference issues, information on therapeutic 
techniques, and/or help in dealing with difficult cases, 
stress, and isolation.
Pearson, 2025

Maintaining Competence (3 of 4)
•Technology:
•The internet presents counselors with myriad 
opportunities for keeping current and improving their 
competence as practitioners. 
Pearson, 2025

Maintaining Competence (4 of 4)
•Making Referrals:
•The A C A Code of Ethics  (2014) states that counselors 
avoid entering into a therapeutic relationship when 
they lack the competence to counsel a prospective 
client. 
•Ethical counselors recognize that they will need to refer 
a client when accepting or continuing to work with 
that client would exceed their boundaries of 
competence. 
•Referrals should be based on competence and rather 
than discrimination.
Pearson, 2025

Erosion of Competence (1 of 3)
•Burnout and Impairment:
•The work of the counselor can be stressful and 
emotionally demanding
•Most counselors could probably be described as 
distressed  at some time during their professional lives
•Unalleviated distress can lead to burnout
•Burned -out counselors have little energy left for their 
clients
•Common indicators of burnout include decline in self -
care, strain in personal relationships, and damage to 
overall emotional health
Pearson, 2025

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 5/9)
content:
Erosion of Competence (2 of 3)
•Impaired counselors are unable to perform their 
responsibilities appropriately, thus, putting their clients at 
risk.
•Impairment is often associated with alcohol and other 
drug abuse and with the blurring of therapeutic 
boundaries that can lead to sexual exploitation of clients. 
•Risk factors for impairment include:
•environmental factors 
•vicarious traumatization 
•preexisting conditions 
Pearson, 2025

Erosion of Competence (3 of 3)
•Corey et al. (2024) have suggested that counselors ask 
themselves some of the following questions if they think 
they might be experiencing burnout:
•Do you ask supervisors or peers for help when you 
need it?
•Are you willing to seek personal counseling if you think 
you need it?
•Do you engage regularly in self -care practices?
•Are you engaged in life outside of work?
•Are you staying connected to family members and 
friends?
Pearson, 2025

2014 ACA codes on impairment
•Read sections C.2.g and F.5.b and identify the following:
What are counselors, students and supervises asked to monitor in 
themselves?
What actions are they asked to take?
•What signs of mild, moderate and severe impairment would you need 
to look for in yourself? What steps would you take at each level?
•What would you do if you noticed a supervisor or colleague showing 
signs of impairment?

If counselors determine they are 
impaired, they must limit, suspend, or 
terminate their professional 
responsibilities and seek assistance to 
address their problems.
Pearson, 2025

CHRISTIAN CHARACTER VIRTUES, WATSON, 2018
Character virtue Professional Ethic Supervision models Spiritual disciplines 
Faith Fidelity Alliance & attachment Cont. Prayer & Church 
Hope Beneficence Vocational mentoring Sabbath & Worship 
Love Respect/autonomy Relational Competence Intercessory prayer 
Justice Justice Diversity Competence Hospitality & Service 
Temperance Nonmaleficence Reflective practice Examen & Fasting 
Courage Integrity/veracity Wellness & Resilience Rule of life & Example 
Practical wisdom Competence Competency based Scripture & discern.

TO CULTIVATE WISDOM (WATSON, 2018)
Dallas Willard in his book on spiritual disciplines writes, “ Study  as 
a spiritual discipline is the focusing of the mind upon God’s works 
and words. In study our mind takes on the order in the object 
studied, and order invariably forms the mind itself and thereby 
the soul and the life arising out of it.” (1998) 
“Regular study of God’s Word informs our inward and outward 
habits of thinking and action towards God’s kingdom” (p. 117).What do you study to gain 
wisdom? Who do you 
respect? Where do you go 
to gain wisdom?
What habits do have to 
cultivate wisdom?

DISCERNMENT (WATSON, 2018) 
Ignatius of Loyola, founder of the Jesuits from the 1500s, 
addressed discernment as a habit, a way of seeing that 
eventually permeates life... He defined the aim of discernment 
as ”finding God in all things in order that we might love and 
serve God in all ” 
 Ruth Haley Barton (2008) 
explains that prayerful 
discernment includes: 
•Collecting and considering 
data 
•Inner reflection and 
confirmation
•Action
•Submission to God’s will and 
guidance

Competence for Terri Watson is supported spiritually by 
seeking God’s wisdom through scripture reading and 
practicing discernment. 
What practices  will be important for you to take to help 
you to act competently in the face of inevitable 
challenges? What are the risks you might encounter that 
could test your ability to make competent decisions in 
counseling?

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 6/9)
content:
Intakes: video 7.1
•In the video, the counselor learns from the client’s intake forms that she is a 
single mother who recently finalized a divorce. Yet, the counselor states: 
“Coming in today, did you have anything you wanted to talk about? Does the 
counselor’s decision to not immediately inquire about the client’s divorce 
illustrate an irresponsible approach to the intake interview? Why, or why not?
•How does the counselor utilize the concept of birth order in a manner that is 
strengths -based and respects his client’s unique identity, rather than reducing 
her personality and attitudes to a narrow label or a confined category?

Assessment
•Counselors have the education and skills necessary to 
conduct certain types of evaluations. 
•Some counselors have jobs in which they conduct 
evaluations on a regular basis
•Some counselors have private practices in which they 
conduct evaluations occasionally or exclusively. 
•To conduct an evaluation, a counselor should have 
expertise in the area that is being evaluated. 
Pearson, 2025

Formal Evaluations
•Clients who are being assessed need to understand that:
•the process is different from counseling 
•what the process entails
•what the final product will be
•how and by whom the assessment is intended to be 
used. 
•According to the A C A Code of Ethics  (2014), the client’s 
written  informed consent should be secured before 
beginning a forensic evaluation ( A C A, 2014). 
Pearson, 2025

Counselor as Expert Witness
•Counselors should avoid conducting evaluations that cast 
them in the role of expert witness unless they have agreed 
to do so. 
•If your job requires that you conduct evaluations, or if you 
complete evaluations in your private practice, you must be 
prepared to testify in court as an expert witness. 
•There is a good chance that eventually you will be 
subpoenaed and ordered to explain and defend your 
conclusions and recommendations.
Pearson, 2025

T esting
•Counselors use tests in various ways. 
•Counselors typically select existing tests, administer them, 
and interpret them for their clients. 
•Competence to use a test always depends on the 
individual counselor’s training and experience with the 
particular test.
•Clients have the right to make an informed choice about 
whether they want to participate or refuse to engage in 
testing procedures.
•Clients can also expect to receive an explanation of the 
results of any test they take. 
Pearson, 2025

Diagnosis (1 of 2)
•Counselors are sometimes uncomfortable with 
participating in the diagnosis of mental disorders. 
•Diagnoses usually are made using the D S M-5-T R 
(American Psychiatric Association, 2022).
•This system of diagnosis is based on the medical model of 
mental illness.
•When clients want to use their health insurance to help 
pay for mental health care, counselors typically must 
assign a diagnosis for their services to be reimbursable. 
Pearson, 2025

Diagnosis (2 of 2)
•Counselors must be knowledgeable about laws that affect 
testing practices.
•Clients must understand the implications of any diagnosis 
that might be assigned to them.
•Counselors must be prepared to work cooperatively with 
physicians when: 
•clients may have a physical condition that is 
contributing to their mental or emotional problems
•clients could benefit from taking medications for their 
condition
Pearson, 2025

Video 7.2
•In the video, the client expresses concern about his diagnosis being reported 
to his insurance company, due to the stigma he associates with bipolar 
disorder. Because the client needs a diagnosis in order to receive health 
insurance reimbursement, is it ethical for the counselor to code a diagnosis 
that the client feels is less -stigmatized, but also reflects similar diagnostic 
criteria? Why, or why not?  In the video, the client is deeply troubled by the provisional diagnosis he 
receives. Imagine that the counselor could have anticipated the impact that this 
diagnosis has had on her client and, instead, chose to refrain from rendering a 
diagnosis. Would her decision —based on a desire to prevent causing her client 
distress —represent an ethical approach? Why, or why not?

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 7/9)
content:
Your cases!
(Isaac, Vijay, Emmanuel, 
Martina
Lorenzo, Hope, Marcie

MALPRACTICE AND 
RESOLVING LEGAL 
AND ETHICAL 
CHALLENGES

Malpractice (1 of 3)
•Malpractice  involves professional misconduct or 
unreasonable lack of skill.
•Has been defined as the failure to render professional 
services to the level expected in a particular 
community by a prudent member of the profession 
with the result that a recipient of services is harmed.
Pearson, 2025

Malpractice
•In order for a client plaintiff to prevail in a malpractice 
lawsuit against a counselor, the plaintiff must prove the 
following elements:
•The counselor had a duty to the client to use 
reasonable care in providing counseling services
•The counselor failed to conform to the required duty of 
care
•The client was injured
•There was a reasonably close causal connection 
between the conduct of the counselor and the 
resulting injury (known as proximate  cause )
•The client suffered an actual loss or was damaged
Pearson, 2025

Most common reasons for malpractice lawsuits
•Mental health professionals are sued most often because 
of sexual relationships with their clients. 
•One of the biggest payou ts to individuals who sued counselors 
have to do with counselors engaging in sexual relationships with 
clients.
•According to WA state law a counselor may never engage or 
attempt to engage in a sexual relationship with a client OR former 
client (see WAC 246-16- 100 ). This is MORE restrictive than the 
ACA code of ethics
•The next leading cause of malpractice lawsuits against 
counselors revolves around situations in which clients 
attempt or complete suicide.
Pearson, 2025

•respond fully and in writing to each of the 
charges made.
•consult with an attorney.
•submit as much documentation as you can that 
will help the ethics committee in its 
deliberations.
•contact your professional liability insurance 
carrier  
Do not contact the client who filed the complaint to see if you can 
work things out.If a formal 
ethics 
complaint is 
filed against 
you, you 
should:

•In the video, the prosecutor calls the counselor’s 
competency into question. How does the counselor’s 
referencing of his clinical records strengthen the 
credibility and clinical justifications for discontinuing 
counseling with the client who is suing him?
•The counselor indicates that he sought consultation for 
his client’s issues on two separate occasions. Do the 
counselor’s efforts to gain outside perspectives and 
insight from other experienced clinicians support the 
client’s accusation of malpractice? Why, or why not?video 8.1

When a client threatens to commit 
suicide, an ethical duty arises to protect 
the client from harm to self.
Pearson, 2025

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 8/9)
content:
Clients at Risk for Suicide
•Confidentiality requirements are waived when disclosure is 
necessary to protect clients or others from serious and 
foreseeable harm  (A C A, 2014, Code of Ethics , §B.2.a.). 
•The counselor’s first responsibility is to determine that a 
particular client may be in danger of attempting suicide. 
•Evaluating and managing suicide risk is one of the 
most stressful situations that you will encounter in your 
work.
•Counselors should focus on assessing client protective 
factors against suicide while also assessing client risk 
factors for suicide. 
Pearson, 2025

Clients at Risk for Suicide
•It is necessary for all counselors to know the warning signs 
that a particular person is at risk for suicide. 
•Counselors have a legal duty to evaluate accurately a 
client’s potential for suicide.
•Counselors can be held liable for overreacting and for 
underreacting. 
•However, courts have generally been reluctant to hold 
counselors accountable for harm that results from 
clients who attempt or complete suicide.
•Some counselors require clients at risk for suicide 
submit to an evaluation by a mental health 
professional who has an expertise in suicide as a 
condition for continuing to provide counseling. 
Pearson, 2025

Making Assessments for Suicide Risk
•If you work in an agency, school, hospital, or other 
organizational setting, you must always follow procedures 
established by your employer. 
•If your employer does not have guidelines for how to 
manage potentially suicidal clients, you should ask that 
guidelines be established for the welfare of clients and to 
protect the counselors who have to make decisions in 
these difficult situations. 
Pearson, 2025

Non-suicidal self injury
•In recent years, the professional counseling literature has 
introduced considerable discussion regarding nonsuicidal  
self-injury 
•such behaviors involve intentional bodily harm that 
cause tissue damage without an individual having the 
intent to die
•The prevailing belief among mental health professionals is 
that self -injury is not automatically a predictor of suicide 
attempts. 
•self-injury and suicidal ideation are two separate 
behaviors and therefore should be assessed separately 
Pearson, 2025

How do you determine if a client is suicidal?
•Do you have a plan?
•Can you tell me specifically what you plan to do?
•Do you have access to the weapons, pills, etc., that you plan to use to end 
your life?
•Have you ever attempted to take your life before?  If yes, what happened

Guidelines for assessing suicidal behavior
•Take direct verbal warnings seriously
•Pay attention to previous suicide attempts
•Identify clients suffering from depression
•Be alert for feelings of hopelessness and helplessness (giving up prized belongings)
•Monitor severe anxiety and panic attacks
•Determine whether individual has a plan
•Identify clients who have a history of severe alcohol or drug abuse
•Be alert to client behaviors (e.g. giving prized possessions away, finalizing business affairs, or revising 
wills)
•Determine history of psychiatric treatment

Clients Who May Be at Risk for Harming Others
•Making this decision as to whether a client may be violent 
in the future is difficult, as there is no scientific certainty 
for such decisions. 
•Standard B.2.a. of the Code of Ethics (A C A, 2014) states 
that the counselor’s confidentiality requirement “does not 
apply when disclosure is required to protect clients or 
identified others from serious and foreseeable harm.” 
•If you do determine that a client may be a danger to 
another person, the law requires that you take whatever 
steps are necessary to prevent the harm and protect the 
foreseeable victim from danger ( Terasoff  case)
Pearson, 2025

Clients Who May Be at Risk for Harming Others
•Choices range from the least intrusive action (obtaining a 
promise from your client not to harm anyone else) to the most 
intrusive (having the client involuntarily committed to a 
psychiatric facility). 
•Numerous possibilities exist between these two extremes, such 
as:
•notifying the client’s family members and getting one of 
them to take responsibility for keeping the client under 
control
•persuading the client to be voluntarily committed to a 
residential facility
•notifying the police 
•calling the client periodically
Pearson, 2025

title: Slides: class 4 competance, 2026 (Part 9/9)
content:
•respond directly to the person making 
the threat, if possible.
•Calm the person down, if possible, and 
listen to the concerns.
•Be careful and not admit to wrongdoing.
•Do not say anything that you would not 
want repeated or would not want to 
repeat under oath at a later time.What 
should you 
do if an 
angry client 
threatens to 
sue you?

The cases of Monica and the case of Javier
1.Review the cases of Monica (p.202) and Jovier  (p.207) in pairs and discuss action steps you 
would take. Then take turns role playing the counselor and client for these two cases.

title: Slides: classes case 3
content:
The case of Isaac:
 
-What are the ethical and legal issues in this case?-Did Dr. Schulz follow proper ethical and legal responsibilities?
-What criteria would you use to determine whether the situation is dangerous enough to warn the potential victim?
-What steps would you suggest Dr. Schulz take?
The case of Vijay.
Vijay, a 16-year-old, is being seen by a counselor, Roshawn, at the request of his parents. Vijay's schoolwork has dropped off, he has become withdrawn socially, and he has expressed to his parents that he has thought of suicide, even though he has not made a specific plan. After the counselor has seen Vijay for several weeks of individual counseling, his concerned parents call and ask how he is doing. They wonder whether they should be alert to possible suicide attempts. Vijay's parents tell Roshawn that they want to respect confidentiality and are not interested in detailed disclosures but that they want to find out if they have cause for worry. Without going into detail, Roshawn reassures them that they really do not need to worry.
      
Did Roshawn have sufficient information to justify telling the parents that they have no need to worry?
Is Roshawn's behavior ethical? Would it make a difference if Vijay were 25 years old?
Does Roshawn have an ethical obligation to inform Vijay of the conversation with his parents?
If the parents were to insist on having more information, does Roshawn have an obligation to say more?
If Vijay were suicidal, what ethical and legal obligations would Roshawn have toward the parents? 
Corey, G., Corey, M., Corey, C., and Callanan, P. (2015). Issues and Ethics in the Helping Profession. Stamford, CT: Cengage Learning. pp. 211-246.
    
 
The case of Emmanuel.
Emmanuel is a middle-aged widower who complains of emptiness in life, loneliness, depression, and a loss of the will to live. He has been in individual therapy for 7 months with a counselor. Using psycho-diagnostic procedures, she has determined that Emmanuel has serious depressive tendencies and is potentially self-destructive. In their sessions he talks about the history of his failures, the isolation he feels, the meaninglessness of his life, and his feelings of worthlessness and depression. With her encouragement he experiments with new ways of behaving in the hope that he will find reasons to go on living. Finally, after 7 months of searching, he decides that he wants to take his own life. He tells his counselor that he is convinced he has been deluding himself in thinking that anything in his life will change for the better and that he feels good about deciding to end his life. He informs her that he will not be seeing her again. The counselor expresses her concern that Emmanuel is very capable of taking his life. She acknowledges that the decision to end his life by suicide is not a sudden one, but she lets him know that she wants him to give therapy more of a chance. He says that he is truly grateful to her for helping him. He says firmly that he does not want her to attempt to obstruct his plans in any way. She asks that he postpone his decision for a week and return to discuss the matter more fully. He tells her he isn't certain whether he will keep this appointment, but he agrees to consider it.
What do you think of the way the counselor dealt with her client?
What would you have done differently if you had been Emmanuel's counselor? How would your viewpoint regarding suicide influence your approach with Emmanuel?
What personal values or issues of your own would be triggered by this case? Which of the following actions might you have pursued?
Committed him to a state hospital for observation, even against his will, for 72 hours
Consulted with another professional as soon as he began to discuss suicide as an option
Respected his choice of suicide, even if you did not agree with it 
Informed the police and reported the seriousness of his threat.
Corey, G., Corey, M., Corey, C., and Callanan, P. (2015). Issues and Ethics in the Helping Profession. Stamford, CT: Cengage Learning. pp. 211-246.
  
   
The case of Martina.
Martina, a counselor, has reason to believe that her 14-year-old client is being physically abused. As part of the abuse, critical medication is being withheld. Martina reports the incident to Child Protective Services and gives all the information she has to the caseworker. She follows up the phone conversation with the caseworker with a written report. A week later, the client tells her that nothing has been done. 
Has Martina adequately fulfilled her responsibility by making the report? Does she have a responsibility to report the agency for not having taken action?
If the agency does not take appropriate action, does Martina have a responsibility to take other measures? Are there opportunities to empower the client to get the medication she needs?
From whom would you seek consultation? What referrals would be important to consider?
• Would it be ethical for Martina to take matters into her own hands and to call for a family session or make a house call, especially if the client requests it?
      Corey, G., Corey, M., Corey, C., and Callanan, P. (2015). Issues and Ethics in the Helping Profession. Stamford, CT: Cengage Learning. p. 211-246.
The case of Lorenzo.
Lorenzo, 16 years old, is sent to a family guidance clinic by his parents. During the first session the counselor sees Lorenzo and his parents together. She tells the parents
in his presence that what she and Lorenzo discuss will be confidential and that she
will not disclose information acquired through the sessions without his permission.
The parents seem to understand that confidentiality is necessary for trust to develop between their son and his counselor.
At first Lorenzo is reluctant to come in for counseling. Eventually, as the sessions go on, he discloses that he has a serious drug problem. Lorenzo's parents know that he was using drugs at one time, but he has told them that he is no longer using them. The counselor listens to anecdote after anecdote about Lorenzo's use of illegal drugs, about how "I am loaded at school every day," and about a few brushes with death when he was under the influence of illegal substances. Finally, she tells Lorenzo that she does not want the responsibility of knowing he is experimenting with illegal drugs and that she will not agree to continue the counseling relationship unless he stops using them. At this stage she agrees not to inform his parents, on the condition that he quits using drugs.
What is your general impression of the way Lorenzo's counselor handled the case?
Do you think the counselor acted in a responsible way toward the client? the parents?
If you were convinced that Lorenzo was likely to hurt himself or others because of his drug use and his emotional instability, would you have informed his parents, even at the risk of losing Lorenzo as a client? Why or why not?
 
Corey, G., Corey, M., Corey, C., and Callanan, P. (2015). Issues and Ethics in the Helping Profession. Stamford, CT: engage Learning. pp. 211-246.
Case of Hope by Chris C. Lauer in Herlihy & Corey, 2015
Hope, a 28-year-old single African American woman, attends weekly therapy sessions in New Orleans. Hope is HIV positive, in good health, and compliant with her medications for HIV and depression. Hope has a 4-year-old daughter named Angela.
Hope proactively sought an HIV test 4 years ago in response to a public service campaign. Shortly after she learned of her status, she sought medical help. Six months ago, during a medical appointment with her doctor, Hope appeared anxious and possibly depressed. At the recommendation of her doctor, Hope agreed to speak with one of the on-staff psychiatrists.
The psychiatrist administered the Beck Depression Inventory (Beck, Steer, & Brown, 1996) and the Beck Anxiety Inventory (Beck & Steer, 1990); Hope's scores indicated both moderate depression and moderate anxiety. After discussing these results with the psychiatrist, Hope decided that she would begin taking an antidepressant and would see a counselor. The psychiatrist prescribed an inexpensive antidepressant and asked to see her in 3 weeks.
Presently, Hope is seeing Patrick, a counselor at NATF. She has been attending counseling weekly for 5 months. Her presenting issue was that she feels entrapped by her circumstances. Hope lives in a newer subsidized housing development. She reports that she does not feel connected to the neighborhood and its residents because her family, with the exception of her aunt, chose to remain in Chicago after Hurricane Katrina. Before the storm, she socialized with her neighbors, but Hidden Oaks' covenants do not allow people to congregate on their front porches. Hope feels torn, unable to figure out how to care for her daughter, maintain her $10 per hour job at a small hotel, and manage her health. Hope wants more but sees little opportunity to improve her life given her income, lack of a high school diploma, HIV status, and being a single mom.
Hope loves her daughter, Angela, and appears to cater to her physical and emotional needs as best she can. Hope also likes to have fun and has reported “living it up,” drinking and smoking pot, on occasion, at her local bar. Today is the third time in 5 months that Hope has brought Angela to her counseling session because neither her aunt nor a neighbor could sit with Angela. During the session Angela begins to whine. Hope stands up, walks over to Angela, and slaps her. The slap isn't overly hard, but neither is it soft. Angela begins to cry.
Questions
Identify the ethical concerns in this case. Does the slap constitute child abuse?
Which steps would you take to ensure you are responding ethically to each of these identified concerns. 
Is disclosure required at this point? If disclosure becomes necessary what are the potential consequences to the counseling relationship? What are the potential consequences for Hope and Angela?
How might culture and life circumstances come into play in this situation? 
Case of Nicole, by Amanda Crawford in Herlihy & Corey, (2015)
Nicole works as community treatment counselor. This program provides mental health services in rural communities. Nicole, the counselor, works with children who have been identified as suffering from a serious emotional disturbance. This program functions similarly to an outpatient facility, yet it is housed in the school.
Nicole began working with a 12 year old, Lauren, and her aunt, Maria. At the initial consultation with Maria, with whom Lauren lives full time, Nicole was informed of Lauren's recent risky behavior both at home and in school. Lauren was struggling in school and at home as evidenced by loss of temper, arguments with adults, compulsive lying, withdrawal from her adult relationships, missing curfew, blaming, anger, and spiteful behavior. 
Nicole met regularly with Lauren in individual, group, and family therapy sessions for 8 weeks. During this time rapport was built between Lauren and Nicole. Lauren disclosed information about relationship struggles, feelings underlying her recent withdrawal from adult relationships, and her current struggles with her sexual orientation. During an individual therapy session 8 weeks after Lauren began counseling, Lauren asked for clarification of confidentiality and then shared that she is sexually attracted to females and is in an experimental relationship with an older 14-year-old girl.
Soon after Lauren's disclosure of her sexual orientation and discussion about her sexual experimenting, Maria came to the office for a treatment plan update meeting. Maria wanted to know how to best support her niece. She is open to being more involved with sessions and would like to be a part of the treatment plan. Lauren has expressed a desire to have a space where she can discuss feelings and where her revelations will not be shared with her aunt.
Identify the ethical and legal issues in this case. What steps would you take?
Would you advise Lauren to disclose anything to her aunt? Would you involve the Aunt in any way in treatment? What are the potential consequences to the counseling relationship? What are the potential consequences for Lauren and Maria?
How would you respond to Maria?
The case of Marcie. (Pearson Education, Inc, 2025)
Marcie is a new client, a 22-year-old female who works as a receptionist in a doctor’s office.
She has just revealed to you, during the intake session, that she has recently lost interest in most activities, has been experiencing fatigue despite sleeping a great deal, and sometimes wishes she could cease to exist. She mentioned feeling as though she were “on an emotional roller coaster” during the past year, throughout her on-again, off-again relationship with a 35-year-old married man. The last breakup with him seemed really final, and Marcie has felt herself sinking deeper and deeper ever since. When probed further about suicidal ideations, Marcie admitted that she has considered killing herself, although she is uncertain whether or not she would actually do it. She said that she is currently in possession of a gun that her friend allowed her to keep in her home, following a rash of burglaries in the neighborhood, but she doesn’t know whether she would actually use it. You have consulted with your supervisor, who has agreed that Marcie should be referred immediately for a psychiatric evaluation and has instructed you to arrange for Marcie to go directly from your office to a nearby hospital. Marcie told you that her mother accompanied her to this appointment and is in the waiting room, but she has emphatically stated that she does not want her mother to know what is going on with her. 
-What are the ethical and legal responsibilities in this case?
-How should this sensitive situation be handled in light of Marcie’s wishes regarding her mother?
-What steps would you take?

C L A S S  2
P R O F E S S I O N A L  
C O M M U N I C A T I O N•Write a template for a letter to a clinical training site 
where you are interested in completing your 
practicum and internship. Demonstrate 
professionalism and a clear counselor identity in your 
communication. Limit the letter to three paragraphs. 
You may choose between:
•Writing a letter to a site where you would like to apply, 
expressing interest in the site, your time frame (one 
year of clinical training from April -April), a bit about 
yourself, your availability and how they can contact 
you.
•Writing a thank -you letter after an interview (what you 
appreciated about the site and why you would be 
good fit).

C L A S S  3
I N F O R M E D  
C O N S E N T•Develop a personalized informed consent document applicable for use in 
clinical training. Bring rough drafts of your document to class #3 (due 1/31). At 
a minimum, please include the following:
•Purposes, goals, techniques, procedures, limitations, and potential risks and 
benefits of services
•Your qualifications (counseling student under supervision with a licensed 
professional)
•Information about termination and referrals
•Emergency protocols and crisis resources
•The role of technology
•The implications of the diagnosis and intended use of testing and reports
•Fees and billing information
•Confidentiality and its limitations
•The right to obtain records and actively participate in ongoing counseling plans
•The right to refuse recommended services or modality change and be advised 
of the consequences of refusal
Site it if you use a template or an example, examples in your text (Appendix B)

C L A S S  6
P R I N C I P L E S  
P A P E RCode of Ethics Principle Paper (due March 14) –140 points
Choose one of the six ethical principles outlined in the American Counseling 
Association’s (ACA) code of ethics (autonomy, nonmaleficence, beneficence, justice, 
fidelity or veracity) and research the principle in depth in three ways. The code of 
ethics should follow APA style formatting and be 6 -8 pages in length.
1. Explore the identified ethical principle within the counseling field. 
 Why 
is the ethical principle foundational to counseling? Consider the history of this 
principle within the field (look for evidence in counseling theory, counselor 
identity and/or development of counseling). Offer two references. 
 Identify other 
mental health ethical codes (iePsychology, MFT, Social Work, Medicine ) that 
consider this principle. Offer two references. 
 What does current counseling 
literature say about this principle? Consider work with diverse populations. Offer 
at least one reference.
2. Explain how ACA standards reflect the principle you researched. 
Identify three standards from the ACA code of ethics and explain how the 
principle you have researched is reflected in each of the standards. Put the 
standards in your own words and consider how you will put them to practice in 
your work. • Consider at least 2 subpoints in each identified standard 
 Each 
student must include a standard from section H: Distance Counseling, 
Technology and Social Media
3. Explore the ethical principle from a Christian perspective. 
 Which 
Christian virtue/virtues relate to the ethical principle? 
 What does Christian 
doctrine teach on this principle? Include at two biblical references. 
 Include one 
example of the virtue within church history or tradition. 
 Identify a personal 
practice that may aid in developing this principle or virtue for a clinician.

C L A S S  7
W R I T T E N  
E X A MIn class, March 26th. A passing score on this exam (80% or above) is required 
(KPI #1)
Each student will be given a case vignette related to legal and ethical 
counseling issues. The student will have two hours to complete a written 
response to the case study where they must: 
1. Identify any legal issues
2. Identify any ethical issues
3. Identify law or ACA ethical codes that address the issues in the case and 
how they are relevant
4. In light of the legal and ethical issues, outline 3 -5 clinical steps (short term 
and long term) they plan to take the client through during their sessions .
5. Discuss what outside agencies/resources should be included or offered to 
the client to address legal and ethical issues in the short -term and long -term.
Students will not be allowed to consult resources or notes when taking 
this exam. This exam is used as a key performance indicator for student 
proficiency in Professional Counseling Orientation and Ethical Practice. 
50 points

B R I E F  
H I S T O RYCounseling Psychology initially recognized individuals with a MA degree 
as professional psychologists. When licensure requirements changed to 
only allow those with a doctoral degree to become licensed, individuals 
with a MA in Psychology sought licenses in counseling. 
School accreditation groups in the 60’s began requiring schools to hire 
counselors.  Almost all states now require that school counselors have a 
master’s degree and have completed courses in internship etc. 
In the 1950’s vocational rehab was growing to serve those with physical 
and mental disabilities
Counseling is established now as a legitimate separate and unique 
profession with its own accreditation process, licensure, associations, 
conferences, journals etc. 
Currently: growth of counseling -in schools, churches, community settings 
etc

O T H E R  
M E N T A L  
H E A L T H  
P R O F E S S I O N SSee pg. 58 -59 for detailed description of the differences 
between mental health providers
Psychiatrist  –MD required; medical physicians trained in 
the medical model. RX ability
Clinical Psychologist -PhD required.  Trained in 
Psychology, medical model. More emphasis in biological 
and social aspects of behavior, assessment, data analysis, 
measurement dx, research and psychopathology . 
Clinical Social Worker - MA required. Focus on diversity, 
human rights, social and economic justice, policy, 
organizations and community health.

F O C U S  O F  
C O U N S E L I N G :
T H E  W E L L N E S S  
M O D E L :
A C C O M P L I S H  
W E L L N E S S ,  
N O T  C U R E  
I L L N E S S•In the illness or medical model, the first thing a professional 
does is diagnose a client’s mental or emotional disorder. Once 
the disorder has been treated successfully, the client is 
discharged until another disorder appears. Then the client 
returns to the professional again for treatment  
•The wellness model of mental health is founded on the 
following beliefs:
oThe best perspective for assisting individuals in resolving 
their emotional and personal issues and problems. 
Assumes Health.
oMost of the issues and problems individuals face in life 
are developmental  in nature and, thus, understanding 
the dynamics of human growth and development is 
essential to success as a helping professional.
oPrevention and early intervention are superior to 
remediation in dealing with personal and emotional 
problems.
oThe goal of counseling is to empower individuals to 
resolve their own problems, independently  of mental 
health professionals, and to teach them to identify and 
resolve problems autonomously in the future.

F O C U S  O F  
C O U N S E L I N G :
A  D E V E L O P M E N T A L  
P E R S P E C T I V E•People’s stories are considered in the context 
of lifespan
•Understanding developmental stages can clue 
us into looking for areas where there is 
stagnation/delay and offer vision for growth
•Before offering a dx a counselor considers 
whether a behavior developmentally aligns with 
the client’s progress (see examples on p. 33).
•With a partner brainstorm examples that you 
might see in practice where a developmental 
perspective would be helpful to consider.

F O C U S  O F  
C O U N S E L I N G :
P R E V E N T I O N  
A N D  E A R L Y  
I N T E R V E N T I O N•Focus and preference for prevention rather than 
remediation.  
•Look for areas where things are going well 
•Early intervention to prevent future challenges
•Seek counseling early 
•Normalize mental health, encouraging families 
and individuals to seek counseling before a 
crisis. 
•Identifying positive traits, practices, strengths

F O C U S  O F  
C O U N S E L I N G :
I N D I V I D U A L
E M P O W E R M E N T•Empower clients to solve their problems 
independently
•Because our field focuses so much on 
cultural competency it is essential for each 
client to identify and utilize the cultural 
framework and values that are important to 
them in order to solve their problems. 
•The primary message to clients is “you are 
capable of developing the skills you need for 
independent living and wellness” (p. 34)

D E F I N I N G  C O U N S E L I N G ?
•Defining counseling as a profession that is similar to another profession, such as psychology or 
social work, is not helpful in educating the public about the profession of counseling…or in helping 
counselors establish our unique professional identity.
•It would be best to explain that you are earning a master’s degree in counseling, which is a 
professional degree that allows graduates to provide professional counseling and mental health 
services to the public.
•It would also be appropriate to explain the o rientation of the profession focuses on wellness, 
prevention, early intervention, empowerment, and lifespan development. 
•Terminal degree for licensure is a MASTER’S DEGREE

title: Slides: ethics class 1 (2026) (Part 6/9)
content:
C AC R E P  A C C R E D I TAT I O N
•Helps with licensure portability as many states require (Ohio, Kentucky, NC, 
Florida) or strongly prefer counseling degrees for CACREP accredited programs
•Simplifies applications for licensure
•In WA 50 hours of postgraduate supervision and 500 hours towards postgraduate 
experience for LMHC licensure
•Prep for the NCE exam
•Foundation for doctoral studies
•Increased employability

C U R R E N T  I S S U E S  I N  C O U N S E L I N G
 •Danger of fragmenting into separate groups along the lines of specialties, instead of working toward 
public recognition as one unified profession of counseling.
•Working toward standardization of licensure requirements and school counselor certification 
requirements across the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and possessions of the United States.
•Scope of practice: Ensuring that other mental health professional groups do not interfere with 
counselors diagnosing and treating mental and emotional disorders or limit the testing practices of 
counselors. Advocating for equal representation, compensation, recognition
•Establishing job classifications in state governments and at the federal level for counselors (titles at 
times do not include a counseling title - “social work, psychologist, metal health technician…”) 
•Telehealth -Standards for practice?

M O R E  I S S U E S  T O  C O N S I D E R
•Third party reimbursement
•Increasing salaries for MA clinicians
•Becoming united and societally -recognized as a profession
•Increasing publications of experimental research studies
•Increase availability of counseling services for everyone

D I S C U S S I O NWhat role can students 
play in the standardization 
and professionalization of 
counseling?
What do you believe to be the major 
obstacles facing the counseling 
profession in the 21st century? 
As a future counselor, how do you see 
yourself advocating for the profession?

L I N K S
•https://doh.wa.gov/licenses -permits -and-certificates/professions -new-renew -or-
update/mental -health -counselor/supervisor -directory
•https://counselingcompact.gov/faq/

E T H I C S

D E F I N I T I O N S
Legal standards are the minimum behavior
society will tolerate  from a professional. Legal 
standards are created by society to protect 
citizens from bad professionals.
Ethical standards are the minimum behavior 
other counselors will tolerate from counselors. 
Ethical standards are created by counselors 
themselves to ensure that counselors practice 
in a way that helps clients and does not hurt 
them.
Best practice represents the ideal for 
counselor practice. It may be difficult to get a 
group of counselors to agree upon what best 
practice would be, but all counselors want to 
strive for best practice, not just meeting 
minimum ethical or legal standards of practice

D E F I N I T I O N SPersonal Values -influenced by culture, 
family, community and your beliefs. Unique 
and important but distinct to the individual 
Professional Values - identified values of a 
professional group
Virtue Ethics -character traits of an 
individual
Principle Ethics -agreed upon assumptions 
or beliefs shared by members of a 
professional group

title: Slides: ethics class 1 (2026) (Part 7/9)
content:
P R O F E S S I O N A L  
P R A C T I C E :  
“ B U I L T  F R O M  
W I T H I N  B U T  
B A L A N C E D  
F R O M  O U T S I D E  
O F  T H E  S E L F ”  
( P .  8 )

P e r s o n a l  V a l u e s
•Apply broadly to both the personal and professional 
functioning of counselors
•Serve as a guide to determining what is good or right 
behavior
•Each of us holds some values more strongly than other 
values
•Each value system is unique and is influenced by 
upbringing, culture, and religious beliefs
•In counseling we are asked not to impose our values, 
rather help the client live out their own. It is critical that 
we know and understand our own values and practice 
bracketing (see case of Carole on p. 4).

P R O F E S S I O N A L  VA L U E S
•What comes to mind when you think of values of the following groups/companies:
•US Army
•Red Cross
•Amnesty International
•Culinary arts ( American culinary federation )
•Interior Design
•Landscape 
•Museums

P R O F E S S I O N A L  V A L U E S  O F  C O U N S E L I N G  
( A C A ,  2 0 1 4 )
Enhancing human development across the lifespan Enhancing
Honoring diversity Honoring
Embracing a multicultural approach Embracing
Promoting social justice Promoting
Safeguarding the integrity of the counselor -client relationship Safeguarding
Practicing competently and ethically Practicing

E t h i c s
•Ethics:  a discipline within philosophy that is concerned 
with human conduct and moral decision making.
•Professional ethics refers to conduct judged as good or 
right for counselors as a professional group. 
•Consensus about right behaviors have been codified and 
have become the ethical standards to which counselors 
are expected to adhere in their professional life.
•Thus, ethics should guide one’s professional  behavior and 
interactions.

T h e o r i e s  o f  E t h i c s
•Ethical theories provide a framework that counselors can use to decide 
whether an action or contemplated action is ethical. 
•Ascribing to an ethical theory will enable you to resolve various ethical 
dilemmas you encounter in your work and help you defend the solutions you 
reach. 
•Principle Ethics in a field answer the question “what should I do”

W H Y  E T H I C A L  
T H E O R I E S  
A R E  
I M P O R T A N TEthical theories provide a framework that counselors can use to 
decide whether an action or contemplated action is ethical
Enable clinicians to resolve ethical dilemmas 
The counseling profession has a written code of ethics. The 
code’s guidelines for practice must be read and understood. 
Simply acting as you think is right and moral may not lead to your 
being compliance with the American Counseling Association Code 
of Ethics (or other counseling codes, as well).
Although counselors often have good intentions, if they violate an 
ethical principle or a law, they can get into serious trouble.

T h e o r i e s  o f  e t h i c s  h a v e  e x i s t e d  
f o r  c e n t u r i e s  a n d  p r o v i d e  a  
f o u n d a t i o n  f o r  t h e  e t h i c a l  
r e a s o n i n g  o f  c o n t e m p o r a r y  
p r o f e s s i o n a l  c o u n s e l o r s .

2 0 1 4 )
autonomy nonmaleficence beneficence
justice fidelity veracity

R E V I E W  C O U N S E L I N G ’ S  P R I N C I P L E

principles come into play?

relationship

W h y  a  C o d e  o f  E t h i c s ?
•Educate members of the profession as well as consumers about 
what constitutes ethical practice
•Help to ensure accountability through enforcement of the 
standards
•Protect the profession from government by allowing the 
profession to regulate itself and function more autonomously
•Promote stability within the profession by establishing agreed -
upon guidelines for practice
•Serve as a catalyst for ongoing improvement of practice

E X A M P L E S  O F  E T H I C A L  C O D E S
 
•ACA: Ethical Standards

Professionals•Evaluation, Assessment and 
Interpretation

Social Media

P r i n c i p l e s  E t h i c s  v s  V i r t u e  E t h i c s
•Principle ethics  refer to the professionally agreed upon 
obligations that are always considered in ethical decision 
making.
•Principle ethics ask: “What should I do?” 
•Virtue ethics  assume that professional ethics involve more 
than moral actions; they also involve traits of character or virtue .  
•Virtue ethics ask: “Who should I be?”

V I R T U E  E T H I C S
•Beyond moral actions, virtue ethics looks at character, virtue ethics asks “Who 
should I be”, not just as a clinician but as a person.  The focus is on YOU as a 
person, not a particular action or decision.
A.https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kq -rOelLciE
•Virtue ethics asks us to consider virtue OVER what emotion dictates, what is 
financially beneficial, the easiest solution. 
•People have different strengths of character and should aim to express the 
virtues most important to them

title: Slides: ethics class 1 (2026) (Part 9/9)
content:
L e g a l  I s s u e s
•Most areas of counselor practice are affected by law
•Counselors must know how to recognize legal issues and how to 
obtain legal advice
•Counselors should: 
•seek advice from fellow mental health professionals when 
they have clinical or ethical questions 
•seek advice from lawyers when they have legal questions
•Following laws typically represents minimum requirement

Y O U  A R E  N O T  
A L O N E !  
S U P P O R T  
S T R U C T U R E S  
O F F E R  
G U I DA N C E  A N D  
S U P P O R T
Consultation
 Supervision
 Continuing 
professional 
development
Laws
 Code of Ethics
 System policies

H O W  S H O U L D  Y O U  G E T  A D V I C E  W H E N  A  
L E G A L  I S S U E  P R E S E N T S  I T S E L F ?
Generally, counselors should ask their immediate supervisor to obtain legal advice for them. The 
supervisor then obtains the advice and reports it to the counselor.
The ACA professional liability insurance company provides a legal hot line  that insured counselors can 
call and talk to an attorney free of charge.  
Counselors employed in an agency do not need to pay for legal advice themselves.  The only exception 
would be if the agency is planning to take adverse action against the counselor because of the legal 
problem. Under those circumstances, a counselor should hire his or her own lawyer.

S T E P S  I N  
M A K I N G  
E T H I C A L  
D E C I S I O N S•A general eight -step process for ethical decision making. Keep in 
mind cultural context  and your emotions . 
1. Identify the problem or dilemma -Is it a legal issue or a 
clinical issue? Take time to review the situation carefully.  
(example -termination/leave p. 23)
2. Involve your client in the decision -making process -
Throughout the process, involve your client, this empowers the 
client and builds the relationship with your client (part of 
safeguarding the integrity of the counselor. (example p. 9)
3. Review the relevant ethics codes -Review codes, laws and 
literature
4. Consider the principles and virtues . Consider not just what 
to DO but who you want to BE in the situation

S T E P S  
C O N T I N U E D5. Tune into your feelings -consider the emotions you are 
experiencing.  Do you have fear/doubt? Do you feel you are 
being influenced by others in a direction you don’t agree 
with?  Be aware of emotions, beliefs, values and motivations 
at play. It is important that your body is relaxed and your 
mind is engaged throughout this process. Tend to your reaction 
and take steps to relax and engage. 
6. CONSULT with colleagues or experts -Emotions can 
interfere. Obtain consultation. This is important from a legal 
standpoint as well. 
7. Consider the context -Be culturally competent. What 
ramifications might there be for the client’s family, community, 
other professionals etc.
8. Make a decision and act on your choice -Don’t linger in 
indecision. Have courage to come to a decision and act it out.

A C T I V I T Y
•Apply the ethical decision model on pages 12 -13 to the scenarios in the text. At 
this point focus particularly on considering the 6 -principle ethics in counseling, 
which of the 6 principle ethics come into play in making a decision?
•case 1 -1, p. 4
•case 1 -2, p. 6
•case 1 -3, p. 11
•case 1 -4, p. 13
•case 1 -5., p. 25

D I S C U S S I O NAssuming that you are a moral and 
responsible person (as are most counselors), 
why do you think it is important for you to 
study ethical and legal principles and the 
decision -making process?

Office Hours:    
By appointment only.  P lease contact me to schedule an appointment.

(7th edition). Pearson. ISBN -13: 978013 8168094

Course description:   
In this course, students will understand the history, philosophy, and practice of counseling.  
Specific attention is given to  legal and ethical standards for licensed counselors.   
         
Course Objectives   
1. Explain the legal and ethical standards in the practice of counseling.  
2. Articulate professional identity and responsibilities of a counselor.

Academic Honesty:  
Northwest University expects honesty from students in all areas, including their academic lives. 
Academic dishonesty is a serious violation of university standards and may result in receiving an 
“F” on the assignment, receiving an “F” for the course, or being  dismissed from the University. 
Instances of academic dishonesty are coordinated through the respective graduate program office 
and typically reported to the Provost’s Office.  
Academic dishonesty includes cheating on assignments or examinations, submitting the same (or 
essentially the same) paper in more than one course without prior consent of the current 
assigning professor(s), sabotaging another student’s work, and plagiarizi ng. Plagiarism is “using 
someone else’s ideas or phrasing and representing those ideas or phrasing as our own, either on 
purpose or through carelessness” (MLA).   Any student disciplined by a faculty member for 
alleged academic dishonesty has the right to a ppeal the disciplinary action. The student should 
initiate his/her appeal according to procedures outlined in the Northwest University Student 
Academic Appeals Policy, which is available in the provost's  office.  
 
Attendance Policy:    
Class attendance is extremely important for student learning and achievement of specified course 
outcomes,  and, in turn, the program aims. Hence, attendance at all class sessions is expected. At 
minimum, students absent for a class session will need to make up any missed assignments or 
exercises and complete the expected readings.  A grade penalty will be appl ied if more than one 
class session is missed. Additional policies and consequences are at the discretion of the 
individual professor of the course. Mo re than one missed class session will result in failure of the 
course and require a retake to continue in the program. In this circumstance, the student is 
encouraged to withdraw from the course and retake the class when it is offered again. In the 
event o f unusual circumstances (e.g., extended illness, emergency), a student may appeal the 
automatic course failure. A proposal outlining a plan for satisfactory course completion will be 
required, composed by the professor,  and agreed to by the student. In add ition, the plan will 
require approval by the program director and Dean.  
 
Disability Accommodations and Procedures:  
For students with learning differences and/or physical disabilities, as defined by the ADA and 
Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, Northwest University takes an individual and holistic 
approach to providing accommodations.  Accommodations are developed a nd approved on an 
individual basis by the Student Development Office, not the CMHC program or individual 
faculty members. Students needing accommodations for a diagnosed disability should follow the 
procedures provided on the Student Development Office’s website for disability 
accommodations.

title: syllabus COUN 5553 Professional Orientation  Law and Ethics - 2026 (2) (Part 2/3)
content:
.  
 

============================================================
PAGE 4
============================================================
  4 
Communication Policy:  
Timely communication between the professor and students is important to the program's overall 
functioning.  Therefore, both students and faculty should respond to email or phone messages 
within two business days. If the professor is out -of-office, she will have an automatic reply 
message (on email) providing the return date and an in -office contact for urgent requests.  
 
Grading Policy:  
Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to 
monitor their progress accordingly. Assignments and tests will generally be graded within two 
weeks. Final papers and projects may take up to three weeks. The pro fessor will let you know if 
there are exceptional circumstances preventing a timely return of grades. If you have concerns 
about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program Chair.  
 
Late Work Policy:  
It is the student’s responsibility to meet all deadlines for class assignments. Late work will be 
accepted one week after the due date with a 10% grade penalty. It is your responsibility to 
communicate with the professor promptly about late work and absences.  
 
Mechanics of the Course:  
 
A) Class Sessions : 
Class sessions will be used for a variety of purposes including:   
1. Lecture  
2. In Class Discussion s 
3. Small Group Exercises  
4. Quizzes  
5. Written  Exam  
 
B) Quizzes  and Tests:  
1. There will be two multiple -choice and/or True -False examinations  in class .  
 
• Quiz  1: Fifth class session  (during regular class time) Covered topics will be lectures 
and chapters 1 -8. 
 
• Quiz  2: Final class session  (during regular class time) Covered topics will be lectures 
and chapters 9-16. Quizzes must be completed by the last day of the semester.  
 
2. Law and Ethics Written Case Study Exam  (in class, March 26)  
 
Each student will be given a case vignette related to legal and ethical counseling issues. The 
student will have two hours to complete a written response to the case study where they must: 1. 
Identify any legal issues, 2. Identify any ethical issues . 3. Identify law or ACA ethical codes that 
address the issues in the case and how they are relevant, 4. In light of the legal and ethical issues, 
outline 3 -5 clinical steps (short term and long term) they plan to take the client through during 
their session s, and 5. Discuss what outside agencies/resources should be included or offered to 
the client to address legal and ethical issues in the short -term and long -term. The law and ethics 

============================================================
PAGE 5
============================================================
  5 
qualifying exam will be graded on the legal and ethical issues identified, and the short and long -
term steps taken to help the client in the case. Students will not be allowed to consult resources 
or notes when taking this exam. This exam is used as a key performance indicator for student 
proficiency in Professional Counseling Orientation and Ethical Practice.  This exam must 
be passed with an 80% or above to pass the class.  
 
 
C) Response, Research, or Other Written Assignments : 
 
1. There will be three professional development tasks (see Discovery for instructions):  
• Professional communication with clinical placement sites (due Jan 24)  
• Online Telehealth Training (due Feb 14)  
• Online Mandatory Reporting Training (due Feb 14)  
 
2. Personalized inform consent document  (due Jan 31 ): develop a personalized informed 
consent document (see examples in your text, Appendix B) that will be applicable for you 
to use in clinical training  at your site . Cite any templates or sources used to create your 
personalized document. Bring rough drafts of your document to class #3. In small groups , 
you will discuss possible additions or deletions to the documents. Final drafts will be due 
the Saturday after class #3. At a minimum, p lease include the following  (50 points, 5 
points fo r addressing each area below ):   
• Purposes, goals, techniques, procedures, limitations, and potential risks and 
benefits of services  
• Your  qualifications (counseling student and under supervision with a licensed 
professional)  
• Information about termination and referrals  
• Emergency protocols and crisis resources  
• The role of technology  
• The implications of diagnosis and intended use of testing and reports  
• Fees and billing information  
• Confidentiality and its limitations  
• The right to obtain records and actively participate in ongoing counseling plans  
•  The right to refuse recommended services or modality change and be advised of 
the consequences of refusal . 
 
3.   Code of Ethics Principle  Paper (due March 14)– Choose one of the six ethical 
 principles outlined in the American Counseling Association’s ( ACA ) code of 
ethics (autonomy, nonmaleficence, beneficence, justice, fidelity or veracity) and research 
the principle in depth in three ways. The code of ethics should follow APA style 
formatting and be 6 -8 pages in length.  
 
1. Explore the identified ethical principle within the counseling field.  
▪ Why is the ethical principle foundational for counseling? Consider the 
history of this principle within the field (look for evidence in counseling 
theory, counselor identity  and/or development of counseling ). Offer two 
references.  

============================================================
PAGE 6
============================================================
  6 
▪ Identify other mental health ethical codes that consider this principle . 
Offer two references.  
▪ What does current counseling literature say about  this principle ? Consider 
work with diverse populations.  Offer at least one reference . 
2. Explain how ACA standards  reflect the principle you researched .  
▪ Identify three standards from the ACA code of ethics and explain how the 
principle you have researched is reflected in each of the standards . Put the 
standard s in your own words and consider how you will put them to 
practice in your work.  
• Consider at least 2 subpoints in each identified standard  
▪ Each student must include a standard from section H: Distance 
Counseling, Technology and Social Media .  
3. Explore the ethical principle from a Christian perspective.  
▪ Which Christian virtue/virtues relate to the ethical principle?  
▪ What does Christian doctrine teach on this principle? Include at two 
biblical references.  
▪ Include one example of the virtue within church history or tradition.  
▪ Identify a personal practice that may aid in developing this principle or 
virtue  for a clinician . 
 
 
D) Participation:  
A portion of the  student's final grade will be based on their participation in class  
including  group discussions, class exercises , individual and group presentation s of discussion 
material , and attendance. Full points will be rewarded to those students who attend all the class 
sessions and actively participate in class activities.  
 
E) Grading : 
Exams and papers  will b
