Counselors who counsel clients at risk for harm to self or others are well advised to arrange 
for those clients to also be under the care of other professionals who have accepted responsibility for the well-being of the clients. Counselors should work to ensure that all clients are able to tolerate counselor absences for reasonable periods of time.
TERMINATION  Termination is an important stage in the therapeutic relationship that sometimes 
is given inadequate consideration. Termination is an intentional process that occurs over time when a client has achieved most of the treatment goals, and it provides opportunities for consolidating, making plans for maintaining, and generalizing client gains made in counseling. Effective termina-tions model how healthy relationships should end and provide clients with opportunities to work through their feelings about ending their therapeutic journeys.
M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   92M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   92 09/11/23   8:34 PM09/11/23   8:34 PM

Chapter 4 • Client Welfare and Informed Consent 93
Proper termination of the counseling relationship conveys caring and helps to prevent harm, which 
are important ethical considerations (Corey et al., 2024; Vasquez et al., 2008). Clients should be able 
to expect that their counseling sessions will end when they have received what they were seeking from counseling or when they have realized the maximum benefit from the counseling services. Counselors should always anticipate termination as a phase or stage in the counseling relationship, and they should provide pre-termination counseling or recommend other service providers when necessary (ACA, 2014, §A.11.c.). In addition, counselors should raise and discuss the issue of termination with the client well in advance of the final session. Doing so allows ample time to plan for the client’s transition to functioning without the counselor and to dealing with the natural and appropriate issues of separation and loss. It is best if the counselor and client mutually agree on the timing of the termination. However, depending on the circumstances, the ultimate decision might be made independently by either the counselor or the client.
PREMATURE TERMINATION  Not all counseling relationships end smoothly and by mutual agree-
ment. In reality, circumstances can arise that allow the counselor to terminate the counseling relationship even against a client’s wishes. According to the ACA Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014, §A.11.c.), it is ethi-cally permissible for counselors to terminate counseling relationships in the  following circumstances:
• It is apparent that the client no longer needs assistance, is not likely to benefit, or is being harmed by continued counseling.
• Clients do not pay the fees as agreed upon.• The counselor is in jeopardy of being harmed by the client or someone with whom the client has a relationship.
When counselors are unable to be of continued assistance to clients or decide to end the relation -
ship over the client’s objection, counselors must give adequate notice and give the client time to secure 
another mental health care provider (Wheeler & Bertram, 2019). In addition, counselors should sug -
gest an appropriate referral and do all they can to facilitate a smooth transition to the new mental health professional. If the client is unable to continue to pay the counselor’s fee, an appropriate referral can be made to free services or to an agency that uses a sliding fee scale, if such alternatives are available. If a client refuses to accept the suggested referral, the counselor is not obligated to provide free services.
Many clients are able to afford counseling services only because they have managed health 
care plans that will reimburse them for a significant portion of the cost. Under managed care plans, people are given only the services that the managed care company deems to be necessary and appro-priate through a process known as utilization review (see Chapter 13 for a detailed discussion of 
health care plans). Counselors face difficult ethical challenges when further treatment is denied by utilization review panels. Counselors must maintain clinical responsibility for the clients’ welfare, and when clients need further counseling, counselors cannot allow financially motivated decisions to supersede their clinical judgment. Counselors should thus appeal, or have their clients appeal, utilization review decisions when they believe clients are in need of continued care.
Sometimes it is the client who terminates the counseling relationship before an agreed-upon 
termination. Swift et al. (2012) have noted that approximately 20% of clients drop out of counsel-ing prematurely. Even when the counselor believes that a client is making progress and that treat-ment goals are being met, clients may call and cancel with or without giving an explanation, fail to keep appointments, or fail to reschedule. The counselor’s ethical obligation in these situations is to attempt to communicate a willingness to continue to meet with the client, either to continue counsel-ing or to summarize and end treatment, or to refer the client to another mental health professional. Wheeler and Bertram (2019) have noted that legal difficulties can occur because of these no-closure terminations, and they advise counselors to document carefully and to send a letter to the client indicating that the client’s apparent decision to terminate has been accepted.
M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   93M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   93 09/11/23   8:34 PM09/11/23   8:34 PM

94 Section 2 • Issues
Both client and counselor characteristics can contribute to the risk of terminating counseling 
services prematurely. Anderson et al. (2019) found that client characteristics of being female and 
identifying as a member of a sexual minority were associated with an increased risk of prema-ture termination and that clients were more likely to terminate services when they perceived their counselor as lacking multicultural competence. There is also some evidence to suggest that when counselors are ethnically or linguistically different from their clients, clients are more likely to ter -
minate prematurely (Maramba & Nagayama Hall, 2002). These findings underscore the importance of counselor sensitivity to the experiences of disempowered members of society and of developing and maintaining multicultural competence, as discussed in Chapter 3.
Preventing Abandonment
Abandonment can be considered a form of inappropriate termination and can occur when the cli-ent’s counseling needs are not being adequately addressed by the counselor, either when treatment ends or during the course of treatment due to counselor unavailability. Ethical guidelines in the ACA Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014, §A.12) and legal decisions related to physicians (Allison v. Patel, 1993; Manno v. McIntosh,1994) prohibit counselors from abandoning their clients. Once a counseling relationship has been established, the counselor cannot arbitrarily end it if the client will be put at risk as a result. Terminating during a client crisis or emergency could put the client at risk and could result in ethical and legal difficulties for the counselor (Wheeler & Bertram, 2019).
As noted, advance planning can help avoid the risk that clients will feel abandoned by their 
counselors. Counselors should take care to give clients ample advance notice when planned inter -
ruptions are anticipated, such as the counselor’s taking maternity or paternity leave, undergoing a surgical procedure, or going on vacation.
The key to ending a professional relationship with a client properly, and thus not being exposed 
to charges of abandonment, is to give the client adequate notice that you are ending the professional relationship and ample opportunity to locate a new counselor if needed. It might be important, too, to discuss with the client how to locate agencies and individuals who offer counseling services. Giving notice of termination and time for the client to find a new counselor may not, however, lead to the client taking the initiative to find a new counselor. If a client refuses to see another counselor after you have given notice that you are withdrawing your services, it will be essential for you to document carefully and fully the notice you gave, your assistance with the referral process, and the time you gave the client to locate a new professional.
Hilliard (1998), an attorney who represents mental health professionals who are sued by their 
clients, has observed the events that lead individuals to sue their mental health providers. As a prac-tical consideration, Hilliard has recommended that mental health care providers never terminate a professional relationship over a client’s objection when the client is angry or when the client is in a serious crisis situation. Taking the time to address a client’s distress is very important to prevent unre -
solved angry feelings that could lead to a lawsuit. Also, a judge or jury probably would not support a mental health professional’s decision to end a relationship in the midst of a crisis (Allison v. Patel, 1993; Manno v. McIntosh, 1994). Ending a counseling relationship because insurance benefits have 
run out, even as the client is at risk for suicide, is very risky (Packman & Harris, 1998) for both the client and the counselor.
Of course, counselors may always refer a client to a psychiatrist or a health care facility that 
evaluates individuals for suicide risk or danger to themselves or others. This referral does not always lead to a transfer of that client to another professional or termination. In many circumstances, coun-selors continue to provide services to a referred client in collaboration with another mental health professional. When such a referral is made, however, it is very important that the counselor inform

Chapter 4 • Client Welfare and Informed Consent 95
all parties whether the referral is a transfer of the client to another professional or the counselor will 
be providing services in conjunction with the other professional.
To protect mental health professionals from being held accountable for abandonment of their 
clients, Hilliard (1998) and Macbeth et al. (1994) developed the following guidelines for mental health professionals to use when they are terminating their relationships with clients in adverse circumstances:
• Honestly discuss with the client your intention to terminate and the specific reason for your decision.
• Give the client an opportunity to make changes that would be necessary to continue the relationship.
• Give written notice to the client that the relationship will be terminated.• Offer the client one to three termination sessions.• Give your client up to three good referrals.• Give your client places to contact in the event of emergencies.• Place in the client’s file a summary of your interactions with the client that are related to the termination issue. Do not transfer that document to another individual or entity without an express written request from the client.
• Give the client plenty of time to find another mental health professional. If more time is requested, allow it.
• Transfer records to the new mental health professional promptly.
 4-2 The Case of Benita
Although Benita has been working with her client Julianna for 3 months, they have actually met 
for only 8 of their 12 scheduled weekly sessions. Twice, Julianna failed to appear for her appoint-ment and later said that she forgot. On two other occasions, Julianna called to cancel a day or two in advance and left a message with no explanation. During the counseling sessions, Julianna has been apologetic about the missed appointments and has been open about her struggles with trying to be more assertive in her personal and work relationships; however, she has not completed any of the assertiveness-training exercises that Benita has suggested as homework. Today, Julianna leaves a message that she doesn’t think counseling is doing her any good and that she doesn’t intend to continue the process.
Benita has to admit to herself that she feels relieved. She sometimes had felt annoyed with 
Julianna for cancelling or not showing up for sessions and for failing to even try the homework assignments. Also, Julianna’s lack of progress had led Benita to question her own effectiveness as a counselor. Benita decides to send Julianna a brief letter acknowledging the termination of their counseling relationship and offering to provide her with a list of referrals if she later decides to resume counseling.
• What do you think about the way Benita has decided to respond to Julianna’s message?
• If you were to advise Benita to self-reflect on her work with Julianna, what might you want her to consider?
Discussion: Benita’s intended letter to Julianna may meet the rudimentary intent of the ethical 
requirements, but it falls short of a response that would reflect best practice. We would advise Benita to attempt to contact Julianna to schedule a termination session in which Benita should encour -
age Julianna to give her honest feedback about the counseling process as she has experienced it.

96 Section 2 • Issues
Benita seems to be blaming herself for Julianna’s lack of progress and to be reacting out of her relief 
that she will no longer need to question her counseling efficacy. If Benita can arrange to meet with Julianna again, even if the client remains steadfast in her decision to terminate their sessions, Benita can convey the message that perhaps Julianna isn’t quite ready to take action toward change but that she is welcome to return at a later time or contact Benita for a referral. Together, they can also review whether other factors may have contributed to the lack of success: Perhaps the goal should have been revisited, or maybe Julianna didn’t feel safe in the relationship. If Julianna isn’t willing to meet again, we would advise Benita to look at some sample letters to see how she might convey in her letter her acceptance of both the decision and the client.
DIVERSITY CONSIDERATIONS  As we have noted, clients sometimes drop out of counseling with-
out discussing with the counselor their intentions for doing so. This can happen for many reasons—
sometimes clients just need to take a break from the intense personal explorations involved in coun-seling, or they may drop out or discontinue the counseling relationship because they do not believe they are benefiting from counseling. Client termination of the relationship will occur in any coun-selor’s practice, and it may be helpful to remember that no counselor is skilled enough to be able to assist every client. Thus, it is important that you self-monitor to discern whether the clients who are discontinuing counseling with you are those who are culturally different from you. If you see such a pattern, this is a signal that you must work to increase your cross-cultural counseling skills.
 INFORMED CONSENT
Informed consent is such a vital and complex component of protecting client welfare that it is discussed as a distinct factor in this chapter. Securing the client’s informed consent is both a legal and an ethical obligation. From an ethical perspective, the rationale for informed consent is simple: Clients have the right to know what they are undertaking when they come for counseling. Most clients are not experts in counseling, so they must trust their counselors to provide them with the information about counsel-ing that they need to make a good decision (Handelsman, 2001). Providing clients with the opportunity to make an informed decision about entering into a counseling relationship demonstrates respect for a client’s autonomy and self-determination and is integral to the formation of an effective therapeutic alli-ance. By communicating the client’s role in making treatment decisions, informed consent can increase the client’s sense of ownership over the process, establish that the counselor and the client are partners working toward common goals, and reduce the client’s anxiety by demystifying the counseling process (Fisher & Oransky, 2008; Margolin et  al., 2005). The more that clients understand how counseling works, the more they are likely to benefit from the counseling process (Corey et al., 2024).
The ACA Code of Ethics spells out in considerable detail the elements that need to be included 
in securing informed consent (ACA, 2014, §A.2.b.). At a minimum, the client must be informed about:
• The purposes, goals, techniques, procedures, limitations, potential risks, and benefits of coun-seling services
• The counselor’s qualifications, including relevant degrees held, licenses and certifications obtained, areas of counseling specialization, counseling experience, and approach to counseling
• Arrangements for continuation of services if the counselor dies or becomes incapacitated• The role of technology• The implications of diagnosis and the intended use of tests and reports
M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   96M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   96 09/11/23   8:34 PM09/11/23   8:34 PM

Chapter 4 • Client Welfare and Informed Consent 97
• Fees and billing information, including procedures for nonpayment of fees
• Confidentiality and its limitations• Clients’ rights to obtain information about their records and to participate in ongoing counseling plans
• Clients’ rights to refuse any recommended services or modality change and to be advised of the consequences of refusal
In addition to the elements addressed by the Code, we recommend that counselors begin the 
informed consent process with new clients or prospective clients by offering a brief statement of the 
counselor’s philosophy (how the counselor sees the counseling process taking place). A counselor can offer a simple sentence that begins with “I see counseling as  . . . ” to set the tone for a discussion that clarifies shared expectations of the process. For example, clients will be better able to make a choice about entering counseling if they are aware that they will be asked to do homework between sessions, complete assessments, or try out new behaviors outside of the sessions (Wheeler & Bertram, 2019). Clients also need to be informed about fees. They need to understand the implications of insurance reimbursement, including the fact that any assigned diagnosis will become part of their permanent health record. The No Surprises Act, passed in 2022, requires all providers of medical care, including counselors, to inform clients about fee arrangements, anticipated length of treatment, and potential additional fees. Clients are entitled to know about the codes of ethics to which the counselor sub-scribes and how to obtain a copy of those codes (Wheeler & Bertram, 2019).
The National Board for Certified Counselors (NBCC) has joined with Chi Sigma Iota to pro-
vide a useful booklet that describes clients’ rights and responsibilities (www.nbcc.org/ethics). This booklet incorporates the elements specified in the ACA Code of Ethics (2014), as well as some 
additional client rights, and presents them in language that is clear and suitable for most clients to understand. The booklet also describes the client’s responsibilities in the counseling relationship. Presenting the information in this way helps to establish that counseling is a shared endeavor in which both parties have rights and responsibilities. You may be thinking that a lot of information must be included in the informed consent process, and, indeed, this is true. For clients to be able to give truly informed consent, they must have considerable information before deciding whether to enter a counseling relationship. You may be concerned that inundating prospective clients with information will overwhelm them and give the impression that the counseling relationship is noth-ing more than a complex, contractual business arrangement. In fact, it is difficult for counselors to achieve the delicate balance between giving clients too little information and giving them so much information that they feel overwhelmed or intimidated (Barnett et al., 2007).
Origins of Informed Consent
As a legal concept, the process of informing recipients of professional services and obtaining their con-sent to receive such services is relatively new. The requirement that health professionals obtain informed consent from their clients before rendering services began in medicine (Appelbaum et.al., 1987).
Fifty years ago, it probably rarely occurred to physicians that they should explain to patients 
what the physician knew about the patient’s medical conditions, tell patients about various treat-ment options, or allow patients to decide which option to choose. Perhaps consumers of medical services were once less educated or less aware of their right to receive such information. It has been only in the last half of the twentieth century that courts have created the requirement that patients must be educated or informed regarding their medical treatment options and consequences before they are able to give valid consent to treatment that is legally binding. The first case in the United States that took this position was Salgo v. Leland Stanford Jr. Univ. Bd. of Trustees (1957).
M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   97M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   97 09/11/23   8:34 PM09/11/23   8:34 PM

98 Section 2 • Issues
The Salgo case provided the essential information that physicians must give to patients. According 
to Appelbaum et al. (1987), these elements are “disclosure of the nature of the ailment, the nature of the 
proposed treatment, the probability of success, and possible alternative treatments” (p. 41). Canterbury v. Spence (1972), a later case, took a different position on informed consent: It found that physicians were required to disclose information about a proposed treatment that a reasonable person, such as the patient receiving treatment, would find necessary to decide to either accept or refuse treatment.
After considering the case law on informed consent, Berner (1998) has argued that there are two 
elements to the informed consent legal standard: the professional element and materiality. The profes-sional element is defined as information that a reasonable physician would provide to another patient in similar circumstances. Materiality, on the other hand, is defined as the amount of information that the average patient would consider adequate in deciding whether to accept the treatment. If Berner is correct, then courts will most likely require that physicians provide basic information to all patients and require that physicians ensure that the particular patient with whom they are dealing understands the information.
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), which went into effect 
April 15, 2003, has had a powerful impact on informed consent practices in medicine and mental health. This federal law and its regulations are explored in detail in Chapter 6. Essentially, HIPAA 
requires all health care providers who transmit records electronically (which most likely includes all providers) to comply with procedures to ensure patient privacy. The requirements include explain-ing to health care recipients in detail their rights related to privacy and records (which essentially is the informed consent process discussed earlier). This federal law has thus transformed the concept of informed consent, which once was rather vague, into a concrete framework that includes dis-closure of steps taken to ensure client privacy, and it also requires clients to sign a document stat-ing that they have been informed of their rights. Figure  4-1 includes a list of essential elements of 
informed consent documents to ensure that counselors and agencies meet the HIPAA requirements.
At present, there have been no appellate legal cases involving the responsibility of mental 
health professionals to obtain informed consent from their clients. But like other legal areas in 
FIGURE 4-1 HIPAA requirements for informed consent disclosure statements
To comply with HIPAA, here are some areas you must include on the form that your clients sign stating 
that they have been informed about your proposed treatment and agreeing to the treatment and the arrangement with you:
 ✓State that your client’s personal information may be used and disclosed to complete treatment. Also state that information may be provided to health care companies related to payment for your services.
 ✓Develop a complete written description of the procedures you will follow in your office regarding the keeping of or disclosing personal information of clients.
 ✓Tell your client that you have a more complete description of the way in which you will keep or dis-close their personal information and that this complete description is available for them to see. State that the client has a right to review the complete description before signing this consent form. Explain that your practices may change in the future and that if the client wants to see these revisions, the client must make that request in writing, after which you will make the revisions available.
 ✓Tell your client that they may request that you restrict how their personal information is used or disclosed. Explain that you will consider any such requests and will notify them whether you have agreed to their request.
 ✓Explain that the client has the right to revoke their consent in writing, except to the extent that actions have already been taken by you based on prior consent.
 ✓Get the client’s signature, and have them indicate the date on the form.
 ✓Keep the form for at least 6 years.

Chapter 4 • Client Welfare and Informed Consent 99
mental health, most of the precedents and rules are created first with physicians and later become 
verified for mental health professionals through cases involving psychologists, counselors, and social workers. Nonetheless, it is probably safe to conclude that counselors do have a legal obliga-tion to obtain informed consent from their clients before they begin treatment with them. Further, the informed consent should include information that would be given to the client by other profes-sionals and should be delivered in a way that the client understands. HIPAA also requires that the informed consent of clients be verified with their signatures.
Written Disclosure Statements
In mental health, written contracts for informed consent are commonly referred to as disclosure  
statements. These statements disclose to clients the nature of the counseling relationship into which they are entering. Disclosure statements are also legal contracts that are signed by both the client and the counselor. Many agencies and other settings in which counselors are employed have standard dis -
closure statements that must be used. For counselors who are self-employed or who must create their own statements, Appendix B includes model disclosure statements for counselors who are providing counseling services to clients in a private practice and for employed counselors who are providing counseling services to clients in an agency or mental health center, respectively. The model counseling disclosure statements for private practice and for agencies or mental health centers are similar in many ways. However, fee issues and some other aspects of the disclosure  statements are quite different. Additional disclosure statements found in Appendix B are for counselors who evaluate individuals, who counsel mandated or involuntary clients, and who counsel in schools.
Beyond the informed consent areas required by the ACA Code of Ethics (2014) and areas 
suggested in the literature, some legal concerns need to be addressed in disclosure statements. The following informed consent issues are particularly sensitive and could lead to legal problems if not handled properly. Examples of problems that could arise are included. A client might have the basis for a lawsuit against a counselor if the client believes, for some reason, that the counselor had done any of the following:
1. Failed to include required HIPAA elements (see Chapter 6). Example: A client was not 
notified when they began counseling that they had the right to review their counseling records. The client complained to the federal government, and the counselor subsequently was asked to verify that they had informed the client of these rights.
2. Guaranteed an outcome as a result of counseling. Example: A wife agreed to enter marriage 
counseling with her husband. She believed that, by agreeing to engage in counseling, she was guaranteed that her marriage would not end. She says the counselor told her that counseling was “the only thing that would save her marriage.” She felt betrayed by the counselor when, after five sessions, her husband moved out of their home and filed for divorce. She sues the counselor for breach of contract, conspiracy, and alienation of affection.
3. Guaranteed privacy with no exceptions. Example: A new client expresses concern to their coun-
selor that others might find out what they tell the counselor in their sessions. To reduce the client’s anxiety, the counselor explains the ethical and legal responsibilities that exist to protect the client’s privacy. The client subsequently believes that the counselor stated that they would never, under any circumstances, reveal to anyone else information shared in their counseling sessions. Later the counselor informs the client’s spouse, over the client’s objection, that the client has expressed sui -
cidal ideas and that the counselor believes that the client should be evaluated to determine whether they are at risk. The client sues the counselor for breach of contract, malpractice, and intentional infliction of emotional distress.
M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   99M04_REML8070_07_SE_C04.indd   99 09/11/23   8:34 PM09/11/23   8:34 PM

100 Section 2 • Issues
4. Agreed to fee arrangements different from what was intended. Example: A counselor begins 
a counseling relationship with a client, charging the client the counselor’s standard rate of  
$75 per hour. After 3 months of sessions once a week, the counselor tells the client that the fees 
will be increased in 1 month to $90 per hour. The client objects, saying that they believe the new hourly rate is too high. The counselor replies that they are not willing to provide further counseling services to the client unless the client agrees to pay the new rate. The client sues the counselor for breach of contract and abandonment.
5. Touched without implied or actual permission. Example: During a group counseling expe-
rience, a counselor asks group members, who are blindfolded, to allow themselves to fall backward into the arms of another group member to demonstrate the difficulty of trusting another person. A female group member reluctantly participates and is caught by a male group member. The female group member is very upset after the exercise and leaves the session vis-ibly shaken. The female client sues the counselor for breach of contract, breach of fiduciary duty, assault, battery, and sexual assault.
6. Misrepresented credentials. Example: A client begins counseling sessions with a  master’s-level 
licensed counselor. The client tells their family members and friends that they are seeing a psy -
chologist for therapy. The client makes out their checks to “Dr.,” notes on the checks that they are paying for “psychological services,” and gives the checks each week to the office receptionist, who deposits them. At the ninth session, the client calls the counselor “Doctor,” and the counselor cor -
rects the client. The client says, “You are a psychologist, aren’t you?” The client is very upset when they realize that the counselor is a master’s-level licensed counselor. The client sues the counselor for breach of contract and fraudulent misrepresentation.
7. Failed to communicate the nature of counseling. Example: A client has taken a new job in 
which they are required periodically to give presentations to small groups of potential clients. The client is very anxious about speaking before groups and has sought counseling to overcome the associated anxiety. The counselor believes that the client’s anxiety is the result of low self-esteem and focuses on positive reinforcement of the client’s positive attributes. After five sessions, the client complains that they are still as anxious as they were when they began counseling. The client tells the counselor that they had expected to be taught how to give presentations without being anxious and does not understand why the counselor has not provided books to read, given advice on how to be less anxious, or helped them practice making presentations during sessions. The client sues the counselor for breach of contract and malpractice.
8. Neglected to warn client about possible stigma. Example: After the first session with a new 
client, the counselor completes an insurance form at the client’s request. The counselor indicates on the form that the client has had a single episode of depression and assigns the appropriate DSM diagnosis. The counselor counsels the client for 10 sessions, terminating the sessions after the client moves to a new city hundreds of miles away. Two years later the client contacts the counselor and complains that they have been denied a security clearance for a job they have applied for because the counselor diagnosed them, without their knowledge or consent, with a mental disorder. The client sues the counselor for breach of contract, malpractice, and defamation.
These eight hypothetical situations illustrate the importance of ensuring that counselors and 
their clients have the same understanding regarding their relationship before counseling begins. All of these problems could have been prevented if the counselors had fully informed the clients regard-ing the counseling relationship. Since HIPAA has been implemented, all counselors must give their clients written informed consent documents to sign. To be effective, however, these documents must include essential information beyond the HIPAA requirements.
