Chapter 9  • Boundary Issues 249
PHYSICAL CONTACT WITH CLIENTS  The boundary question of whether counselors should touch 
or hug their clients is not easy to resolve. For certain clients, at certain times, a reassuring touch or 
a gentle hug can be facilitative. Yet counselors who engage in these behaviors risk having their ges-tures misinterpreted as sexual advances or as violations of the client’s personal space.
The issue of therapeutic touch has a lengthy history. When talk therapy first began in the 
Freudian era, physical contact with clients was prohibited because of its presumed negative effect on transference and countertransference. Later, in the 1960s and 1970s, touching became an accepted practice within the human potential movement. In more recent decades, it appears to us that con-cerns about the consequences of physical contact with clients have been driven more by legal than by ethical concerns. Gutheil and Gabbard (1993) related a story of a therapist who was caught by surprise when a client suddenly hugged her on her way out at the end of the counseling session. The therapist felt somewhat uncomfortable about the incident but left it undocumented and did not engage in further self-reflection. Some weeks later she received a letter from the client’s attorney. Probably because such stories are frightening, counselors usually are now trained to be cautious about making physical contact. They are often advised to hug a client only when the client requests it or, at the very least, when the counselor first secures the client’s permission. Certainly, profes-sional liability insurance carriers have become concerned that clients might bring suit against their counselors for even well-meaning instances of physical contact. Some applications for malprac-tice insurance directly ask the question “Do you ever touch a client beyond a routine handshake?” Counselors who answer “yes” must attach an explanation and run the risk of being deemed a risky applicant and having their application for insurance rejected. In contrast to the legal perspective, counselors seem to value touch for its therapeutic potential and view physical contact with clients as ethical. Of the mental health counselors who responded to a survey by Carlisle et al. (2022), nearly 80% believed it was ethical to console a client by touching them (e.g., placing a hand on the shoul-der), and nearly 73% believed it was ethical to hug a client.
DIVERSITY CONSIDERATIONS IN TOUCHING CLIENTS.  When you begin to practice as a counselor, 
you will need to determine your own stance toward physical contact with your clients. Many factors might be considered, such as the age of the client. Routinely hugging elementary school children is probably more acceptable than giving frequent hugs to adult clients. However, counselors who hug clients run the risk of a sexual impropriety complaint if people report having seen them hugging their young clients. With teens and adults, you will want to assess the likelihood that the client may sexualize or misinterpret your touch. The client’s diagnosis and history may also be relevant. For instance, hug-ging or touching a client who has been sexually abused generally is contraindicated.
Cultures vary widely with respect to the acceptability of touch. For example, people from 
some European countries routinely kiss each other on both cheeks as a way of greeting, and these clients might think you are cold and distant if you avoid physical contact with them.
The bottom line regarding the issue of therapeutic touch is that it is a matter of professional 
judgment. There are no definitive guidelines to be found in codes of ethics. In making your own determination about whether to touch a client, you will thus need to be clear about your motivations for doing so. Zur and Nordmarken (2010) have cautioned against avoiding touch simply out of fear of litigation. If you do use therapeutic touch in your practice, you must be able to demonstrate that touching has served the client’s needs and not your own.
CULTURAL CONSIDERATIONS  Cultural factors are often important variables in ethical decision mak-
ing regarding therapeutic boundaries. In this chapter, we have noted that bartering, gift giving, self-disclosure, and therapeutic touch are viewed differently in various cultures. Also, a counselor’s own 
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   249M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   249 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

250 Section 2 • Issues
cultural background may contribute to their perspective on therapeutic boundaries: In some cultures, it 
is considered polite and respectful to adhere strictly to boundaries of personal space and roles, whereas such behavior is considered rude and rejecting in other cultures. The counselor’s religious affiliation is another potentially influencing factor; some religions support and encourage extended relationships among congregants and, as was noted, some clients prefer to work with a counselor who shares their religious affiliation. The counselor’s gender may also make a difference, as female therapists tend to be more conservative than males in their ethical decision making around boundary issues (Sonne, 2005).
Ethical Decision-Making Models
Several writers (Burian & Slimp,  2000; Herlihy & Corey, 2015b; Pope & Keith-Spiegel, 2008; Younggren, 2002) have offered decision-making models to assist counselors who are faced with potential dual or multiple relationships. These decision-making models have much in common. They suggest, as a first step, that the counselor determine whether the potential dual relationship is potentially beneficial or harmful and whether it is avoidable. Before embarking on or declining to participate in a dual relationship, counselors must judge whether the benefits outweigh the risks or whether the reverse is true. They need to consider the factors that create a potential for harm, including differences in the client’s expectations of the counselor in the two roles, the counselor’s divergent responsibilities in the two roles, and the power differential in their relationship. Pope and Keith-Spiegel (2008) suggested that counselors imagine what might be the best possible outcome and the worst possible outcome of crossing a boundary or engaging in a dual relationship.
If the counselor believes that the risk of harm to the client is greater than the potential ben-
efits, the counselor should decline to enter the dual relationship and refer if needed. An explanation should be given so that the client understands the rationale for not proceeding with the problematic part of the relationship.
If the counselor believes that the potential benefits to the client are great and the risk of harm 
is small, or if the potential dual relationship cannot be prevented, then the dual relationship can be initiated, as long as the following safeguards are put in place:
• Secure the client’s informed consent to proceed with the dual relationship. The counselor and the client should discuss the potential problems and benefits and reach an understanding regard-ing how they want to handle these problems if they arise. Younggren (2002) has recommended that the client sign an informed consent document, although he also cautions that clients can never give truly informed consent to something that poses a severe risk to them or that violates the prevailing standard of care. For example, even if a client were to sign a consent to enter into a sexual relationship with a counselor, the consent document would not assist the counselor, in either the ethical or the legal arena, in a defense of the relationship.
• Seek consultation with colleagues. Because one of the most intransigent problems in managing dual relationships is the counselor’s loss of objectivity, it is vitally important for the counselor to seek ongoing consultation for help in monitoring the relationship and the risk for harm.
• Engage in ongoing discussion with the client. As we discussed in Chapter 1, ethical decision making can be a shared process, and as we discussed in Chapter 4, informed consent is not a one-time matter. The counselor can involve the client in a mutual, ongoing monitoring of the rela-tionship and can discuss any potential problems they might foresee and how they will bring those problems up for discussion. A mutual decision is best in resolving any problems that do arise.
• Document and self-monitor. Although we have heard of instances in which counselors were advised to keep any mention of a dual relationship out of their case notes, we think this is unwise. If a dual relationship ever became an issue in a complaint proceeding before a licensure 
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   250M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   250 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

Chapter 9  • Boundary Issues 251
board or in a court of law, behavior that appears as if the counselor is trying to hide something 
will not be seen favorably by the parties adjudicating the complaint. It is much better for coun -
selors to be able to demonstrate that they were aware of dualities, that they considered the risks and benefits for the client, and that they took steps to protect the client.
• Obtain supervision. If the risks in a dual relationship seem high, if the relationship is particularly complex, or if the counselor is concerned about their ability to assess the situation objectively, seek-ing consultation may not be sufficient. In these instances, counselors are wise to engage a fellow mental health professional in ongoing supervision of their work throughout the dual relationship.
It is also important that counselors remain vigilant to warning signs of potential problems 
with boundary management. “Red flags” include going out of your way to extend counseling ses-
sions (e.g., scheduling them at the end of the day); self-disclosing more information than is usual for you; allowing a client to accumulate unpaid bills or fall behind in payments; communicating with a client via email, text, instant message, or other electronic means more frequently than you do with other clients; acting impulsively toward a client; and feeling attracted to a client (Reamer, 2020). Whenever you find yourself making small exceptions to your usual practices, it would be wise to follow the guidelines suggested above.
In concluding this section, we want to emphasize that boundary issues and dual relation-
ships pose some complex and difficult questions to which there are no easy answers. Each client is unique, each counselor is unique, and each situation is unique and constantly evolving (Pope & Keith-Spiegel, 2008). Counselors will struggle with boundary setting throughout their professional careers. In the absence of certainties, counselors must think carefully about the consequences of their decisions, have a clear rationale for any boundary crossings, be open to discussing the issues with their clients (who are equally affected by any decisions), and consult with colleagues.
SEXUAL DUAL RELATIONSHIPS
The prohibition against sexual intimacies with help seekers is one of the oldest ethical mandates in the health care professions, predating even the Hippocratic Oath. Nonetheless, ethics codes of mental health professions made no mention of this behavior until research began to demonstrate its prevalence and the harm done to clients (Pope & Vasquez, 2016). Although the problem of sexual relationships between counselors and their clients has existed for many decades, it remained unac-knowledged in the professional literature through the 1970s. Counselor–client sexual intimacy was the problem with no name (Davidson, 1977) because helping professionals were reluctant to con-
front the issue. The reluctance was so pervasive that Pope (1988) concluded that helping profession-als had engaged in “massive denial” (p. 222) of the problem. It was through the pioneering work of a few researchers—such as Gartrell et al. (1987) in psychiatry and Pope (1986, 1988) in psychology and counseling—that helping professionals were made aware that such violations were occurring, and in startling numbers.
Although the limitations of self-report data make it difficult to gauge how commonly counselor– 
client sexual intimacies actually occur, a substantial number of studies were conducted in the 1980s and 1990s, indicating that approximately 7% of male counselors and 1.6% of female counselors reported sexual relationships with their current or former clients. It is safe to state that these esti-mates are probably conservative. When Pope (1986) surveyed patients rather than counselors, as many as 20% of them reported having had sexual contact with their counselors.
In recent years, results of studies of complaints to state counselor licensure boards consistently 
have revealed that dual relationships, both sexual and nonsexual, are one of the most frequently 
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   251M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   251 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

252 Section 2 • Issues
claimed types of violations that resulted in disciplinary action (Wilkinson et al., 2019). Wilkinson  
et al. (2019) found that if sexual and nonsexual dual relationships were considered as a single cate-gory, this would be the most frequent type of violation that led to disciplinary action. Unfortunately, sexual relationships with clients continue to be a frequent complaint against counselors that are filed with counselor licensure boards.
It is likely that few counselors who enter into sexual dual relationships with clients initiate these 
relationships with the intention of harming the client. As is true with nonsexual dual relationships, the onset can be insidious. Sexual boundary violations often begin as minor boundary crossings, which are repeated and then evolve (Cruikshanks & Burns, 2017) into serious boundary violations.
The Offending Mental Health Professional
Mental health professionals who engage in sex with their clients have not been well studied. Results of the few studies that were conducted prior to the year 2000 seem to suggest that offending coun-selors do not fit a single profile, although they do seem to share characteristics with other impaired professionals, including professional burnout and a pattern of attempting to meet their own personal needs through their clients.
It is natural to feel compassion for some offenders who feel remorse for their behavior, but it 
is important to remember that ignorance is never a valid excuse, nor is blaming the client for being seductive. It is always the responsibility of the helping professional to ensure that sexual intimacies do not occur.
Harm to Clients
It has been amply demonstrated that sexual relationships with counselors are extremely detrimental and sometimes even devastating to clients (Moleski & Kiselica, 2005). The harm is deep, lasting, and occasionally permanent.
Pope (1988) suggested that clients are likely to suffer from therapist–patient sex syndrome, 
with reactions similar to those of victims of rape, spouse battering, incest, and posttraumatic stress disorder. He described a range of associated symptoms: Clients often experience a deep ambiva-lence toward the offending counselor. They are trapped between extreme dependency on and fear of separation from the counselor and a longing to escape from his power and influence. Like many victims of incest and battering, clients vacillate between wanting to flee from the abuser and want-ing to cling to and protect him.
Clients often suffer from feelings of guilt, believing that they are to blame for the relationship. 
They may think that they did something to invite the counselor’s behavior. Although they may be deeply angry with the counselor, their rage remains suppressed due to their feelings of ambivalence, their sense of guilt, and the counselor’s continuing power. This guilt and rage, when turned inward, lead to an increased risk of suicide.
Because the offending counselor insists that the client keep their sexual relationship secret, 
clients feel isolated and cut off from the normal world of human experience. Not surprisingly, many victims manifest a profound confusion about their sexuality and about appropriate roles and inter -
personal boundaries. When roles are reversed and the client becomes the counselor’s emotional caretaker, the client does not know where safe and appropriate boundaries lie.
Counseling involves a high degree of trust, and violation of that trust can have life-long 
consequences. Clients’ impaired ability to trust often prevents them from seeking help from other counselors and may impair their ability to form other close relationships.
In addition, many clients display symptoms similar to those experienced by victims of post-
traumatic stress disorder. These symptoms include difficulties with attention and concentration, the 
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   252M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   252 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

Chapter 9  • Boundary Issues 253
reexperiencing of overwhelming emotional reactions when they become involved with a sexual 
partner, and nightmares and flashbacks.
The harm to clients caused by sexual intimacies with their counselors is now well recognized. 
The ethical standards of all mental health providers’ professional organizations, including the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), prohibit sexual relationships with clients. There are no credible voices in the profession arguing that sexual relationships with clients should be allowed.
The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) prohibits counselors from having sexual or romantic interac-
tions or relationships with current clients, their romantic partners, or their family members (§A.5.a.). Romantic or sexual relationships with former clients (and their romantic partners or family mem-bers) are prohibited for at least 5 years following the last professional contact (§A.5.c.). These pro-hibitions extend to electronic or online interactions.
Typically, codes of ethics for mental health professionals prohibit sexual relationships with for -
mer clients for a minimum of 2 years. As just noted, the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) specifies a mini-mum of 5 years. However, it is doubtful that a court of law would require a 5-year interval. Ethical standards reach for the ideal, and legal standards define the minimally acceptable behavior that society will tolerate from a professional counselor. A board of ethics might find a counselor in violation of the ethics code for having had a harmful romantic relationship with a former client’s sister-in-law 4 years after the counseling relationship ended, but it is doubtful that a judge would find that the counselor had committed malpractice for the same behavior. Interestingly, lawyers are still debating whether it is acceptable for attorneys to have sexual relationships with their clients (Buckner & Sall, 2008).
Legal Consequences for Offending Counselors
The harm caused to clients by sexual dual relationships is universally recognized and can have severe legal as well as ethical consequences. As Kaplan et al. (2009) have noted, the fact that sexual relationships with clients are the only exclusion contained in most professional liability insurance policies is an indication of just how harmful these relationships are. Most professional liability poli-cies will provide an attorney to defend a counselor against an accusation of sexual improprieties with a client, but if the counselor is found guilty, the insurance company will not pay for damages and will also expect to be reimbursed by the counselor for any legal fees incurred.
CIVIL LAWSUITS  Because the impropriety of sexual intimacies with clients is universally recog-
nized, clients who sue counselors for having been sexually involved with them have an excellent chance of winning their lawsuits if the allegations are true. These lawsuits are civil, which means that one citizen must sue another for action to be taken against a person who does something wrong.
Clients who were sexually victimized by their counselors and who are ashamed, uneducated, 
or lacking in self-confidence might choose not to file formal complaints or civil lawsuits against their perpetrators. When that happens, counselors who violate our ethical standards and violate the rights of their clients are never held accountable. In fact, many believe that the lack of accountability encour -
ages offending counselors to sexually violate other clients. When some counselors observe that others are getting away with having sexual relationships with clients, they may be inclined to do so as well.
Counselors who engage in sexual relationships with their clients may be sued on a number 
of grounds. Jorgenson (1995) has listed the following causes of action that victimized clients might allege in their lawsuits: malpractice, negligent infliction of emotional distress, battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress, fraudulent misrepresentation, breach of contract, breach of warranty, and spouse loss of consortium (love, companionship, and services). As discussed in Chapter 8, one of the elements a client must prove in a malpractice suit is that a counselor behaved in a manner that breached the standard of care expected or required of counselors.
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   253M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   253 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

254 Section 2 • Issues
To encourage clients who are victimized by their mental health professionals to sue, a number 
of state legislatures have now passed laws that automatically charge negligence for certain catego-
ries of mental health professionals who have sexual relationships with their clients (e.g., Cal. Civ. Code sec. 43.93; Ill. Ann. State. Ch. 70, secs. 801–802; Minn. Stat. Ann. Sec. 148A; Wis. Stat. Ann. Sec. 895. 70(2)). Those who sue must still prove they were harmed because of the sexual relation-ships, but harm can be emotional and financial as well as physical.
Some of these statutes have unusual and forceful components. For example, the Wisconsin 
statute (Wis. Stat. Ann. sec. 895. 70(5)) forbids accused mental health professionals from settling their cases without public disclosure. In other words, mental health professionals (or their profes-sional liability insurance companies) who are sued by their clients for having engaged in sex with them cannot agree to an out-of-court settlement that is never reported to the public.
CRIMINALIZATION OF SEX WITH CLIENTS  U.S. society has become so convinced that counselor–
client sexual relationships are wrong that some states have now passed statutes that make it a crime for mental health professionals to engage in sex with their clients. Each state statute varies its in language, but the following professionals have been included in some of the laws: psychotherapists, counselors, marriage and family counselors, clergy, social workers, psychiatrists, and psychologists. Losing a civil lawsuit for having sex with a client can be distressing for counselors, but going to jail for the same thing is a much more dramatic result.
Some of these criminal statutes take unusually tough positions with mental health profes-
sionals. For example, the Colorado statute (Colo. Rev. Stat. secs. 12-43-708 [b & c], 1988) permits prosecutors to file injunctions that will put a mental health professional out of business even before the individual has been found guilty, if it can be proven that the professional presents a risk to clients by continuing to practice.
Postcounseling Sexual Relationships
Ethical standards of the various mental health professions are divided on the issue of whether it is ever ethical for professionals to have sexual relationships with former clients. Social workers are forbidden from ever having a sexual relationship with an individual after a professional relationship has been established (National Association of Social Workers, 2021). However, counselors (ACA, 2014) and psychologists (American Psychological Association [APA], 2017) have determined that after a set period of time following termination, sexual relationships may be permissible with for -
mer clients. ACA has set 5 years after termination as an acceptable period before a sexual relation-ship with a former client might be acceptable, and APA has set 2 years. The APA and ACA codes should not be interpreted as blanket permission to engage in post-termination sexual relationships, however. Counselors who consider entering romantic or sexual relationships with former clients, even after several years have passed, still have an ethical responsibility to ensure that no harm is done. Not only do all the risk factors for post-termination friendships apply to post-termination sexual relationships, but because of the severity of potential harm, it is even more important to put safeguards in place. The ACA Code states that before counselors engage in sexual or romantic interactions or relationships with clients, with clients’ romantic partners, or with clients’ family members, even after 5 years, counselors must “demonstrate forethought and document (in written form) whether the interaction or relationship can be viewed as exploitive in any way and/or whether there is still potential to harm the former client” (ACA, 2014, §A.5.c.).
Just as professional associations disagree about whether sexual relationships are acceptable 
after a waiting period, there is some evidence that counselors are also divided on the issue. Among the mental health counselors surveyed by Williamson et  al. (2019), nearly 40% believed it was 
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   254M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   254 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

Chapter 9  • Boundary Issues 255
ethical to become sexually involved with a client at least 5 years after the counseling relationship 
has ended. In our opinion, sexual relationships with former clients are not appropriate under any circumstances. The risk of harm is far too high.
Sexual Attraction to Clients
Although the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) explicitly forbids sexual relationships with clients, it does not address more subtle ways that sexuality can become part of a counseling relationship. It is not at all unlikely that, at some point in your professional career, you will find that you are sexually attracted to a client. When this happens, it is natural to react with feelings of guilt or self-doubt. Sexual attraction to a client is not aberrant or unusual, however (Pope et al., 2021). What is impor -
tant to understand is that feeling a sexual attraction to a client is not unethical. What is unethical is to act on that attraction.
When you feel a sexual attraction to a client, you have an ethical responsibility to acknowl-
edge and deal with it appropriately. Some useful strategies include consulting with colleagues, carefully considering issues of client welfare, seeking supervision, and self-monitoring to ascertain whether you are feeling particularly needy or vulnerable. If your own needs and issues seem to be causing you to act in ways that are uncharacteristic of you, you will be wise to seek counseling for yourself to resolve those issues. Koocher and Keith-Spiegel (2016) emphasized the importance of counselors discussing a sexual attraction with a trusted colleague, a supervisor, or a counselor.
We often hear from counselors that their graduate programs gave little if any attention to the 
issue of sexual attraction to clients. If counselor educators do not discuss this, they may be implic-itly reinforcing the idea that sexual attraction is unethical or inappropriate, or that it is a rare occur -
rence that doesn’t warrant attention. Counselors will be better equipped to handle sexual attractions if they have opportunities to discuss them as students and as supervisees, in a safe and accepting environment (Corey et al., 2024; Jacob & Prikkhidko, 2022; Jacob et al., 2022).
Sometimes it will be the client who develops and expresses an attraction to the counselor. The 
counselor must handle such client disclosures with tact and work to ensure that the client under -
stands that a dating or sexual relationship is not possible. Counselor educators can role-play this type of encounter so that students develop skill and comfort in managing these client disclosures.
Counseling Clients Who Have Been Abused  
by Previous Counselors
In your practice, you should be prepared to counsel clients who report that a previous counselor has 
sexually exploited them. Welfel (2016), after reviewing prevalence studies, concluded that between 22% and 65% of mental health professionals have encountered such clients.
Counselors who provide services to individuals who have been sexually abused by mental 
health professionals are put in awkward positions. Most counselors feel obligated to take some action to bring attention to the wrongdoing of a fellow mental health professional. These counselors are afraid that if they do not take some action, more clients may be abused.
We caution you, though, that if you are ever put in this situation, you should be respectful of your 
client’s wishes in the matter. Clients must be willing not only to allege that a mental health professional has abused them but also to testify at formal hearings, where they will probably be cross-examined in a hostile and accusing manner and will have to deal with the emotional strain of the process.
Your role, as a counselor, is to assist your client in meeting the counseling goals that the two 
of you establish together. If the client decides to proceed against the perpetrator in some manner, you can be helpful in assisting the client with this very difficult situation.
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   255M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   255 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

256 Section 2 • Issues
It most likely will do no good for you to file an ethics complaint against another mental 
health professional if the victim refuses to participate. You would be violating your client’s privacy 
if you disclosed the client’s identity without his or her permission. In addition, most licensure boards, criminal prosecutors, or certification groups will not proceed in a case without a witness who was a victim.
Your role as a counselor is not to push your client toward accusing the mental health profes-
sional. You must guard against intrusive advocacy in your zeal to ensure that the wrong is redressed (Wohlberg, 1999). Rather, your job is to provide appropriate counseling services, to avoid imposing your own values, and to assist clients in meeting their personal goals.
It is important to be aware that clients who have been sexually exploited by a previous mental 
health professional tend to be especially vulnerable to revictimization when their counseling needs are not recognized and addressed. Counseling these clients requires sensitivity and expertise. If you undertake a counseling relationship with such a client, you should read the literature on the topic, get qualified supervision, and be prepared to have strong emotional reactions to the client’s disclo-sures (Welfel, 2019).
To avoid becoming inappropriately involved if a client is trying to decide whether to accuse a 
former mental health professional of inappropriate sexual activity, you might consider referring the client to an advocacy group. Many such groups are available to assist clients in making the initial decisions and then to support them once the process begins.
Summary and Key Points
Therapeutic boundaries and dual relationships are among the most controversial of all ethical issues in the counseling field. A boundary can be conceptual-ized as a frame around the therapeutic relationship that defines the participants’ roles in that relation-ship. Counselors enter into dual relationships when-ever they take on two or more roles, simultaneously or sequentially, with their clients.
Codes of ethics discourage nonprofessional 
or dual relationships, except for those relationships that potentially could benefit a client. The only type of dual relationship that is absolutely forbidden is sexual intimacy in most circumstances. Counselors must consider a number of factors when they are contemplating entering into a nonsexual dual rela-tionship, in order to determine the potential for harm to the client.
Most counselors occasionally engage in 
boundary crossings, which are departures from usual practice that are made to benefit a particular client at a particular time. For instance, a counselor would not routinely meet clients at out-of-the-office functions but might choose to attend a client’s graduation or other special occasion. From a legal perspective, counselors who develop a pattern of frequent boundary crossings are at risk.
Several specific boundary issues were dis-
cussed in this chapter. These included bartering, social and business relationships with clients, post-termination friendships, accepting gifts from clients, counselor self-disclosure, and physical contact with clients. Throughout the discussion of these issues, multicultural considerations were highlighted. Guidelines for ethical decision mak-ing completed the first section of this chapter. Key points with respect to nonsexual dual relationships are as follows:
• Dual relationship issues are complex and can be 
difficult to resolve.
• The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) provides exten-
sive guidance on the types of roles and relation-ships that must be avoided due to potential harm to clients.
• The ACA Code of Ethics also acknowledges that 
some nonprofessional relationships can poten-tially be beneficial.
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   256M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   256 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

Chapter 9  • Boundary Issues 257
• With the exception of sexual dual relationships, 
the thinking of the counseling profession has evolved toward more flexibility in determining boundaries.
• Counselors who are considering entering into dual relationships should carefully consider the risks and potential benefits.
• Dual relationships may be unavoidable in some settings, such as rural communities, the mili-tary, pastoral counseling, addictions treatment, in-home counseling, disaster response envi-ronments, and counselor education programs.
• A number of counselor behaviors do not by themselves constitute dual relationships but have the potential to create dual relationships. These behaviors can be termed boundary crossings to distinguish them from boundary violations, which are harmful.
• From both an ethical and a legal perspective, coun-selors are well advised to avoid developing a pat-tern of frequent boundary crossings with clients.
• Bartering with a client for goods or services is fraught with potential problems and should generally be avoided, unless certain criteria are clearly met or it is common practice among pro-fessionals in a community or culture.
• Although counselors may be tempted to develop social relationships or even friendships with cur -
rent or former clients, counselors should not get their own social needs met through their interac-tions with clients.
• Business or financial relationships with clients should be avoided.
• Although counselors should be cautious about accepting gifts from their clients, there may be instances when accepting a gift is appropriate. 
Counselors should consider a number of factors when deciding whether to accept or refuse a gift from a client.
• Self-disclosure on the part of counselors is appropriate only when it is done for the benefit of the client.
• The issue of physical contact with clients is dif-ficult to resolve, and many factors must be con-sidered. Today’s legal climate tends to discourage counselors from the practice of therapeutic touch, although counselors do not tend to see touching as unethical.
• Decisions about dual relationships require con-sideration of cultural variables.
The second section of this chapter focused on 
sexual dual relationships. Sexual intimacies with clients are probably the most harmful of all types of dual relationships. The following are key points about sexual dual relationships:
• Pope (1988) has described the harm to clients as 
a therapist–patient sex syndrome that is similar 
to posttraumatic stress disorder.
• Legal consequences for offending counselors can be severe. They can be prosecuted in civil court and, in an increasing number of states, in criminal court as well.
•  Counselors who provide services to clients who have been sexually abused by their former coun-selors face some difficult decisions. They must therefore keep in mind that their role is to assist these clients in meeting goals that are chosen by the clients themselves.
M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   257M09_REML8070_07_SE_C09.indd   257 09/11/23   8:24 PM09/11/23   8:24 PM

title: Chapter 10: Technology in Counseling
content:
258
10Technology in Counseling
 LEARNING OBJECTIVES
10.1 Demonstrate an understanding of why it is 
important for counselors to understand how their clients are using technology and social media.
10.2 List advantages and disadvantages of counselors providing virtual counseling instead of in-person counseling.
10.3 Describe advances in counselors’ use of technology and discuss potential concerns.
10.4 Explain the ethical issues involved in counselors using electronic means to communicate with their clients.FOCUS QUESTIONS
1. What do you think about 
counselors providing counseling services to clients using Zoom or a similar type of compressed video technology?
2.  What problems do clients bring to counseling sessions that are directly related to their use of social media or other forms of technology?
3. What steps do counselors need to take to ensure that counseling records kept electronically are secure and that client privacy is maintained?
4. What do you think about the idea of an avatar or a robot that mimics human interactions counseling a client?Counselors’ use of a variety of technologies has increased significantly during the current decade. The use of com-pressed video technology to provide counseling services is too new for the popular or professional literature to have set-tled on terminology that will be used regarding the phenom-enon. Counseling using compressed video is being referred to as virtual counseling, telemental health counseling, tele-therapy, online counseling, remote counseling, e-counseling, and many other terms. In this chapter we use the term virtual counseling to refer to the phenomenon of counselors pro-viding services to clients where both can see and hear each other while they are located in different places.
The Impact of the COVID-19 
Pandemic on Technology in Counseling
Since the last edition of this textbook, the world of counseling 
has changed dramatically due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Using compressed video and electronic communications rather than in-person sessions for counseling, supervision, and the teaching of counseling to graduate students had been considered experimental and a questionable practice.  
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   258M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   258 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 259
Then suddenly, almost overnight, when the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown occurred, that practice 
became the norm. The pivot from traditional to digital approaches in the counseling profession was surprisingly successful, but many concerns were expressed by counselors, supervisors, and counselor educators about whether the transition from in-person to online counseling was ethical, legal, or pro -
fessional (Becnel et al., 2023; Lipschitz et al., 2022).
Professionals continue to question whether counseling delivered virtually is as effective as 
in-person counseling (McCord et al., 2022; Uhl et al., 2022). However, research studies are being published that suggest virtual counseling is as effective as in-person counseling with specific popu-lations and client concerns. Despite uncertainties about the effectiveness of virtual counseling, the practice is taking place everywhere. Even though in-person counseling services have resumed to some degree, it appears that virtual counseling is here to stay in the United States. Artificial intelli-gence has been introduced into the counseling profession, and in some instances, avatars that mimic humans instead of human counselors are interacting with clients.
In this chapter, we summarize the ethical and legal issues surrounding the use of technology 
in counseling and pay particular attention to guidelines that have been developed by professional associations, certification boards, state licensure boards, and governmental agencies regarding the use of technology in the delivery of mental health services. The COVID-19 pandemic has had an impact on clients’ use of technology and virtual counseling and on counselors’ use of technology. We discuss each of these areas in this chapter.
 THE IMPACT OF TECHNOLOGY ON  
THE COUNSELING PROFESSION
Even before COVID-19, technological advances in society, especially the internet, were having a sig-
nificant impact on the profession of counseling (Hertlein et al., 2014; Kraus et al., 2010; Ziv, 2014). The counseling profession has established a substantial presence on the internet: Technology offers counselors abundant opportunities to access virtual libraries for researching the latest information on client problems and effective counseling techniques, as well as to collaborate and consult with other professionals around the world. The National Board for Certified Counselors (NBCC), the Council for Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs (CACREP), and many of the divisions of the American Counseling Association (ACA) have homepages on the internet, which means that you can communicate quickly and directly with professional groups that are working to strengthen counsel-ing as a profession. Videoconferencing on the internet enables you and counselors everywhere to obtain further preparation without having to travel great distances. You can also subscribe to mailing lists that allow you to share experiences, ask questions, and exchange information and ideas.
Technology is being used today in mental health in ways that were previously unimagined. In a 
study completed prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Steele et al. (2020) found that school counselors have increased their use of technology for student planning and are continuing to use technology in responsive services. Counselors are providing real-time, two-way virtual counseling sessions to clients who are on the other side of the world; clients are presenting issues related specifically to their social media activities such as being addicted to the internet, being the victim of cyberbullying, and leaving current relationships for ones they found online. In addition, counselors are utilizing biofeedback in their practices (Crockett et al., 2017), and clients with dementia are being tracked with GPS devices (Landau & Werner, 2012). Virtual reality interventions and smartphone apps are being used to treat clients with anxiety disorders (Firth et al., 2018) and other conditions. Smartphones allow counselors to utilize multiple forms of technology in their practices (Epstein & Bequette, 2013). Legal and ethical challenges abound as counselors utilize technology in creative ways in their practices each day.
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   259M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   259 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

260 Section 2 • Issues
The various forms of technology that have been developed and are still being created all have 
the same basic purposes—to facilitate the transfer of information and enhance the ability to commu-
nicate with others. In this transfer and communication process, situations inevitably occur in which sensitive or confidential information might be inappropriately accessed. Every form of technology discussed in this section creates records or recordings of information that, if not treated carefully, could lead to the privacy of clients being compromised.
Certainly, counselors want to maximize their effectiveness by utilizing as many forms of 
technology as possible (Sampson & Bloom, 2001). It is also imperative that counselors educate themselves regarding the subtle ways in which their clients’ privacy might be affected by the use of technology (Cottone et al., 2022). When the ACA Code of Ethics (2014) was last revised, an entirely new section was added to address technology issues (i.e., Section H: Distance Counseling, Technology, and Social Media). Many other standards related to technology issues are found throughout the 2014 Code.
The introduction to Section H of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), which is devoted 
to ethical issues related to technology, states that counselors, whether or not they want to, must adapt: “Counselors actively attempt to understand the evolving nature of the profession with regard to distance counseling, technology, and social media and how such resources may be used to better serve their clients. Counselors strive to become knowledgeable about these resources.” Standard H.1.a. continues, “Counselors who engage in the use of distance counsel-ing, technology, and/or social media develop knowledge and skills regarding related technical, ethical and legal considerations (e.g., special certifications, additional course work).” So, the Code of Ethics requires that counselors actively attempt to understand the new issues related to technology in counseling, and further, that if they engage in the use of technology, they develop knowledge and skills for doing so.
In the following discussion, we identify a number of areas in which technology is having an 
impact on the counseling process, and we provide guidelines for ensuring that the privacy of clients is protected. In addition, counselors’ personal and professional use of technology is reviewed in relation to ethical and legal obligations.
Client Use of Technology
Clients use technology in numerous ways of which counselors need to be aware. Clients go online to use various types of social media and entertainment technology and to engage in self-monitoring and self-improvement, all without counselor supervision.
SOCIAL MEDIA  The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) Glossary defines social media as 
“ technology-based forms of communication of ideas, beliefs, personal histories, etc. (e.g., social networking sites, blogs).” Social media have been categorized by a number of experts, without consensus regarding what is included in social media (Boundless, 2014; Grahl, 2014; White, 2014). Grahl (2014) has listed six types of social media: (1) social networks, (2) bookmarking sites,  
(3) social news, (4) media sharing, (5) microblogging, and (6) blog comments and forums. These categories are not discrete, and sometimes they overlap in content and function.
• Social networks allow you to connect with other people and usually include a user’s profile and various ways to interact with other users. The most popular social networks are Facebook, X (formerly Twitter), Instagram, WhatsApp, TikTok, and Snapchat.
• Bookmarking sites offer services that allow you to save, organize, and manage links to websites and resources on the internet. The most popular bookmarking sites are X (formerly Twitter), Pinterest, Pocket, Google Bookmarks, and Mix (previously StumbleUpon).
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   260M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   260 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 261
• Social news includes services people use to post various news items or links to articles. Users 
then vote, and the items receiving the most votes are displayed the most prominently. The most popular social news sites are Reddit, Digg, and Propeller.
• Media sharing allows users to upload and share media, including photos and videos. The most popular media sharing sites are Facebook, YouTube, and WhatsApp.
• Microblogging is characterized by short updates that are sent to those who subscribe. The most popular microblogging sites are X (formerly Twitter), Tumblr, Mastodon, and LinkedIn.
• Forums allow members to post messages that are available to other members. Blog comments are similar, except that they are attached to blogs and include discussions about the blog. There are too many popular blogs and forums to identify the most popular ones.
Despite the lack of consensus about what is included in social media, everyone agrees that it is 
very popular and that people of all ages are involved in social media in a variety of formats. Client use 
of social media is a pressing concern for school counselors (Casares & Binkley, 2022; Giordano et al., 2022; Mullen et al., 2014) and child and adolescent counselors. As social media began to be devel -
oped, some counselors were unsure as to how to respond to their clients’ posts. Counselors questioned whether they had an obligation to view what their clients were posting or were not supposed to pry into their clients’ social media posts. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) takes the position that “Counselors respect the privacy of their clients’ presence on social media unless given consent to view such infor -
mation” (ACA, 2014, §H.6.c.). As a result of this standard, it generally is not appropriate for coun -
selors to view posts made by their clients. If clients give their permission for their counselors to view their social media posts, however, counselors may look at them. Even if counselors have been granted permission by their clients to view their social media posts, doing so could lead to liability problems. If counselors view their clients’ social media posts and see that a minor is engaging in behaviors that are concerning, the counselor might have a responsibility to notify their parents or guardians.
Many of the problems that clients now bring to counseling are directly related to the phenom-
enon of social media. A few of these problems include social media addiction, the fear of missing out (FOMO), social isolation, cyberbullying, sexting, and identity theft.
Social media addiction or digital addiction occurs when the personal and professional lives of 
individuals are negatively affected because they cannot stay away from social media sites (Didelot et al., 2012; Griffiths, 2013). Social media use has been directly related to social isolation (Primack et al., 2017), reduced productivity, unhealthy social relationships, and reduced life satisfaction (Sun & Zhang, 2021). Hormes et al. (2014) concluded, in a study of undergraduate students, that the use of social networking sites is potentially addictive. Carlisle et al. (2016) have suggested that internet addiction is a process addiction, or a compulsive behavior that targets a particular activity (such as gambling or binge eating). Chibbaro et al. (2019) indicated that counselors need to be able to iden-tify young people who have developed digital addiction. They noted that the signs and symptoms of individuals who have digital addiction include loss of control over time, cravings for engagement, and social problems. Young (2023) has developed an instrument to help assess internet addiction and has identified these symptoms: neglecting friends and family; losing sleep to stay online; feel-ing ashamed, anxious, or depressed as a result of online behavior; backaches, headaches, or carpal tunnel syndrome; and withdrawing from social or pleasurable activities. Researchers are continuing to investigate how social media addiction develops so that prevention approaches can be developed (Sun & Zhang, 2021).
Younger clients who have been using technology their entire lives are sometimes referred 
to as digital natives (Gallo et  al., 2016). Some adults may not have heard of FOMO, but youngsters have (Blackwell et al., 2017; White, 2013). FOMO, the fear of missing out, occurs 
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   261M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   261 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

262 Section 2 • Issues
when people are anxious because they believe everyone else is having more fun than they are 
(Wortham, 2011). A result of FOMO is people wanting to stay continually connected to those they know and monitoring what others are doing (Fioravanti et al., 2021; Gori et al., 2023). Results of studies indicate that those in the age range from 18 to 30 have the highest rates of FOMO. Researchers at universities in England (University of Essex, 2013) and the United States have developed a test that allows individuals to determine the extent to which FOMO negatively affects their ability to enjoy what they are doing rather than worry about what others are up to (Przybylski et al., 2013).
Social networking sites such as Facebook have been identified as causing social isolation (Parigi 
& Henson, 2014). However, Grieve et al. (2013), after completing two studies on the topic, concluded that Facebook use could lead to developing and maintaining relationships and to positive psychologi -
cal outcomes. Further research is needed to determine the effects, both positive and negative, of cli -
ents’ use of Facebook. 
CYBERBULLYING  Cyberbullying has been identified as a problem that some clients bring to coun-
seling sessions (Burnham et al., 2011; Florang, 2020; Güllü et al., 2023; Kowalski et al., 2014; Lanzillo, 2021; Olenik-Shemesh et al., 2012; McGuckin et al., 2013; Wright, 2012). Florang (2020) has suggested that past mistakes and misconceptions have hindered efforts to prevent cyberbully-ing. Perpetrators of cyberbullying have been found to be deterred more by the threat of  punishment from their parents and the school and less by the threat of punishment from the police (Patchin & Hinduja, 2018). Helping youngsters understand that adults need to know when they are being bul-lied is important in helping victims. According to Olenik-Shemesh et al. (2012), victims of cyber -
bullying often feel hopeless and are reluctant to report the abuse because they fear their parents will take away their access to social media. Florang (2020) has advised administrators and parents to avoid minimizing the impact cyberbullying has on victims, stop punishing victims by taking away their privileges and freedom, and teach them the skills necessary to overcome cyberbully-ing. After investigating the bullying policies on nine social media platforms, Tinstman et al. (2019) concluded that the terms used vary and are unclear, the language used was punitive and negative, 
cultural awareness was limited to anti-discrimination, and mental health concerns were addressed but seldom associated with cyberbullying.
SEXTING  An area of concern that has emerged for adolescent prob-
lems related to technology is the practice of sexting, which involves sending sexual messages or images through text messaging (Ibtisam, 2017; Kosenko, 2017; McEachern et al., 2012), along with sextortion.
Sextortion has been defined by O’Malley and Holt (2022) 
as a threat to distribute intimate, sexual materials unless a victim agrees to some type of demand such as paying money. The prac-tice of youth sexting and sextortion has been increasing over time (Mori, 2022). After analyzing 39 studies of youth under the age of 18,  
Madigan et  al. (2018) reported that 14.8% had sent sexts and 27.4% had received sexts. In addition, 12% of the young people studied had forwarded sexts without the consent of the persons who had originally posted them. Utilizing a national sample of adolescents between 12 and 17 years old, Giordano et al. (2022) found that approximately 53% had seen pornography, almost 15% had sent a sext, and 24.3% had received 
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   262M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   262 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 263
a sext. There is thus a need to provide youngsters with information about the legal consequences 
of sexting and advice about what to do if they receive a sexting image (Mitchell et al., 2012; Wolfe et  al., 2014). Lunceford (2011) observed that despite the highly publicized suicides that have occurred as a result of sexting, teenagers seem to be unaware of the potential for embarrassment and humiliation when sexts are shared with unintended viewers or even posted on the internet.
VIRTUAL COUNSELING
The traditional process for counseling involves a counselor and one or more clients meeting  face-to-face in a professional office. When the internet came into existence, some counselors began offering vir -
tual counseling. After the COVID-19 pandemic arrived, almost all counseling services were delivered virtually through compressed video, and today clients can find virtual counseling services easily if they prefer that approach over meeting in person with counselors. In addition to virtual counseling, counseling is being provided through telephone calls (Dadfar & Lester, 2021; Rowen et al., 2022); synchronous online interaction, better known as ChatGPT (Dowling & Richwood, 2013); and text 
messages and email exchanges (Fang et al., 2018; Nieuwboer et al., 2015).
A growing number of telehealth platforms, also known as practice management software, 
afford counselors a range of features for delivering virtual counseling services to clients. Popular platforms include Doxy.me, SimplePractice, thera-LINK, TheraPlatform, and TherapyNotes. When selecting a platform, counselors should identify their essential technology needs and consider the suitability of various platforms for serving the broadest range of clients. Some other important con-siderations include monthly cost, ease of use for client and clinician, BAA- and HIPAA-compliance, functionality for individual and multi-person sessions, dependability of technology and customer service, and optional features such as electronic session notes, scheduling, billing and payment processing, and secure file sharing.
10-1 The Case of Abby
Abby specializes in counseling individuals who have experienced the death of a twin brother 
or sister after reaching adulthood. Abby has written many popular magazine articles about her work and has appeared on national television talk shows. She lives in a small town. She has been getting many requests from all over the United States to counsel clients who have lost an adult twin brother or sister. Abby has been counseling clients locally using Zoom since the COVID-19 pandemic began, but she has never counseled anyone from out of state. She now wants to offer virtual counseling services for a fee to clients who do not live in her state.
• What are some of the ethical and legal considerations Abby should address before begin-ning to offer virtual counseling services to out-of-state clients?
Discussion: First, Abby should be licensed for independent private counseling practice in the 
state in which she lives. In addition, she should investigate state laws and regulations in the states in which potential clients whom she wishes to serve are located to determine if she is eligible to offer residents counseling services without being licensed in that state. Most states require that counselors be licensed to practice in that state to offer counseling services to residents of the state or that counselors meet exceptions that have been created in licensure 
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   263M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   263 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

264 Section 2 • Issues
The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) includes a number of standards related to providing virtual 
counseling to clients. To begin, counselors must ensure that clients understand that they have a choice 
and do not have to engage in virtual counseling if they prefer not to (ACA, 2014, §H.2.a.). This stan -
dard also requires that counselors allow clients to determine whether they are willing to use social media or technology within the counseling process. During the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown period, many clients were not able to choose between virtual counseling and in-person counseling because in-person counseling was suspended. Today, clients still may have to accept virtual counseling because that is the only option offered. This standard requiring that clients be given a choice between in-person and virtual counseling most likely will be revised when the ACA Code of Ethics is updated.
Informed Consent
When virtual counseling, social media, or other types of technology are used in counseling, the informed consent obtained from clients must go beyond the typical requirements and must include, according to the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), the following elements: “distance counseling credentials, physi-cal location of practice, and contact information; risks and benefits of engaging in the use of distance counseling, technology, and/or social media; possibility of technology failure and alternate methods of service delivery; anticipated response time; emergency procedures to follow when the counselor is not available; time zone differences; cultural and/or language differences that may affect delivery of ser -
vices; possible denial of insurance benefits; and social media policy” (ACA, 2014, §H.2.a.).
The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) also contains a provision that assumes that electronic records 
are less secure than paper records. Standard H.2.b. states, “Counselors acknowledge the limitations of maintaining the confidentiality of electronic records and transmissions. They inform clients that individuals might have authorized or unauthorized access to such records or transmissions (e.g., col-leagues, supervisors, employees, information technologists).” It is possible for electronic records to be accessed by unauthorized persons, but counselors who use standard precautions such as encrypted communications and password-protected records storage will be able to keep their clients’ privacy board rules. An example of one narrow exception is the rule in Ohio that allows counselors 
licensed out of state to provide counseling services to Ohio residents for a short period of time (https://cswmft.ohio.gov/). As more states pass statutes to participate in the interstate coun-seling compacts, the problem of providing counseling to clients across state lines will lessen. However, Abby probably will have to restrict her practice to clients living in her state unless she is willing to become licensed as a counselor in other states as well.
It will be important for Abby to determine whether her present professional liability insur -
ance policy covers virtual counseling activities. If not, she should obtain a policy that does. Abby should also review all of the virtual counseling standards in the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics to ensure that she is practicing in an ethical manner. These standards are discussed in detail later in this chapter. Finally, Abby should consider taking continuing education workshops or courses that focus specifically on virtual counseling to increase her awareness of the issues and to gain skills in that area. To increase her credibility in this new area of practice, she should consider earning the Board Certified-TeleMental Health Provider (BC-TMH) credential from the Center for Credentialing and Education (www.cce-global.org), an affiliate of the NBCC (www.nbcc.org). For best practice, and prior to accepting virtual counseling clients, it would be wise for Abby to seek clinical supervision from an experienced virtual counselor.

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 265
secure to the extent possible. Even when only in-person counseling took place and records were kept 
on paper, client privacy could never be 100% guaranteed by counselors. Counselors are expected only to take reasonable measures to protect client privacy.
Despite a number of concerns regarding virtual counseling that are discussed later in this 
chapter, ever since the COVID-19 pandemic, the practice has become an accepted approach to counseling in the United States. Training in virtual counseling is now required by some state licen-sure boards such as Louisiana’s (https://www.lpcboard.org/), and virtual counseling training pro-grams are readily available to counselors. NBCC (2018), through the Center for Credentialing and Education, offers the Board Certified-TeleMental Health Provider (BC-TMH) credential for coun-selors who wish to demonstrate their skills in this particular area of counseling (https://ww w.cce-global.org/credentialing/bctmh).
 Advantages of Virtual Counseling
Virtual counseling presents several advantages compared to face-to-face counseling in the same physical space. These advantages include: (a) increased accessibility of services for those who would otherwise find it difficult to receive services, such as low-income clients; elderly persons whose mobility is limited; individuals who are chronically ill or severely physically disabled; rural or iso-lated individuals; and clients who suffer from agoraphobia, social phobia, extreme introversion, or serious physical illnesses; (b) perceived anonymity may decrease client anxiety, thus encouraging people to ask for help and increasing the extent to which clients are willing to share intimate issues; (c) greater freedom in scheduling; (d) a reduction in lost work time and travel time for clients; and (e) facilitation of improved preparation by allowing better choice of written words over spoken ones (Bouchard et al., 2000; Haberstroh et al., 2007, 2014; Hamburger & Ben-Artzi, 2000; Heinlen et al., 2003; Kiriakaki et al., 2022; Lawson et al., 2022; Lee, 2010; Lin et al., 2022; National Alliance on Mental Illness, 2014; Reeves, 2011; Rummell & Joyce, 2010; Schultze, 2006; Shaw & Shaw, 2006).
After reviewing the results of numerous surveys completed after the COVID-19 epidemic 
began, Moreno et al. (2020) concluded that the general public is experiencing increased symptoms of depression, anxiety, and stress. These symptoms are attributed to stressors including life disrup-tion, fear of illness, and fear of negative economic effects. According to Egede et al. (2020), impor -
tant challenges in the United States since COVID-19 have been slow implementation of telemental health services and state licensing laws that forbid practitioners from servicing clients across state lines. These laws in most states were lifted during the pandemic, and there is a call to make changes permanent that would give access to virtual counseling services to all citizens.
Several researchers have studied clients’ reactions and attitudes toward virtual counseling. 
Some scholars have noted positive responses by clients to virtual counseling services (Cook & Doyle, 2002; Day & Schneider, 2002; Frye et al., 2022). Manhal-Baugus (2001) reported that 90% of clients surveyed believed that their online therapists had been helpful to them. Lee et al. (2023) found that clients in a Midwest community counseling center rated virtual counseling favorably when they were able to apply counseling sessions to real-life circumstances and had frequent coun-seling sessions, and when they perceived that their counselor had skills that helped them in their sit-uations. Scott et al. (2022) found that persons with depression reported no difference in satisfaction between virtual counseling and face-to-face immediately post-treatment, or after 3 or 12 months, and Lee et  al. (2023) concluded that virtual counseling may be a viable alternative for persons with depression. Reese et al. (2006) found that 58% of 186 survey respondents who had received both virtual counseling and physically present, face-to-face counseling preferred virtual counsel-ing. Further, 96% of their respondents said they would seek virtual counseling again, whereas only 63.1% said they would seek face-to-face counseling in the future. The results of this study suggest 
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   265M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   265 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

266 Section 2 • Issues
that some clients prefer virtual counseling over face-to-face counseling. Other researchers have 
found that although clients were satisfied with virtual counseling, they preferred face-to-face coun-seling (Leibert et al., 2006; Rochlen et al., 2004).
In addition to client satisfaction research, outcome research is beginning to demonstrate the 
clinical effectiveness of virtual counseling (e.g., Barak et al., 2008; Griffiths & Christensen, 2006; Labardee, 2009; Richards & Vigano, 2013). Results of studies suggest that virtual counseling is as effective as in-person counseling. Studies have also concluded that virtual counseling is effective in counseling combat veterans (Brief et al., 2018); treating persons with depression (Königbauer et al., 2017); counseling persons with binge-eating problems (Rom et al., 2022); treating individuals with substance use disorders (Sistad et al., 2023); and group counseling using dialectical behavior therapy (Bean et al., 2022). Gerton et al. (2023) found that clients who transitioned from in-person counseling to virtual counseling fared well.
Limitations and Boundaries
In the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), §H.4. focuses on distance counseling relationships—now com-monly called virtual counseling relationships—that are delivered through the use of technology applications. These applications are listed as including, but not being limited to, “computer hard-ware and/or software, telephones and applications, social media and internet-based applications and other audio and/or video communication, or data storage devices or media.” This section of the 2014 Code gives counselors guidelines related to boundaries, the ability of clients to use technology, 
the effectiveness of technology services rendered, access to services, and communication differ -
ences when using technology.
Standard H.4.b. of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) requires counselors to “discuss and estab-
lish professional boundaries with clients regarding the appropriate use and/or application of tech-nology and the limitations of its use within the counseling relationship (e.g., lack of confidentiality, times when not appropriate to use).” It would appear that the boundaries to which this standard is referring might include setting limits on when clients may or may not communicate by email mes-sages or texts, for example.
Counselors are required to make reasonable efforts to ensure that clients with whom they use 
technology understand how to use it and have the capacity to use it (ACA, 2014, Code of Ethics, §H.4.c.). This standard requires that counselors “follow up with clients to correct possible mis-conceptions, discover appropriate use, and assess subsequent steps.” Counselors must also provide information regarding gaining access to technology applications when they are being utilized in the counseling relationships.
When counselors or clients determine that virtual counseling services are not effective, 
§H.4.d. of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) requires that “counselors consider delivering services face-to-face. If the counselor is not able to provide face-to-face services (e.g., lives in another state), the counselor assists the client in identifying appropriate services.” This section of the ACA Code 
of Ethics needs to be updated. During the lockdown period of the COVID-19 pandemic, no face-to-face counseling services were available, and today face-to-face counseling services may be difficult to locate or may not be available to some clients.
Standard H.4.f. (ACA, 2014) requires that counselors warn clients about possible misunder -
standings when virtual counseling is being utilized. This standard states, “Counselors consider the differences between face-to-face and electronic communication (nonverbal and verbal cues) and how these may affect the counseling process. Counselors educate clients on how to prevent and address potential misunderstandings arising from the lack of visual cues and voice intonations when communicating electronically.”
M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   266M10_REML8070_07_SE_C10.indd   266 09/11/23   8:19 PM09/11/23   8:19 PM

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 267
The virtual counseling provisions of the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics provide ethical guidelines 
for practice. However, there are many issues in virtual counseling that are unresolved and are being 
discussed in the literature. A legal question exists as to where virtual counseling takes place: in the location of the counselor, the location of the client, or both locations. Which laws govern the counsel-ing process related to issues such as reporting abuse is another question. Because virtual counseling is still relatively new, these questions will not be resolved until lawsuits are filed for cases in which virtual counseling was used. Counselors who provide virtual counseling services need to ensure that their professional liability insurance provides coverage for virtual counseling (Reeves, 2011).
Counselors who consider offering counseling services over the internet should ensure that 
they are not violating state laws (in the state of the counselor or of the client) that require counsel-ors to be licensed. Standard H.1.b. (ACA, 2014) provides this guidance regarding following legal requirements when using technology in counseling: “Counselors who engage in the use of distance counseling, technology, and social media within their counseling practice understand that they may be subject to laws and regulations of both the counselor’s practicing location and the client’s place of residence. Counselors ensure that their clients are aware of pertinent legal rights and limitations governing the practice of counseling across state lines or international boundaries.”
A Rapidly Changing Environment
A potential problem with virtual counseling is related to the fact that the field is changing so rapidly. The pace of change will make it difficult for counselors to maintain a full measure of competence in its use. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Trepal et al. (2007) suggested that counselor educa-tors prepare counselors to provide virtual counseling because it had become so accepted in society. Since the pandemic, all counselor education programs are preparing counselors to provide virtual counseling services because that type of training is essential. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014, §H.1.a.) says, “Counselors who engage in the use of distance counseling, technology, and/or social media develop knowledge and skills regarding related technical, ethical, and legal considerations (e.g., special certifications, additional course work).”
Although guidelines for the use of technology in health and mental health are proliferating, 
not all guidelines are helpful to counseling practitioners. Many guidelines related to the use of technology in counseling overlap and are repetitive. For example, Anvari et al. (2023) reviewed 60 existing guidelines for the provision of virtual medical care. About half of the guidelines were published after the COVID-19 pandemic began. Most of the guidelines (92%) specified the prac -
tice area in which their guidelines applied. Many of the guidelines addressed confidentiality issues, technology requirements, and patient consent. The researchers concluded that the quality of the published guidelines was poor. Anvari et al. concluded that best practices for telemedicine include paying attention to confidentiality, patient consent, preparation before a visit, and clear patient communication. The review of virtual medical care completed by Anvari et al. (2023) provides some insight into similar guidelines that are being published regarding virtual counseling in that guidelines for the use of virtual counseling that will be developed in the future also need to be as client-focused as possible.
The ACA Code of Ethics (2014) includes a section on the use of technology in counseling 
entitled, “Section H: Distance Counseling, Technology, and Social Media.” However, since this code was adopted in 2014, the COVID-19 pandemic occurred and the use of technology in coun-seling has exploded, which has necessitated the development of additional recommendations. A number of professional associations, certification bodies, and other entities have developed guide -
lines for the use of technology in mental health counseling. Figure  10-1 lists some of the docu-
ments that are providing guidance to counselors in their use of technology.

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 269
With virtual counseling, there is no reliable method of regulating the services offered, of ensuring 
the competence of the providers, or of protecting consumers from ill-advised internet-based services 
(International Society for Mental Health Online, 2008; Peterson et al., 2007; Shaw & Shaw, 2006). On the other hand, advances are being made in regulating virtual counseling services delivered across state lines. The Telehealth Certification Institute (https://www.telementalhealthtraining.com/), a private company that delivers telehealth training and consultation services, offers a website where counselors can determine whether a particular state has specific regulations on telemental health services, laws regulating insurance reimbursement, or provisions for out-of-state counselors to practice in that state. See https://telementalhealthtraining.com/states-rules-and-regulations.
Despite the possible advantages of virtual counseling and the prevalence of its use (Lee et al., 
2023), some mental health professionals believe that virtual counseling services have multiplied more quickly than the evidence that such services are safe or effective (Welfel, 2016). A number of concerns have been raised (Froehlich, 2023; Kiriakaki, 2022; Lawson et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2022), including: (a) the possibility exists that counselors may operate from erroneous assumptions due to the absence of visual and auditory cues; (b) lack of security of communications could lead to breaches of confidentiality; (c) a relationship-building human presence between counselor and cli-ent is lacking; (d) issues that are interpersonal in nature cannot be addressed effectively using this medium; (e) counselors may not be able to discern when a client’s condition poses a threat to self or others; (f) counselors may find it difficult to determine an appropriate fee structure for virtual counseling; (g) the health of counselors may suffer; and (h) protecting clients from incompetent or unqualified virtual counselors may be difficult. Disadvantages to virtual counseling from the client’s perspective include the absence of personal warmth (Leibert et al., 2006), frustration with the pace of online counseling, and problems with using the technology (Haberstroh et al., 2007).
Whether clients’ health insurance providers reimburse for virtual counseling services is an
