If plan members or their providers want services that exceed those allowed under a managed care system, they must request exceptions from the company.
A controversy regarding managed care revolves around whether health care companies are 
indeed containing health care costs appropriately by eliminating unnecessary procedures and lim-iting allowable services or are simply increasing their profits at the expense of health care recip-ients. Many health care providers are dissatisfied with managed care systems because managed care administrators make decisions about health care services that professionals believe they should make themselves. Most health care companies today limit the number of outpatient visits for mental health concerns that members are allowed each year. The idea of limiting counseling services to a set number of visits is objectionable to many mental health care providers. They would rather have health care companies allow clients as many visits as professionals believe are necessary to resolve a mental health concern. Of course, clients who are plan members could have more visits than their health care plan allows, but they would have to pay the fees themselves.
Types of Health Care Plans
Health care companies offer many different types of plans to the public. The most common kinds available today include health maintenance organizations (HMOs), preferred provider organiza-tions (PPOs), and traditional indemnity health insurance policies. Of course, many other types of health care plans exist, such as exclusive provider organizations (EPOs), managed care organiza-tions (MCOs), and individual practice associations (IPAs). Combinations of two or more types are occasionally formed, and sometimes what appears to be an HMO is actually a PPO.
When individuals purchase health care plans from an HMO, they agree to go to the HMO for 
all their health care needs, and they agree to accept the provider of the health care services who is assigned to them by the HMO. Although most HMOs offer some flexibility in choosing providers within the organization, members cannot go outside the organization for their health care. Most HMOs hire providers of health care. Members do not pay for services and then get reimbursed. Instead, they receive health care services from the HMO for no fee or for the co-payments that they have agreed to in their contracts.
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   357M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   357 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

358 Section 2 • Issues
Individuals who purchase health care plans from a PPO agree to go to providers who have 
been preapproved by the organization for all of their health care needs. PPO policy members can 
choose their providers but only from the list of approved providers given to them by the PPO plan. PPOs do not hire providers of health care. The approved providers on their list usually are in private practice or work for a separate health care agency. PPO providers collect co-pays and deductibles and bill insurers, but members are responsible for what the insurer does not cover.
Indemnity insurance works something like a PPO, but individuals who purchase indemnity 
health insurance do not have to go to providers who have been preapproved by the organization. Policyholders can choose their health care provider as long as the provider is in a category that the insurance company recognizes. Health care providers collect co-pays and deductibles and bill insurers, and persons insured by indemnity health insurance companies are responsible for what the insurer does not cover.
Counselors as Service Providers
Some counselors or counseling agencies refuse to accept clients who intend to use their health care plan to assist with fees (Chamlou, 2023). The position of these counselors and agencies is that dealing with health care plans or insurance companies is too time consuming or is objectionable philosophically. The clients of such counselors and agencies are, therefore, directly responsible for any fees charged for the services rendered to them. Of course, such clients must have the financial resources to be able to afford the services, which means that the counselors in these practices coun-sel only individuals who have the financial means to afford their services.
In contrast, many agencies that employ counselors require that they become providers for 
health care plans (Kassirer et al., 2013). In addition, most counselors in private practice find that they must become providers in order to have enough paying clients to maintain their business.
Becoming a provider for a PPO is not an easy process. Counselors must first determine 
which PPOs exist in their community and then apply to each one separately to become a provider. Sometimes the application process is difficult and time consuming. Because PPOs purposefully limit their lists of preferred providers, not all eligible mental health professionals in the commu-nity will be accepted by a PPO, which can accept or reject any application it receives as long as it does not illegally discriminate against a professional. To become a provider for a particular PPO, a counselor may have to apply many times over a long period. Despite these difficulties, counselors do seem to be succeeding at becoming accepted providers. Once a counselor has been accepted by a PPO as a provider, or when a counselor has accepted a client who has indemnity health insurance that will reimburse the client for the counselor’s services, it is important that the counselor complete health plan forms in an appropriate and professional manner.
Most health care plans demand accountability from their providers. Plans may require coun-
selors to submit detailed justifications for their diagnoses. In addition, most health care plans expect providers of mental health care services to develop objective and measurable goals as a part of each client’s treatment plan. Some plans send auditors to providers’ offices to determine whether the provider is offering a professional setting for rendering services, is keeping confidential information secure, is accessible to clients for services, and is keeping records appropriately. If providers do not comply with a health care plan’s requirements, the plan has the option of removing them as provid-ers. In fact, to avoid liability, health care plans must remove from their lists any providers who are ineffective professionally.
It is vital for counselors to request detailed information from each health care plan for which 
they provide services regarding the plan’s expectations from providers. Each plan is unique and 
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   358M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   358 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

Chapter 13 • Professional Relationships, Private Practice, and Health Care Plans 359
requests various types of information in a multitude of formats. In order to maintain provider status, 
counselors must comply with the requirements of each health care plan: Counselors must be “accu-rate, honest, and objective in reporting their professional activities and judgments to appropriate third parties, including courts, health insurance companies, those who are the recipients of evalua-tion reports, and others” (ACA, 2014, §C.6.b.).
Counselors who serve as providers must be prepared to deal with a number of ethical and 
legal issues that are unique to managed care and other types of health plans. Issues that counselors must contend with in managed care environments include ensuring client privacy, providing proper diagnoses, securing informed consent, receiving payment for services, continuing treatment despite denial of services, and not engaging in fraud. Welfel (2016) has pointed out that counselors who work with managed care companies must also deal with a conflict of interest in that they must avoid alienating the company from which they need referrals and must be advocates for quality care for their clients at the same time.
Federal Health Care Plans
Counselors have been politically active in each state to become recognized as mental health care provid-ers by health care companies under state laws. Counselors have also been recognized as providers under federal health care plans. Fullen et al. (2020a, 2020b) investigated the advocacy beliefs and behaviors of counselors related to Medicare reimbursement for counselors and found, among other things, that coun-selors had been negatively affected by not being eligible to provide services to Medicare clients and that counselors expect CACREP and ACA to provide leadership in advocacy areas such as Medicare reimbursement for counselors. CACREP specifically accredits counseling graduate programs and does not have the mission of advocating for the counseling profession. While ACA provides lobbying at the national level for issues such as health care reimbursement for counselors, ACA administrators count on practicing counselors to be involved in advocacy efforts. Perhaps the expectation of practicing coun-selors that CACREP and ACA should be the primary advocates for important professional issues leads to practicing counselors not being individually as active in advocacy efforts as they should be (Westcott et al., 2022). After many years of lobbying the federal government and its agencies by individual coun-selors and their professional associations, licensed counselors have been recognized as mental health providers for military TRICARE recipients and Medicare beneficiaries. Mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists were recognized as outpatient mental health providers under Medicare Part B on January 1, 2024, after Congress passed the Mental Health Access Improvement Act (S. 828/H.R. 432). See https://www.congress.gov/bill/117th-congress/house-bill/432/text. Prior to the pas-sage of this legislation, counselors were excluded from the list of approved mental health providers, which already included psychiatrists, psychologists, clinical social workers, and psychiatric nurses (NBCC, 2023c). See Chapter 2 for discussions of these developments.
On February 1, 2023, the U.S. Department of Veteran Affairs (V A) formally launched an occu-
pational series for mental health counselors. In the past, counselors employed by the V A held job titles other than counselor (National Board for Certified Counselors [NBCC], 2023d). There contin-ues to be no occupational series for professional counselors within the general federal government, where job positions are categorized in one of the Occupational Series classifications established by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM; NBCC, 2023a). NBCC is pursuing government affairs initiatives focused on helping counselors become providers of mental health services and is advocating for additional counselor employment by the Department of Veterans Affairs, the Indian Health Service, and the Military Health Systems and for the creation of a federal occupations series for counselors beyond the one recently developed by the V A.

360 Section 2 • Issues
 Client Privacy
When clients contract with health care plans, they sign forms that give health care providers per -
mission to transmit information regarding their health care to their health care plan administrators. 
Clients, in the past, believed that they had to agree that service providers could release any informa-tion regarding their treatment that the plan administrators requested. However, HIPAA has changed that situation significantly: Under HIPAA, insurance companies are limited in the types of infor -
mation they may request, and they are not allowed to disallow claims if clients refuse to provide them with certain types of information, such as psychotherapy notes. See Chapter 6 for a thorough discussion of the provisions of HIPAA.
Many mental health professionals feel uneasy about providing detailed information regarding 
their clients’ diagnoses and treatment to administrators who usually are not mental health profes-sionals. Their discomfort is compounded by the fact that they have no control over what happens to the information after it has been released to the provider. Yet if counselors wish to be providers, they must transmit this information. When counselors become providers for PPOs, they should request a copy of the privacy waivers that PPO members routinely sign. If a counselor is ever unsure about whether a client wants the counselor to provide information requested from a health care plan administrator, it would be best to ask the client to sign a waiver giving permission for the informa-tion to be sent.
In our opinion, it is fruitless for an individual counselor to challenge the idea that health care 
providers inappropriately invade the privacy of their clients. If a counselor believes that a health care plan policy should be changed, the counselor should communicate directly with a company repre-sentative regarding the policy. Counselors who believe that laws or regulations should be changed should work through the state or national counseling association to seek legislative changes. Counselors might take additional steps, such as increasing their role in the  decision-making pro-cesses of managed care companies and working with companies to establish clearly structured appeals processes. Knapp and VandeCreek (2008) have reminded counselors that they have an ethi-cal obligation to advocate for their clients by informing policymakers about problems with the pres-ent health care system. Most professional associations for counselors have government relations offices that advocate for counselors and they often request that counselors help influence legislation favorable to counselors by contacting legislators.
Diagnosis
In Chapter 7, we noted that using the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM, 2022) creates con-
flicts for counselors who espouse a wellness or developmental orientation. Most managed care organizations require that mental health professionals assign a DSM diagnosis to their clients in order to qualify for reimbursement of services. Most managed care organizations also limit in some way the diagnoses for which they will pay benefits. Some organizations will not reimburse for V-code conditions, typical developmental transitions, personality disorder diagnoses, adjustment disorders, or family or couples counseling.
Counselors are sometimes caught in a conflict because they want to promote client welfare by 
helping clients receive reimbursement and, at the same time, to provide accurate diagnoses. Given the choice between an honest diagnosis without insurance reimbursement and a deceptive diagnosis with third-party payment, some counselors may consider rendering inappropriate diagnoses that are tied to the chance of being reimbursed by health care companies. Rather than yield to these tempta-tions, counselors can improve the chances that their treatment plans will be approved by assessing clients thoroughly, setting realistic treatment goals, and complying with health care companies’ 
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   360M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   360 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

Chapter 13 • Professional Relationships, Private Practice, and Health Care Plans 361
recommended treatment protocols. Regardless, counselors are ethically obligated to “take special 
care to provide proper diagnosis of mental disorders” (ACA, 2014, Code of Ethics, §E.5.a.).
Informed Consent
Managed care can complicate informed consent procedures in several ways. Because managed care plans typically limit treatment options and the number of sessions they will reimburse, counselors must address these limitations with clients at the outset of the counseling relationship. Informed consent also includes ensuring that clients know what information their managed care organization requires the counselor to disclose as well as the implications of any diagnosis that might be assigned (ACA, 2014, Code of Ethics, §A.2.b.). More detailed information about informed consent is provided in Chapter 4.
Receiving Payment for Services
Most PPOs pay providers directly for services rendered to the PPO members. These fees are sent to providers after they have completed necessary forms that document the services rendered. This arrangement means that counselors often receive payment long after services have been rendered.
When counselors provide services to indemnity health insurance policyholders, counselors can 
handle payment in one of two ways. Counselors may require that the client pay for services as they are rendered and then file for reimbursement. Counselors may also allow clients to assign their reimburse -
ment to the counselors so that the payment goes to the counselor from the insurance company. With either method, counselors must verify to the insurance company that they provided services for the client and must provide all the information regarding the services requested by the insurance company.
Usually, both PPOs and indemnity health insurance companies require clients to pay a portion 
of the counselor’s fees directly. This amount is called a co-payment. It is vital that counselors collect co-payments from clients when such payments are a requirement of the PPO or indemnity health insurance company.
After counselors have succeeded in becoming approved providers under managed care plans, 
they confront some ethical issues. Most managed care companies approve only brief mental health treatment as a cost containment measure. Thus, counselors must be prepared to provide brief coun-seling for many of their clients who want their health care plans to pay for services. In the past, counselors typically were not trained in brief counseling. Since then, counselor training programs have recognized the need to teach not only the traditional approaches but also the brief counseling models. Programs are now preparing students to meet the clinical and ethical challenges created by managed care. Therefore, practitioners whose training did not adequately prepare them to deal with managed care must seek continuing education in the brief counseling approaches and seek supervi-sion and consultation while developing their competencies (ACA, 2014, §C.2.b.).
Counselors who participate in managed care plans must be able to assess which clients will be 
well served in a time-limited context. They must also be knowledgeable about referral resources for clients whose needs cannot be met under the limitations of their health care plans. Because counsel-ors have an ethical obligation to be advocates for their clients (ACA, 2014, §A.6.a.), they must also be skilled at appealing health care companies’ decisions to deny reimbursement when clients need more sessions than their plan allows.
 Continuing Treatment and Denial of Services
Although counselors may do their best to serve clients whose health care plans reimburse only for time-limited counseling, inevitably there will be clients who will need more sessions than their health care plan allows or approves. In these situations, counselors may be caught between the 
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   361M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   361 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

362 Section 2 • Issues
need to uphold their ethical standards and the need to remain financially viable in private prac -
tice. They have ethical obligations to avoid abandoning clients (ACA, 2014, §A.12.), to ensure 
appropriate termination (§A.11.c.), and to help clients find alternative resources for continued treatment (§A.11.d.).
Counselors face issues of abandonment when they have to terminate client relationships 
before they believe they should. It is not ethical to discontinue counseling services for lack of pay-ment when the client is in a state of crisis or emergency. In such situations, counselors must con-tinue providing treatment until the emergency is resolved or appropriate alternative care can be arranged (Welfel, 2016). At the same time, counselors cannot be expected, except in emergency situations, to continue indefinitely to counsel clients without receiving payment for their services (ACA, 2014, §A.11.c.).
Lawsuits have been filed when patients suffered injuries after their health plans refused to 
cover services that were requested (Wickline v. State of California, 1986; Wilson v. Blue Cross, 1990). In these cases, the health plans argued that their denial of payment or reimbursement for services was not a denial of care. They argued that the treating health care provider had the final responsibility for appropriate care. The courts in these cases held that health plans could be held liable if they were negligent in denying health care services that were necessary to prevent a patient being injured. In Wickline, the court also found that the physician involved in the case should have protested the health plan’s denial of care that he had requested. It was the court’s position that a health care provider is not liable if a health plan refuses to provide necessary care but that the provider does have a responsibility to protest if the recommendation for care is not accepted.
Mental health care services under managed care probably present more challenges to provid-
ers than do physical health care services. First, mental health care often is limited to levels that seem inappropriate to mental health care providers. For example, a health care plan may allow only five reimbursed counseling sessions a year, or the plan may require that a member have a diagnosis of a serious mental or emotional disorder, while severely limiting the services that may be provided under the plan.
The court cases discussed previously provide a foundation for making some recommenda-
tions to counselors providing services to clients covered by managed health care companies. These recommendations will help you protect yourself from legal liability:
• If you believe that a client requires counseling services beyond what has been approved by a managed health care company, request the additional services on behalf of the client. If the request is denied, file a written protest or complaint with the company.
• Instruct the client regarding their right to appeal a decision that denies additional services.• If the client’s condition indicates a danger to self or others or if the client is in a crisis and cannot afford to pay for your services directly, continue providing services for the client until care can be transferred to another professional or to a facility that provides mental health care.
Anderson (2000), who is a counselor and has been a managed care case manager, has offered 
suggestions to counselors about how to deal constructively with managed care companies. He has 
suggested that counselors avoid treating managed care case managers as the enemy but, instead, establish a positive relationship with them for the benefit of clients. Anderson (2000) has also rec-ommended to counselors that they emphasize good treatment plans in their practices, use proper code numbers in submitting claim forms, and use empirical research results to support exceptions to decisions that have been made.
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   362M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   362 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

Chapter 13 • Professional Relationships, Private Practice, and Health Care Plans 363
Avoiding Fraud
Counselors who complete the forms required by PPOs or health insurance companies must be careful 
not to engage in fraudulent practices. Fraud occurs when counselors misrepresent to health care com -
panies any facts regarding their services so that their clients will receive payment or reimbursement for the counselors’ services (Robinson, 2020). If fraudulent practices are discovered, health care com -
panies can file civil lawsuits to recover payments or reimbursements made inappropriately. Because fraud is a crime, the companies can even cause prosecutors to file criminal charges against counselors.
Because many counselors do not know what constitutes health care fraud, it is possible for them 
to commit fraud without realizing they are doing so. New counselors should be careful not to pattern their behavior after that of seasoned practitioners because some actions that mental health professionals have been taking for years are actually fraudulent. We have observed that some counselors occasionally engage in health care fraud activities because they are motivated to help their clients receive mental health care services that are paid for or reimbursed by health plans. For example, counselors report inac-curate information to health care plans so that their clients will be able to begin or continue counseling. Unfortunately, counselors also benefit from fraudulent practices in that health plans pay for their ser -
vices based on the inaccurate information they have provided. Therefore, it appears to outside observers that counselors are being self-serving when they give false information to health care plans.
13-5 The Case of Dan
Dan has just been licensed as a professional counselor and has decided to open a private prac-
tice. He knows from talking to other licensed counselors in his state that a few of the major PPOs and health insurance companies in the state do not pay or reimburse clients for the services of licensed counselors. Dan’s good friend Monique is a licensed clinical psychologist. Monique’s clients do receive payment or reimbursement for her services. Monique agrees to sublease office space to Dan for his private practice. In addition, Monique agrees to sign off on PPO and health insurance forms indicating that she is the client’s psychologist even though Dan is the one see-ing the clients. Monique and Dan meet weekly and review each of Dan’s cases to ensure that Monique is comfortable with his treatment of clients. All clients are told that Monique is supervis-ing Dan. In exchange for Monique’s assistance, Dan agrees to give Monique 25% of all fees he collects from PPO and health insurance clients.
• What is the major problem with this arrangement?
• What do you think would happen if Monique indicated on the insurance forms that Dan provided the mental health care services and that Monique supervised him in providing those services?
 
Discussion: This arrangement between Dan and Monique appears on the surface to be one that 
serves everyone’s purposes well. Monique is helping out her friend and realizing extra income through subleasing her office space, supervising Dan, and receiving 25% of the PPO and health insurance client fees that Dan collects. Because his clients’ health care plans will pay or reimburse as long as Monique continues to supervise Dan and sign off on his treatment plans, Dan is able to see clients who normally would not be able to see him. Clients are pleased that their health care plans are paying for their counseling services. This may seem like a perfectly reasonable arrangement, and some mental health practitioners have long been involved in these kinds of arrangements.
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   363M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   363 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

364 Section 2 • Issues
The problem is that Monique and Dan are fraudulently misrepresenting their services to 
the PPOs and health insurance companies. Although it may seem unfair that health care plans do 
not reimburse licensed counselors for their services, health care plans in some states do have the right to refuse to reimburse for the services of licensed counselors. If a health care company dis-covered this arrangement, the company could sue Monique, Dan, and Dan’s clients for fraud and perhaps collect all of the fees they had paid out to them, plus other damages. In addition, the company could request that the local prosecutor file criminal fraud charges against all the parties and might also file ethics complaints against Monique and Dan with their licensure boards.
There is actually nothing wrong with licensed counselors affiliating with other mental health 
professionals, such as licensed psychologists or licensed physicians. If the counselor is going to 
provide the direct service to clients, then PPO and insurance forms must be completed to reflect that situation. In the case example you just read, no fraud would have been involved if Monique had indicated on forms that Dan, a licensed counselor, was providing the direct service and that she, a licensed psychologist, was supervising Dan’s work. A PPO or health insurance company might have refused to pay or reimburse for services provided under such an arrangement (which it would have a legal right to do), but possibly payment or reimbursement would have been approved. If they had fully disclosed their arrangement to the payor, then the entire arrangement between Dan and Monique would have been acceptable. There is nothing illegal or unethical about fee splitting under arrangements such as theirs as long as there are no misrepresentations to PPOs or health insurance companies.
In the following list, you will find a number of fraudulent practices that could lead to seri-
ous trouble for mental health professionals. Counselors commit fraud when they report to PPOs or health insurance companies that:
• An approved professional is providing direct services to a client, but a counselor who is not approved for payment or reimbursement actually is providing the services.
• A client is assigned a DSM diagnosis that is approved for payment or reimbursement, but the counselor actually does not believe the diagnosis is accurate.
• The counselor is claiming to provide individual counseling that is reimbursable but is actually providing couples, family, or group counseling that is not reimbursable.
• The counselor is collecting a required co-payment from a client even though the counselor is actually waiving the co-payment.
• Counseling services were said to be rendered on a specific date, even though the client actually missed the session, but the counselor billed for the missed session anyway.
• The counselor appears to be a physician by signing forms in the space marked physician’s signature and doesn’t note that he is a licensed counselor rather than a physician.
• The counselor claims that they are beginning a counseling relationship with a client for the first time, when actually they have been involved in an ongoing counseling relationship, because health care providers often will not pay or reimburse for preexisting conditions.
•  The client says they are requesting payment or reimbursement from only one health care plan, but the counselor knows that the client is actually requesting payment for the same services from two or more plans.
As discussed in Chapter 7, counselors who are willing and properly trained to diagnose emo-
tional and mental disorders have an ethical and legal obligation to diagnose according to accepted 
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   364M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   364 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

Chapter 13 • Professional Relationships, Private Practice, and Health Care Plans 365
standards in the mental health field (ACA, 2014, §E.5.a.). If a counselor determines that a client 
does not have a DSM disorder for which a health care plan will pay or reimburse, then the counselor must not assign such a disorder just to allow a client to obtain health care plan financial assistance.
The Changing Nature of Health Care Plans
We have summarized the current types of health care plans available and the issues they raise for mental health care providers. Health care plans have changed substantially over the past two decades and are expected to continue to change as legislators and the public discuss and amend health care delivery systems in the United States. It is important for counselors who provide services for clients with health care plans to constantly monitor changes in the health care industry. What may be an acceptable practice today may be prohibited tomorrow.
Diversity Considerations
As noted, some people in the United States do not have health care insurance. Unfortunately, many of these individuals are the very people who can ill afford to pay out of pocket for mental health care services. Wilcoxon et al. (2008) have urged counselors to confront the business paradigm of the health care industry and have suggested that counselors advocate for changing corporate approaches to cli -
ent care that further disadvantage those who already are disadvantaged. Counselors in private practice grapple with conflicts between their desire to assist these individuals and their need to generate suf -
ficient income to maintain their practices. Private practitioners are thus encouraged to provide some pro bono service—that is, service for which there is little or no financial return (ACA, 2014, Section A Introduction). Knapp and VandeCreek (2008) have pointed out, though, that decisions about pro bono and reduced-cost services should be made only after careful consideration of possible ramifications. For example, will the client devalue the worth of the services if the services are free?
13-6 The Case of Isabelle
Isabelle has been in private practice for 10 years. She has an office in an affluent part of town, 
and all of her clients either have insurance that pays for her services or are wealthy enough to pay her from their personal funds. Isabelle feels some guilt, however, because she entered the counseling profession to be of help to individuals and to society. She also is aware that the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) encourages counselors to provide some services pro bono. Isabelle is won-dering how she should provide free services to some clients.
• If Isabelle decides to see some clients for free or at a reduced rate in her private practice, what problems might that cause?
• How else might Isabelle meet her pro bono obligations?
 
Discussion: If Isabelle were to accept some clients into her private practice who pay nothing, 
she would need to have clear criteria regarding how clients could qualify for the services. If her other clients were to somehow learn that she was serving some clients pro bono and they did not understand how she came to that decision, they might feel resentful even if they can afford her full fee. Perhaps a better option for Isabelle to fulfill her pro bono obligation would be to volun-teer her counseling services in a community agency for a period of time each week or to provide free workshops in the community.
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   365M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   365 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

366 Section 2 • Issues
Summary and Key Points
This chapter focuses on three related topics— 
professional relationships, private practice, and health care plans. Counselors who practice in any setting interact regularly with other professionals and must conduct themselves in an ethically and legally appropriate manner. Although private prac-tice is idealized by many counselors in training, private practice is a business that carries associated risks and requires knowledge of business practices. It is important for counselors to understand the var -
ious types of insurance plans.
Key points regarding professional relation-
ships include:
• Counselors have different roles depending on 
the circumstances of their interactions with other professionals.
• Because most counselors function at vari-ous times as employees and as employers, it is important that they understand the basic princi-ples of employment law.
• Counselors as employees have the right to be free of discrimination in the workplace and to refuse to follow a supervisor’s directive if it con-stitutes a crime.
• Counselors as employees do not have a right to employment, and in all but limited circumstances they can be dismissed from their jobs.
• Counselors and their employers sometimes disagree about what is ethical, and several steps can be taken to attempt to resolve these disagreements.
• Counselors as employers need to be clear about their roles and their expectations of those whose work they supervise.
• Counselors as employers should avoid having close personal relationships or friendships with their employees.
• Before transferring private information about cli-ents, counselors should obtain client permission; counselors must also work to preserve client confidentiality when transferring clients’ private information.
• Although counselors cannot make referrals in ways that might benefit the counselors them-selves, they may make referrals to other profes-sionals as dictated by client welfare or the poli-cies of their employers.
• Relationships between counselors and other mental health professionals are characterized by respect, honesty, and fairness.
• Counselors must avoid making statements about other professionals that could be libelous or slanderous.
Private practice is a business. Counselors 
who wish to start their own practice need to under -
stand certain aspects of business practice. Key points regarding private practice include:
•  Counselors who wish to start their own practice 
must be knowledgeable about taxes; business licenses; and the various forms of businesses, including sole proprietorships, partnerships, and corporations.
• Private practitioners must set fees that are rea-sonable by community standards.
• Counselors must understand the appropriate and inappropriate uses of a sliding scale fee.
• It is a good practice for counselors to collect pay-ment at the time they render their services.
• Counselors in private practice need the services of attorneys and accountants to assist them in managing their businesses.
• It is vitally important that counselors carry  
adequate professional liability insurance.
• Private practice is not for every counselor. The risks and benefits must be carefully weighed before a decision to enter private practice is made.
Most individuals today have some type of 
health insurance that is paid for or subsidized by their employer or by the government. Several types 
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   366M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   366 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

Chapter 13 • Professional Relationships, Private Practice, and Health Care Plans 367
of insurance plans raise complex issues for coun-
selors who wish to become approved providers of mental health care services. Key points regarding health insurance include:
• Each state in the United States has laws that gov-
ern which health care plan contracts may be sold in that state.
• There are several types of health care plans, including HMOs, PPOs, and traditional indem-nity plans.
• Managed care has had a significant impact on mental health care services.• With some perseverance, counselors usually can 
become approved providers for managed care companies.
• Ethical and legal issues raised by managed care and other types of health plans include maintain-ing client privacy, rendering appropriate diagnoses, ensuring informed consent of clients, receiving payment for services, continuing treatment after benefits end, dealing with problems caused by denial of services, avoiding committing insurance fraud, and meeting the needs of individuals who do not have mental health insurance.
M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   367M13_REML8070_07_SE_C13.indd   367 30/10/23   5:41 PM30/10/23   5:41 PM

title: Chapter 14: Issues in Counselor Education
content:
368
14Issues in Counselor Education
 LEARNING OBJECTIVES
14.1 Identify the major ethical and legal issues that 
counselor education programs must address to serve students and protect their future clients.
14.2 Describe the varied issues involved in relationships between counseling students and faculty members. FOCUS QUESTIONS
1. What kinds of information do 
prospective students need to receive about a counselor training program so that they can make an informed decision regarding whether to apply?
2. What do you think you should do if you know that a classmate is struggling and does not seem to be learning the skills, behaviors, and dispositions needed to become a competent counselor?
3. What kinds of relationships outside the classroom between counselor education professors and students are appropriate? In this chapter, you will learn about the ethical and legal issues that must be addressed during the process of educat-ing graduate students to become counselors. The faculty in counselor education programs have responsibilities to ensure that prospective students have adequate information to make an informed decision whether to enter the programs, and admissions standards must be clear and fair. Gatekeeping is perhaps the responsibility that causes the most consternation among educators and faculty alike, yet it is a necessary pro-cess to protect future clients of students’ counseling services. In this chapter, a variety of issues that affect relationships between students and faculty are also explored.
 The Impact of the COVID-19 
Pandemic on Counselor Education
Although computer-enhanced counselor education has existed 
since 1984, the vast majority of CACREP-accredited programs were traditional in structure at the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic (Becnel et al., 2023; Haddock et al., 2020; Snow &  
Coker, 2020; Snow et  al., 2018). The pandemic caused an unprecedented disruption in higher education (Ghazi-Saidi et al., 2020), particularly in applied education programs, such as counseling, that had depended on external structures to pro-vide field experiences and needed to ensure all courses could be taught and standards met online in a secure and confiden-tial manner (Christian et al., 2021).
Initially, this unexpected and sudden transition caused 
confusion, chaos, and frustration among students and faculty 
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   368M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   368 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 369
alike (Li, 2022). Counseling students, suddenly unable to attend classes in person, had to adapt to 
online delivery platforms and management systems such as Blackboard and Canvas while learning to cope with their own life changes necessitated by the lockdown. Students who were enrolled in their practicum and internship courses were concerned about how they would complete their clinical field experiences and obtain their required practicum and internship hours (Becnel et al., 2022). As it became apparent that face-to-face instruction would not resume quickly, counselor educators had to set aside their concerns as to whether applied courses such as counseling skills and group counseling could be taught effectively using virtual means because no other options were available.
Despite these challenges, counselor education programs adapted quickly to the new realities. 
Counselor educators, realizing that simply transferring their course materials into virtual manage-ment systems would not promote student engagement, developed new teaching strategies and forged online student learning communities (Haddock et al., 2020). Students, as well, became acclimated to a virtual learning environment. In one study, most counseling students reported that although they initially experienced a wide variety of disruptions, they found their educational and informal support systems to be adequate for them to be able to continue their studies (Crockett & Ivers, 2022).
Today, virtual learning is well-established in counselor education (Coker et  al., 2021; 
McKenna & Manis, 2021), and the trend is continuing in the post COVID-19 era. The conveniences of online learning have been retained in a mixture of in-person, virtual, and hybrid courses in many counselor education programs. Counselor educators have established procedures for encouraging student autonomy in virtual learning environments (e.g., flipped classrooms), providing adequate instructor support (e.g., virtual office hours), and facilitating student interaction and collaboration (e.g., discussion boards; Lee, 2022). Practices such as these may be particularly well suited to our high-paced technological society (Gay & Swank, 2021). In the post-pandemic era, the focus of research has shifted to developing best practices in virtual counselor education, such as strategies for infusing a social justice lens (Rollins et  al., 2022), for addressing inherent racism (Hannon et al., 2023), for incorporating telehealth training into the curriculum (Gallo et al., 2022), and for integrating virtual technology that presents content-specific immersive and experiential learning that emulates real-world interactions (Sacco-Bene et al., 2022). In the future, it will be necessary to assess the effectiveness of such transformations and to continue to develop teaching practices specifically tailored to virtual counselor education (Elliott & Henninger, 2020; Li & Su, 2021). The pace of change is likely to continue to accelerate, presenting new opportunities and challenges for the transformed process of counselor education.
COUNSELOR EDUCATION PROGRAMS
Informed Consent
Just as clients have the right to know what they are undertaking when they come for counseling, students have the right to know what will be expected of them before they choose to enter a graduate program in counseling. In keeping with the spirit of informed consent, prospective students need enough information to enable them to make wise choices about their graduate studies.
In the American Counseling Association’s Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), §F.8.a. lists quite 
explicitly the information that must be provided to prospective students before they enter a program. This information includes the following:
• The values and ethical principles of the profession• The type and level of skill and knowledge acquisition required for successful completion of the training program
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   369M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   369 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

370 Section 2 • Issues
• Technology requirements
• Program training goals, objectives, and mission and the subject matter that will be covered• Bases for evaluation• Training components that encourage self-growth or self-disclosure as part of the training process
• The type of supervision settings and the requirements of the sites that are needed to obtain the required clinical field experiences
• Student and supervisee evaluation and dismissal policies and procedures• Up-to-date employment prospects for graduates
Application materials, including those found on websites, must include all of these elements and 
must be thorough and current. Counselor education programs also have an obligation to ensure that prospective students can contact a knowledgeable individual at the university if they have questions.
You learned in Chapter 4 that informed consent is a legal as well as an ethical matter. The law 
of informed consent requires that individuals be properly informed before their consent to engage in counseling sessions is valid. Before graduate students can appropriately agree to enter a program into which they have been accepted and subsequently meet the program’s requirements for graduation, they must be informed of all the requirements. In addition, the program’s requirements for graduation must be presented in sufficient detail to allow entering students to fully understand their obligations.
Jorgensen and Brown-Rice (2016) found that 27.7% of CACREP-accredited programs require 
applicants to undergo a criminal background check (CBC). Although this may seem intrusive, such a check can prevent future problems for some applicants. All 50 states require a CBC for school coun-selors. In 2016 a total of 17 state licensure boards required applicants to pass a CBC (Jorgensen &  
Brown-Rice, 2016), and it is likely that all 50 states now require a criminal background check as part of the counselor licensure application process. Applications for licensure typically ask whether the applicant has been convicted of a felony. As an informed consent issue, we believe it would be wise for all programs, whether or not they require a CBC, to inform prospective students of the pos-sible consequences of any legal violations in their past.
Perhaps the most important goal of informed consent in admissions is to ensure that prospec-
tive students understand that becoming a counselor involves more than just acquiring knowledge. Counseling students are instead expected to engage in a variety of learning experiences that differ from those required in most other disciplines (Pease-Carter & Barrio Minton, 2012). Applicants should be made aware that their training will challenge them on the personal level as well as on the academic level (Herlihy & Corey, 2015a).
Admissions
Counselor educators strive to admit into training programs individuals who are likely to suc-ceed at developing the knowledge, skills, and characteristics needed to become effective coun -
selors. Academic ability is one important criterion, and relevant work experience might also be considered. Some graduate programs list minimum criteria for admission, which might include a minimum Graduate Record Examination (GRE) score or a Miller Analogy Test (MAT) score, a minimum undergraduate or graduate grade point average (GPA), or a minimum number of years of work experience. Swank and Smith-Adcock (2014) found that 70% of counselor education programs use standardized test scores and that 95% use GPA as a criterion for admission into mas -
ter’s-degree programs. There is some evidence to suggest that undergraduate GPA and GRE scores are useful for predicting both graduate GPAs and student scores on the Counselor Preparation Comprehensive Exam (CPCE; Hatchett et al., 2017). Many counselor education programs require 
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   370M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   370 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 371
that students pass the CPCE to graduate. Many programs also attempt to gather information by 
requiring applicants to submit letters of reference or a personal statement or essay. With the advent of artificial intelligence, however, counselor educators have become concerned that personal state -
ments may have been written by an avatar. Concerns surrounding artificial intelligence will need to be addressed on an ongoing basis in admissions processes as new technologies emerge.
Demonstrated academic ability is a relatively straightforward criterion to apply. Admissions deci-
sions become more difficult when we consider that learning to be a counselor involves more than mas-tery of academic content. Admissions screening also involves an evaluation of the personal qualities and interpersonal skills of applicants (Garner, 2020). Personal characteristics associated with the ability to succeed as a counselor include maturity, flexibility, emotional stability (Nagpal & Richie, 2002), accep-tance, open-mindedness, empathy, warmth, genuineness, and sensitivity (McCaughan & Hill, 2014). As we noted in Chapter 7 on competence, it is possible for a student to have strong intellectual abilities and still not possess the personal and interpersonal characteristics to be an effective counselor. Some individuals who are attracted to careers in counseling seem to have personality or adjustment problems that would negatively affect their ability to develop the competencies needed to become effective coun-selors. Therefore, counselor educators attempt to screen applicants carefully and to select only those students who possess the characteristics associated with counseling effectiveness. As you can imagine, it is difficult for counselor educators to assess whether program applicants have these personal and interpersonal attributes. The importance of assessing counselor dispositions has become institutional-ized in the CACREP accreditation standards (CACREP, 2024); accredited programs are required to assess the dispositions of their students both at the time of admission and throughout their matriculation in their respective programs. However, the absence of dispositional measures that reliably predict coun-selor performance makes this standard difficult to uphold (Hatchett, 2023).
Admissions decisions for applicants are usually based on a review of several factors. These fac-
tors are considered in relation to the number of individuals that the program can accommodate and the credentials of other applicants. Counseling graduate programs should make it clear that they make admissions decisions in this manner. University administrators and judges generally would not substi-tute their judgment for that of faculty members unless it was clear that an applicant had been unfairly discriminated against or that the faculty had failed to follow the procedures that they had established.
Counselor education programs cannot legally discriminate against prospective students who 
are in any category that is protected by the U.S. Constitution or federal statutes and their imple-menting regulations. These categories include race, sex, disability, age, residence, and alien status (Kaplin & Lee, 1995; Welfare et al., 2017). Counselor education programs must also be careful to avoid discriminating against students with disabilities.
In the past, programs have given special consideration to applicants whose characteristics are 
underrepresented in the program or in the profession of counseling. Admissions practices are being scrutinized in the wake of the U.S. Supreme Court decision in Standards for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President and Fellows of Harvard College (2023), which prohibited affirmative action practices in which race was considered in admission decisions. Regardless of developments in the legal arena, however, nondiscrimination remains an ethical mandate. Counselor educators have an ethical respon-sibility to ensure that counselor education programs reflect the cultural diversity of our society. According to the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014):
Counselor educators [should] actively attempt to recruit and retain a diverse student body. Counselor 
educators [should] demonstrate commitment to multicultural/diversity competence by recognizing and valuing diverse cultures and types of abilities students bring to the training experience. Counselor educators [should] provide appropriate accommodations that enhance and support diverse student well-being and academic performance. (§F.11.b.)
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   371M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   371 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

372 Section 2 • Issues
For some counselor education programs that are located in geographical areas where the 
population is not very diverse, recruiting a diverse student body can be a challenge. Even when a 
diverse range of students can be attracted to a program, a recursive situation may occur in which a program continues to admit and then ultimately lose students who are members of marginalized societal groups because the students are always entering programs in which most of the students are members of the dominant culture (Behl et al., 2017; Avent Harris et al., 2017). Hipolito-Delgado et al. (2021), after interviewing 19 counseling graduate students of color, discovered that the par -
ticipants’ motivations for attending graduate school included their interest in serving marginalized communities, their ability to find programs committed to diversity, and the support they received during the application process to graduate school. Some programs now use flexible admissions criteria to increase student diversity rather than continue to rely on GPA and GRE scores, which are less reliable predictors of success for racially and ethnically diverse applicants. Hannon et al. (2023) have recommended that counselor education programs increase the racial or ethnic diversity of their applicant pools by removing standardized test requirements (e.g., the Graduate Record Exam) for admission. Hammonds and Perguson (2022) have advocated for a holistic admissions process that includes evaluation of applicants’ life experiences, academic metrics, and attributes or dispositions.
Curriculum Issues
In Chapter 2, we discussed in some detail the curricular components that a counselor education program must contain to be accredited by the Council for Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs (CACREP). CACREP standards (CACREP, 2024) are generally accepted by the profession as a model curriculum for master’s-level preparation of counselors. Here, we high-light some of the ethical responsibilities of counselor educators in delivering the curriculum and explore one of the most ethically sensitive components of training—self-growth experiences.
TEACHING ETHICS, MULTICULTURAL COUNSELING, AND SOCIAL JUSTICE  Counselor edu-
cators must make students aware of the ethical standards of the counseling profession and their responsibilities to the profession (ACA, 2014, §F.7.e.). Historically, in some counselor education programs, ethics was taught as a separate course, whereas it was infused into existing courses in other programs. Today, best practice is for ethical and legal issues to be both taught as a discrete course and infused throughout the training process when students are at various developmental levels. Students who are just beginning their studies in this area may look at ethical issues one way. Later, as they acquire direct experience with clients during their practicum and internship, their views are likely to evolve.
Ethical decision-making skills are essential; experts polled by Herlihy and Dufrene (2011) 
identified helping students develop these skills as the most important ethical issue in counselor preparation. Ametrano (2014) has described how an ethics course can teach ethical decision mak-ing, increase tolerance for ambiguity, and enhance student awareness of how values influence decision making in the wake of the Ward v. Wilbanks (2010) legal case (described in detail in Chapter 3).
Counselor educators have strengthened their focus on multicultural pedagogy in recent decades 
(Hall et al., 2014) in response to a growing recognition that multicultural competence is essential to ethical practice. As was noted in Chapter 3, it is almost certain that counselors will encounter clients whose cultural backgrounds are different from their own, which presents a challenge for counselor educators who must determine how best to prepare multiculturally competent counselors. Counselor educators are thus obligated to “infuse material related to multicultural/diversity into all courses and workshops for the development of professional counselors” (ACA, 2014, §F.7.c.).
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   372M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   372 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 373
An often-overlooked resource is the large numbers of international students in counselor educa-
tion programs, who can offer diverse perspectives on curricula that may be Eurocentric (Kuo et al., 
2018). In addition, counselor educators must infuse multicultural/diversity competency in their training and supervision practices and “train students to gain awareness, knowledge, and skills in the compe-tencies of multicultural practice” (ACA, 2014, §F.11.c.). Despite these mandates, some students find it difficult to remain open to multicultural course content and react with reluctance, attitudinal rigidity, and entrenchment of prejudiced beliefs (Malott et al., 2023); and counselor educators have invested considerable effort in determining the most effective approaches to increase these students’ receptivity. As a result, multicultural counseling courses often are taught using a combination of didactic, experien-tial, and community service learning approaches. Killian and Florin (2020) found that approaches that enforced both direct exposure and self-reflection (rather than passive knowledge attainment) were best for facilitating the development of students’ multicultural competence.
Social justice, a growing force within the counseling profession, has created a shift in how 
prospective counselors are prepared for the profession, and counselor education programs are infusing issues related to multiculturalism and social justice into all counselor education courses. Students often believe that they are inadequately prepared in social justice and advocacy, and counselor educators are responding to this belief by implementing pedagogical strategies such as exploring the dynamics of privilege and oppression, engaging students in the development of critical consciousness, using experiential activities, infusing social justice advocacy into supervi -
sion (Bevly et al., 2017), and engaging students in service learning projects to shift learning to beyond the classroom (Manis, 2012; Steele et al., 2014). Counselor educators are teaching students the skills involved in broaching (Day-Vines et al., 2021) the topics of race, ethnicity, and culture so that social justice issues are addressed in the counseling relationship. Singh et al. (2020) have noted that although numerous improvements were made in the attention given to multicultural, social justice, and advocacy issues in the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics, ethical practice entails more than adherence to the Code, and the spirit of social justice must be infused throughout all compo-nents of counselor training.
TEACHING THEORIES AND TECHNIQUES  Counselor educators have an ethical responsibility to 
teach current theories and techniques that are appropriate to the populations that students will be coun -
seling. This obligation is articulated in the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), which states that counselor educators should “provide instruction based on current information and knowledge available in the profession” (§F.7.b.). This means that counselor education programs must expose students to a wide range of counseling theories and encourage students to develop their own theoretical stances. Before the 1960s, models of counseling that were taught were almost exclusively those developed by Euro-American males. It was generally assumed by counselor educators that these models had universal application. However, today’s counselor educators are aware that traditionally taught theories may be inadequate to address the needs of diverse clientele. They also emphasize multicultural, feminist, relational–cultural, ecological, and other systemic approaches that may be more applicable to diverse client populations.
Counselor educators should “promote the use of techniques/procedures that are grounded in 
theory and/or have an empirical or scientific foundation” (ACA, 2014, §F.7.h.). When counselor educators introduce students to techniques or modalities that are developing or innovative, they must explain to students the potential risks and ethical considerations of using those techniques or modalities. The intent of this standard is not to discourage innovation; rather, it serves as a reminder of the importance of intentionality in selecting techniques and as a caution against implementing techniques that may be risky to use with clients if those techniques are not well proven.
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   373M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   373 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

374 Section 2 • Issues
EXPERIENTIAL LEARNING  Counselor education programs, whether they are offered in a tradi-
tional or an online format, must integrate academic study and supervised practice (ACA, 2014, 
§F.7.d.). Typically, training programs present students with a graduated series of opportunities to practice the skills they are learning, beginning with an introductory skills or techniques course and progressing through a supervised practicum and internship.
A particular type of experiential learning that occurs in counselor education programs involves 
self-growth experiences in which students increase their understanding of interpersonal dynamics and their awareness of self and others. An ethical concern regarding self-growth experiences is how counselor educators can balance students’ rights to privacy and the need to provide effective train-ing. Until the mid-1980s, professors who had any administrative, supervisory, or evaluative author -
ity over students were prohibited from offering students self-growth experiences, and counselor education programs were required to offer students alternatives to self-growth experiences without penalty or prejudice. These guidelines, which certainly protected students’ privacy rights, allowed for the possibility that a student could go through an entire training program without participating in a self-growth experience. In recent decades, there has been a shift in thinking regarding self-growth experiences. The current Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) clarifies that self-growth is an expected com-
ponent of counselor training. Standard F.8.c. states that counselor educators should be “mindful of ethical principles when they require students to engage in self-growth experiences.” Counselor educators should therefore inform students that they have a right to decide what information about themselves they will share or withhold in class. Evaluation has to be independent of the student’s self-disclosure—that is, students cannot be graded on their level of self-disclosure. In addition, counselor educators may require students to seek professional help to address any personal con-cerns that have the potential to affect their professional competence (§F.8.d.). It can be uncomfort-able for students to confront their own biases and prejudices that may emerge in group discussions, so it is important for students to understand that a rationale exists for requiring such experiences. As was discussed in Chapter 3, students who are unaware of their own countertransference issues or biases are at risk of providing inadequate or even harmful services to clients.
DEVELOPING AND EVALUATING COUNSELING SKILLS  Effective counseling requires coun-
selors to apply techniques and behaviors that are accepted as effective in the professional litera-ture (Schmidt, 2016). In addition, they must know when to engage in which behaviors and how to acknowledge the unique personalities of the counselor and the client in the counseling process. Counseling includes scientific principles that are artfully applied. Students may be uneasy about being evaluated on their skill development because these criteria are less objective than those in tests of academic performance that use scores or numbers.
Counselor educators who teach counseling skills must define effective counseling in specific 
and concrete terms so that students can become proficient in their skill development. Students who are not performing well in counseling skills classes have the rights to be made aware of their defi-ciencies and to receive recommended steps to improve their performance.
If an evaluation or grade is challenged, counselor educators have to explain to peers, and later 
perhaps to a judge or jury, the criteria they used to evaluate a student’s counseling skills. Counselor educators are obligated to:
• Indicate the skills to be learned in the course in terms that are as specific and behavioral as possible.
• Give periodic, specific feedback regarding the development of those skills.• Have a method of transferring evaluations into numbers or grades that can be justified.
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   374M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   374 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 375
14-1 The Case of Naomi
Naomi is a first-semester master’s-degree student in a counselor education program. Over the course 
of the semester, she does excellent work in her classes in counseling theories and human growth and development, earning an A in both classes. Naomi is also enrolled in the beginning counsel-ing techniques class. During the first half of the semester, she is evaluated twice by the instructor on her skill development as demonstrated in role-play counseling sessions with classmates. After she receives a grade of unacceptable on her first evaluation, she makes an appointment with the professor, who gives her specific feedback regarding the areas of performance in which she needs to improve. At midterm, she shows very little improvement, and the instructor asks her to make another appointment to receive individualized feedback. Naomi does not follow up on this request. After observing Naomi’s counseling performance for a third time, the instructor takes her aside after class and explains that Naomi is at risk of failing the course if she continues to be unable to demon-strate the basic active-listening skills required of counselors. Together, they develop a growth plan with specific behavioral objectives. For the remainder of the semester, Naomi is so anxious about performance evaluations that she freezes up and makes no progress. She also ignores the instruc-tor’s suggestion that she seek counseling to deal with her performance anxiety. At the end of the semester, she does not receive a passing grade, which means, by program policy, that she cannot continue in the program. Naomi files an appeal, asserting that she is an excellent student, as evi-denced by her A grades in her other two courses.
• Why do you think a graduate student in counseling, such as Naomi, might get A’s in two courses but a failing grade in her counseling techniques course?
• What might Naomi have done differently to improve her chances of receiving a passing grade?
Discussion: Naomi has very little chance of succeeding with her appeal. She received feedback 
throughout the semester and was given specific instructions regarding what she needed to do to improve her performance. In addition, she failed to respond to the instructor’s requests to make a second appointment or to take steps to deal with her performance anxiety. There is no evidence that the instructor violated procedures or was unfair in evaluating Naomi. This case illustrates the effort required to evaluate students on their skill development. This course instructor devoted much more time and effort in working with Naomi than would have been required had the course been a traditional, didactic course in which grades were based on performance on objective tests. We would hope that Naomi seeks personal counseling to learn to cope with her anxiety if she wishes to continue in her graduate program. She will be expected to demonstrate her skills if she repeats the class and to continue to do so in other courses throughout the program.
Evaluation issues can be very complex for educators who teach skills courses, particularly for 
those who teach group counseling courses. Among counselor educators, the consensus is that stu-
dents learn best when didactic instruction is accompanied by experiential learning about group pro-cess and dynamics. Generally, the course instructor avoids serving as the facilitator of the  experiential component of the class because this dual role creates ethical dilemmas when evaluating students for continuation in the program. Although students are informed about the limits of confidentiality at the outset of the group experience, a level of trust can develop in the group that fosters personal risk taking. Student self-disclosures may allow course instructors to have knowledge about a student that 
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   375M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   375 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

376 Section 2 • Issues
might cause educators to have concerns about the student’s fitness for the profession. The counselor 
educators would then face the dilemma of not wanting to violate the student’s confidentiality but also feeling the need to fulfill their gatekeeper role. Because of these dual-role conflicts, counselor educa-tion programs have developed several alternative methods for delivering the experiential component of a group counseling class. Some of these alternative methods include having advanced students or counselors from the community lead the groups, having the component led by adjunct instructors who have no ongoing evaluative role with students as they progress through the program, having actors play the roles of group members (Fall & Levitov, 2002), sending students off campus to par -
ticipate in group experiences, using videos to engage students in observational learning, or using a fishbowl technique to combine observational and experiential learning (Spargo et al., 2010). During the COVID-19 pandemic, most of these alternatives were unavailable. Urkmez et al. (2021) studied a group of students who began the group experience in a face-to-face format and abruptly had to tran-sition to an online format because of COVID-19. Although the students reported a reasonably good experience with online groups, including comfort and cohesiveness with the other group members, most voiced a preference for face-to-face sessions. Nonetheless, we anticipate that virtual group 
counseling courses will remain an option for students. Wathen et  al. (2021) have offered a model that considers the ethical challenges as well as the adaptations that are needed to ensure success with these courses.
EVALUATIONS OF FIELD EXPERIENCE PERFORMANCE  Just as evaluat-
ing the development of counseling skills can be difficult, evaluating students’ performance in their practicum and internship experiences is  challenging (Cook et al., 2018; Storlie et al., 2017). It is important for counselor educa-tion programs to have specific and understandable procedures for students to enter into and complete field experiences. Developing practicum and intern-ship sites, communicating with on-site supervisors, and orienting everyone involved are complex tasks for counselor educators, yet these responsibili-ties are clearly delineated in the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014). According to §F.7.i., counselor educators must develop clear policies regarding field placement and other clinical experiences, and they must provide clearly stated roles and responsibilities for students, site supervisors, and program supervisors. Counselor educators must also confirm that site supervisors are qualified to provide supervision and are informed of their ethical and profes-sional responsibilities as supervisors.
During field experiences, graduate students are evaluated by a 
number of professors and  on-site supervisors. If a student’s performance is evaluated negatively, counselor educators must ensure that the evalu-ations are fair. To prevent having negative evaluations successfully chal-lenged, counselor educators must follow all the procedures that have been established by the program for field placements.
PERSONAL AND PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT  Because there is a well-established relation-
ship between clinical competence and personal and professional development, counselor educators have an ethical obligation to evaluate student development in areas other than academic perfor -
mance and skills acquisition. CACREP accreditation standards (2024) require counselor educators to assess students’ professional and personal growth and development. Characteristics that have been deemed to be crucial to personal and professional development are discussed in Chapter 7.
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   376M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   376 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 377
When student development proceeds according to faculty expectations, problems are 
unlikely to arise. However, sometimes students struggle in their personal and professional 
development (Lundquist et al., 2020; Um & Bardhoshi, 2022). Historically, counselor educa-tors have shown a preference for using informal means to address these struggles (Foster & McAdams, 2009). Counselor educators are aware that a great deal of subjectivity is involved in such evaluations and may be reluctant to negatively evaluate students along these dimen -
sions because of concern about being unfair, a hope that the struggles will self-correct with further training, or fear that the students will bring lawsuits against them (Gaubatz & Vera, 2002). Despite this possible reluctance, however, counselor educators recognize the needs to be clear and specific when evaluating personal and professional development and to communicate their expectations to students. Most programs have developed written assessment forms that include criteria for personal and professional growth. Foster and McAdams (2009) have noted that these types of evaluation can be anxiety provoking for stu-dents and that students who are not familiar with the programs’ assessment policies and procedures may consider the policies to be punitive.
Classmates often are aware of peers who are having prob-
lems with professional competency. Results of one study sug-gested that 95% of peers had been personally affected by this situation (Rosenberg et al., 2005), which indicates that the learn-ing of all students is disrupted by these problems. Brown-Rice and Furr (2019) found that approximately two-thirds of doctoral students were aware of peers with problems with professional competency. These students reported that they were affected  negatively by being in a program with these peers and were frus-trated with educators for allowing problematic peers to continue their doctoral training. Generally, students understand the impor -
tance of intervention for the sake of the classmate’s future clients as well as for the profession (Foster et al., 2014), but they may be uncertain as to whether or how they should take action. Brown-Rice and Furr (2013) and Parker et al. (2014) have recommended that counselor educators develop, implement, and inform stu-dents about formal procedures that provide an opportunity for students to approach a faculty member when a peer is struggling.
14-2 The Case of Nasima
Nasima is a second-year master’s-degree student who has just begun her practicum experience, 
counseling clients in a community agency. As Nasima has progressed through her degree pro -
gram, she has become friends with a group of four other students who entered the program at the same time Nasima did. They have all remained friends and socialize together. One of these friends, Tonya, is enrolled in practicum at the same agency. Tonya has confided in Nasima that she is finding her work with clients to be very stressful, and Nasima has noticed that Tonya is becoming intoxicated when the two of them go out socializing with their friends. Tonya has also confided that she is struggling as a counselor and is not selecting the videotapes of her bad counseling sessions 
M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   377M14_REML8070_07_SE_C14.indd   377 09/11/23   8:11 PM09/11/23   8:11 PM

378 Section 2 • Issues
to share with her supervisor. Nasima is concerned about Tonya and is worried that Tonya’s clients 
are not being well served. At the same time, Nasima doesn’t want to take any action that could “get Tonya in trouble with her supervisor.” Nasima doesn’t know what to do.
• Does Nasima have an ethical obligation to take some action to protect Tonya’s clients?
• What do you think Nasima should do in this situation?
Discussion: According to the ACA Code of Ethics (2014), Nasima as a student has the same 
ethical obligations as does a counselor who is already in practice. She cannot ignore the pos-sibilities that Tonya may be in danger of becoming impaired and may not be providing adequate counseling services to clients. Nasima’s desire to protect her friend is understandable, and her first step should be to talk with Tonya about her concerns. She should then encourage Tonya to seek personal counseling to deal with her stress and excessive drinking and to inform her super -
